conditions.remedy: Let soil dry halfway; repot if waterlogged.
conditions.prevention: Water when top 3 cm of soil is dry; not on a schedule.

conditions.name: Drooping from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Leaves droop and lose their glossy rigidity.
conditions.remedy: Water thoroughly; recovers in a few hours.
conditions.prevention: Check soil weekly; don't skip waterings.

conditions.name: Fading variegation
conditions.symptoms: Variegated types lose their pink/cream markings.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; prune back solid-green stems.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light preserves variegation.

conditions.name: Sticky latex sap
conditions.symptoms: White sap oozes from cuts.
conditions.remedy: Wipe with a damp cloth; wear gloves as sap irritates skin.
conditions.prevention: Keep out of reach of pets; sap is mildly toxic.

conditions.name: Bottom leaves dropping
conditions.symptoms: Oldest leaves yellow and drop over time.
conditions.remedy: Normal aging; check for overall plant health.

facts: Water only when the top 3–4 cm of soil is dry — the thick rubbery leaves store water for weeks.
Wipe the glossy leaves with a damp cloth every few weeks — dust reduces both photosynthesis and the plant's signature shine.
Keep away from cold draughts — rubber plants drop leaves below 12 °C.
Prune the top to encourage branching; otherwise it grows as a single tall stem.
Ficus elastica is in the Moraceae family and is the same genus as the common edible fig.
The milky latex sap of rubber plants was harvested commercially to make rubber before Hevea brasiliensis took over as the main rubber source.
Rubber plants produce aerial roots that can eventually fuse into trunk-like columns in their native habitat.
In Meghalaya, India, living bridges of woven Ficus elastica roots span rivers — some are over 500 years old.
Ficus elastica was introduced to Europe in 1815 as part of the Victorian obsession with exotic foliage.
Rubber plants were once a Victorian parlour staple; they fell out of fashion in the 20th century and then returned via 2010s plant trends.
A single cutting rooted in water can develop into a small tree within 3–4 years.
The sap is mildly irritating to skin and eyes — wear gloves when pruning.
Rubber plants are mildly toxic to cats and dogs if ingested.
'Burgundy' cultivars develop near-black leaves when grown in bright light.
In its native Assam and Sumatra, Ficus elastica can grow more than 30 m tall with a crown spanning a small building.

care.placement_hints: Commonly sold with braided trunks; tolerates a bit more water than most houseplants.

lore: The braided trunk fad began with a Taiwanese truck driver in the 1980s, who wove young trunks together as a Feng Shui talisman for prosperity. Now a global shop-opening gift.
etymology: 'Pachira' is a Guyanese indigenous name; 'aquatica' means 'of the water', for the swampy riverbanks where it grows wild.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Max 2 hours morning sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leggy stems with leaves only at the top
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow scorched leaves

placement_tips: 1–2m from a bright east or south window
Tolerates a wider range of moisture than most houseplants
Humidity ≥40%
Rotate monthly for even growth

conditions.name: Yellow dropping leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaflets yellow and fall off.
conditions.remedy: Usually overwatering — let soil dry fully; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Water thoroughly, then let top 5 cm dry before next watering.

conditions.name: Crispy leaf tips from dry air
conditions.symptoms: Tips of leaflets turn brown and papery.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity; move away from vents.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥40%; group with other plants.

conditions.name: Leggy growth in low light
conditions.symptoms: Long bare stems with leaves only at the top.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; prune back to encourage branching.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light; rotate monthly.

conditions.name: Braided trunk splitting
conditions.symptoms: The decorative braided trunk starts separating.
conditions.remedy: Gently re-tie the trunks together with soft twine.
conditions.prevention: Don't let braids grow too much before re-tying.

conditions.name: Fuzzy mold on soil
conditions.symptoms: White fuzzy mold on the soil surface.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off the mold; let soil dry deeper between waterings.
conditions.prevention: Don't keep soil constantly moist; improve airflow.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Water the money tree deeply, then let the top 5 cm of soil dry before watering again.
Mist the leaves occasionally or keep it near a humidifier — brown leaf tips indicate dry air.
Unbraid older trunks carefully if they start growing together; forceful separation can damage the stems.
Rotate the pot weekly for even growth — money trees lean strongly toward light.
Pachira aquatica is in the Malvaceae family — the same family as cotton, cacao, and hibiscus.
The five leaflets on each leaf are often said to represent the five elements in Chinese tradition.
In the wild Pachira grows in swamps and riverbanks — the roots tolerate being flooded for weeks at a time.
Feng shui placed money trees near entryways in Taiwanese homes throughout the 1980s, fuelling global demand.
The braided trunk tradition started in Taiwan in the 1980s when a truck driver began weaving young seedlings.
The actual seed pods of Pachira aquatica are edible — roasted they taste similar to peanuts or cashews.
Money trees can technically flower indoors but almost never do; in the wild the spidery cream flowers open at night and attract bats.
A money tree can live 50+ years indoors when treated well.
Pachira aquatica is non-toxic to cats and dogs, a rarity among tropical houseplants.
The common name 'money tree' refers to a Taiwanese legend of a poor farmer whose Pachira brought him wealth.
A single seed can germinate in 1–2 weeks and begin forming a swollen base by the end of the first year.

care.placement_hints: Tolerates lower light; sensitive to fluoride and chlorine in tap water.

common_conditions: brown leaf tips from tap water fluoride
bare stems from very low light
lore: Related dragon trees bleed a red resin called 'dragon's blood' — traditionally used as varnish, medicine and dye across the ancient Mediterranean. Modern Dracaena marginata is entirely popular as a forgiving office plant.
etymology: 'Dracaena' from Greek 'drakaina' (female dragon), for the red resin; 'marginata' refers to the red margins on each leaf.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None — avoid direct sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Bare lower stem, slow growth, drooping tuft
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow bleached leaves

placement_tips: 1–3m from a window; tolerates interior rooms
Use rain or filtered water to avoid brown tips
Rotate weekly to keep it upright
Keep above 15°C

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from tap water
conditions.symptoms: Tips of long narrow leaves turn brown and crispy.
conditions.remedy: Switch to filtered, distilled, or rain water.
conditions.prevention: Avoid fluoridated tap water; flush pot quarterly.

conditions.name: Yellow leaves from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Whole leaves yellow, often starting at the tip.
conditions.remedy: Let soil dry fully; trim yellow leaves.
conditions.prevention: Water sparingly; the plant is drought-tolerant.

conditions.name: Thrips
conditions.symptoms: Silvery streaks on leaves; tiny slender insects hiding in leaf creases.
conditions.remedy: Wipe leaves with insecticidal soap; treat weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect monthly.

conditions.name: Bare lower stem
conditions.symptoms: Long bare stem with only a tuft of leaves on top.
conditions.remedy: Natural growth habit; prune back the stem to a desired height — new shoots will emerge below the cut.
conditions.prevention: Prune in spring if you want a bushier shape.

conditions.name: Leaning toward the light
conditions.symptoms: Tall stem leans significantly toward the window.
conditions.remedy: Rotate the pot a quarter turn weekly.
conditions.prevention: Rotate weekly to keep growth even.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Water only when the top 3–4 cm of soil is dry; dragon trees prefer to dry out between drinks.
Use filtered water if possible — fluoride and chlorine in tap water cause brown leaf tips.
Prune off the top of a leggy stem; it will re-sprout into 2–3 new heads from the cut point.
Keep above 15 °C year-round; dragon trees will drop leaves in cold rooms.
Dracaena marginata is native to Madagascar, and was first cultivated in European greenhouses in the late 1800s.
The name 'Dracaena' comes from the Greek drakaina, meaning 'female dragon' — a reference to the red sap.
The red resin produced by older Dracaenas is called 'dragon's blood' and has been used in medicine, varnish, and even Stradivarius violin finishes.
Dragon trees are on NASA's Clean Air Study list for removing formaldehyde, xylene, and trichloroethylene.
In Spanish folklore the closely related Dracaena draco was said to bleed the blood of actual dragons when its trunk was cut.
Dragon trees can live for centuries — the most famous specimen, 'El Drago Milenario' in Tenerife, is estimated at 800+ years old.
Dracaena marginata leaves are mildly toxic to cats and dogs, causing drooling and vomiting if chewed.
Each leaf lives for 2–3 years before yellowing and dropping — this is normal, not a sign of distress.
The narrow leaves have red edges that grow brighter in strong light, giving the cultivar its name 'marginata'.
A single stem cut into 20 cm sections can be rooted to create multiple new plants.
Older dragon trees develop multiple branching heads, each with its own fountain of red-edged leaves.

care.placement_hints: Needs high humidity; bathroom or grouped with other plants; never let the rootball dry.

common_conditions: crispy brown fronds from dry air
lore: The 'Boston' cultivar is a spontaneous 1894 sport discovered among a shipment of ferns routed through Boston — it had fuller fronds and quickly became the Victorian parlour plant of the era.
etymology: 'Nephrolepis' from Greek 'nephros' (kidney) and 'lepis' (scale), after the kidney-shaped spore covers; 'exaltata' means 'raised up', for its tall arching fronds.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Max 1 hour gentle morning sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Sparse pale fronds
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Crispy brown fronds

placement_tips: Bathroom or kitchen with natural light is ideal
Humidity ≥55% non-negotiable
Never let the rootball dry out
Keep away from heating vents

conditions.name: Crispy brown fronds
conditions.symptoms: Whole fronds turn brown and papery.
conditions.remedy: Trim the crispy fronds; raise humidity; check soil moisture.
conditions.prevention: Never let the rootball dry; keep humidity ≥50%.

conditions.name: Yellowing from cold drafts
conditions.symptoms: Fronds yellow after the plant sits near a cold window.
conditions.remedy: Move away from the draft; yellow fronds won't recover but new ones will come.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 16°C; away from draughty windows and vents.

conditions.name: Shedding in low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Leaflets drop off, creating a mess around the plant.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity with a tray or humidifier; trim bare stems.
conditions.prevention: Bathroom or kitchen placement; avoid dry heated rooms.

conditions.name: Scale on fronds
conditions.symptoms: Brown or tan bumps on the underside of fronds.
conditions.remedy: Wipe individual fronds with insecticidal soap; cut off heavily infested fronds.
conditions.prevention: Inspect monthly; isolate new ferns for 2 weeks.

conditions.name: Runners escaping the pot
conditions.symptoms: Thin runners reach out of the pot and over the rim.
conditions.remedy: Natural; trim if you want a tidy look, or let them dangle.
conditions.prevention: Repot annually; runners signal active growth.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

facts: Keep soil consistently moist — Boston ferns wilt and brown quickly if they dry out.
Humidity above 50% is essential; set the pot on a pebble tray or in a bathroom.
Avoid direct sun — bright indirect light brings out the deepest green fronds.
Prune dead or yellowed fronds at the base to keep new growth looking lush.
Nephrolepis exaltata is a true fern in the Lomariopsidaceae family and reproduces via spores, not seeds.
The underside of each frond carries rows of reddish-brown sori — clusters of spore-producing sporangia.
Boston ferns are among the oldest plant lineages on Earth — ferns have been around for 350+ million years.
Boston ferns were the signature Victorian houseplant — every parlour of the 1880s displayed one on a pedestal.
The 'Boston' name comes from the fern's 1894 introduction to the USA via a Boston-based nursery shipment from Philadelphia.
Nephrolepis exaltata was featured in NASA's Clean Air Study for removing formaldehyde and other indoor pollutants.
Boston ferns are completely non-toxic to cats and dogs, making them one of the safest pet-friendly ferns.
A healthy Boston fern sends out wiry, hair-like runners called stolons; these root and produce new plantlets.
In the wild, Nephrolepis exaltata is found in humid forests from Florida to the Caribbean to West Africa.
The genus name 'Nephrolepis' comes from the Greek words for 'kidney' and 'scale' — a reference to the shape of the sori covers.
Boston ferns can live for decades indoors; in warmer climates they naturalise outdoors and can become invasive.

care.placement_hints: The diva fern — wants constant moisture, high humidity, no direct sun.

common_conditions: fronds crisping within hours of drying out
spider mites in warm dry rooms
lore: Water beads off the fronds without wetting them — an effect so reliable the ancient Greeks associated the plant with Aphrodite, whose hair stayed dry even when she emerged from the sea.
etymology: 'Adiantum' from Greek 'adiantos' meaning 'unwetted'; 'raddianum' honours the 19th-century Italian botanist Giuseppe Raddi.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None — direct sun crisps fronds within hours
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Sparse pale fronds
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached fronds that don't recover

placement_tips: Bathroom with natural light is almost mandatory
Humidity 60–80% — consider a cloche or terrarium
Never let soil dry out, even for a day
Avoid any direct sun

conditions.name: Fronds crisping within hours
conditions.symptoms: Whole plant turns brown after a single missed watering.
conditions.remedy: Trim brown fronds to the base; water thoroughly; new fronds emerge within 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Never let soil dry out; water every 2–3 days.

conditions.name: Brown edges from dry air
conditions.symptoms: Frond edges brown even when soil is moist.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity to 60%+; use a cloche or terrarium.
conditions.prevention: Bathroom placement or humidifier on full time.

conditions.name: Bleached fronds from direct sun
conditions.symptoms: Fronds fade to pale green or yellow-white.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect light; damaged fronds don't recover.
conditions.prevention: No direct sun ever — bright indirect is the ceiling.

conditions.name: Spider mites in dry rooms
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing and stippling on fronds.
conditions.remedy: Rinse in the shower; boost humidity dramatically.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥60%; mites hate it.

conditions.name: Slow recovery from trim
conditions.symptoms: After a hard cut-back, new growth takes a month or more.
conditions.remedy: Keep conditions ideal; fresh fronds will come.
conditions.prevention: Trim in spring for fastest recovery.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.

facts: Never let maidenhair soil dry out — even one missed watering can kill the fine fronds.
Mist daily or use a humidifier; 60%+ humidity is non-negotiable for this fern.
Use rainwater, distilled, or filtered water — chlorine and hard water burn the delicate foliage.
Trim off any dried fronds at the base; new ones will emerge from the rhizome quickly.
Adiantum raddianum is part of the Pteridaceae family and one of the most delicate ferns commonly sold as a houseplant.
The name 'Adiantum' comes from the Greek adiantos, meaning 'unwetted' — water rolls straight off the fronds.
The fronds are hydrophobic thanks to a microscopic wax coating, which is how they earned their name.
In Ancient Greece, Adiantum was called 'capillus-veneris' — literally 'Venus's hair' — and woven into bridal wreaths.
Maidenhair fern extracts have been used in traditional medicine across Europe and South America for centuries, often as a cough remedy.
Adiantum raddianum was introduced from Brazil in the 19th century and quickly became a Victorian conservatory favourite.
Maidenhair ferns are completely non-toxic to cats, dogs, and humans.
A maidenhair fern can produce 10+ new fronds in a single good week when humidity and light are ideal.
The stems (called stipes) are a distinctive glossy black — a classic identification feature.
Mature plants reproduce by spores formed along the undersides of the frond margins.
In terrariums, maidenhair ferns thrive far better than in open pots thanks to the trapped humidity.

care.placement_hints: Water into the soil, never the central crown rosette.

common_conditions: crown rot from water in center
wavy brown frond edges from low humidity
pale fronds in very low light
lore: An epiphyte that grows high in rainforest tree canopies; its central rosette collects leaf litter which decomposes into a perched garden of soil. Some Taiwanese and Polynesian cultures eat the young unfurling fronds as a vegetable.
etymology: 'Asplenium' from Greek 'a-' (not) and 'splen' (spleen) — ancient herbalists believed it cured spleen disorders; 'nidus' is Latin for 'nest', describing the rosette's shape.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None — direct sun scorches fronds
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale floppy fronds
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Brown wavy frond edges

placement_tips: 1–2m from a bright window
Humidity ≥50%
Water at the soil edge, not the central crown
Keep above 16°C; off cold windowsills

conditions.name: Crown rot from central watering
conditions.symptoms: Center of the rosette blackens and rots.
conditions.remedy: If crown is fully rotten, discard. If partial, trim and let dry out.
conditions.prevention: Water the soil at the edge of the pot, never the center nest.

conditions.name: Wavy brown frond edges
conditions.symptoms: Edges of fronds turn brown and wavy.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity; trim brown edges for cosmetics.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥50%; avoid drafts.

conditions.name: Scale on frond undersides
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps on the underside of fronds.
conditions.remedy: Wipe with insecticidal soap; cut heavily infested fronds.
conditions.prevention: Inspect frond undersides monthly.

conditions.name: Pale fronds in low light
conditions.symptoms: Fronds lose their vibrant green.
conditions.remedy: Move to medium indirect light.
conditions.prevention: Medium to bright indirect light; not too dark.

conditions.name: Crinkled new fronds
conditions.symptoms: New fronds unfurl wavy or deformed.
conditions.remedy: Usually natural for the 'Crissie' varieties; otherwise check for pests.
conditions.prevention: Know your cultivar — some are naturally crinkled.

facts: Water directly into the soil around the base — never into the central rosette — to avoid crown rot.
Keep humidity above 50%; bathrooms are ideal spots for a bird's nest fern.
Use a chunky, airy mix; pure potting soil stays too wet for this epiphytic fern.
Do not clean the new, fragile fronds unfurling from the centre — they are extremely easy to damage.
Asplenium nidus is an epiphyte — in the wild it lives in the branches and crotches of trees, catching leaf litter in its central rosette.
The scientific name 'nidus' is Latin for 'nest' — a reference to the bird-nest-like rosette shape.
Its fronds are undivided and strap-like, unlike most ferns, which have complex pinnate foliage.
Bird's nest fern is in the Aspleniaceae family, the same family as Hart's tongue fern.
In traditional Taiwanese cuisine, the young furled fronds of Asplenium nidus are eaten as a vegetable called 'shan su'.
Bird's nest ferns are on the NASA Clean Air Study list for removing low levels of indoor air pollutants.
The crinkled 'Crissie' and 'Victoria' cultivars have wavy, frilled frond edges — pure ornamental selections.
A mature bird's nest fern can grow fronds over 1.5 m long under perfect tropical conditions.
Bird's nest ferns are non-toxic to pets, a rarity among tropical houseplants.
Indoors, the plant rarely produces spores — in the wild, dark brown sori line the underside of mature fronds.
In Hawaii, 'ekaha' fern (Asplenium nidus) is sometimes used in hula dance costumes and leis.

care.placement_hints: One of the few palms that genuinely tolerates lower light.

lore: The definitive Victorian parlour palm — tough, low-light, and small enough for a terrarium. Exported from Central America in such numbers in the 19th century that wild populations still haven't fully recovered.
etymology: 'Chamaedorea' from Greek 'chamai' (on the ground) and 'dorea' (gift) — a gift within reach, since the fruit hangs at an accessible height; 'elegans' simply means elegant.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None — direct sun bleaches fronds
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Sparse pale growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow bleached fronds

placement_tips: One of the few palms that tolerates lower light
1–3m from a window
Humidity ≥40%
Keep above 15°C

conditions.name: Brown tips from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Leaflet tips turn brown and papery.
conditions.remedy: Raise humidity; trim tips for cosmetics.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity ≥40%; keep away from heating vents.

conditions.name: Yellow fronds from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Whole fronds yellow from the base.
conditions.remedy: Let soil dry halfway; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Water when top 3 cm is dry; don't water on a schedule.

conditions.name: Spider mites in dry air
conditions.symptoms: Stippled leaves and fine webbing.
conditions.remedy: Rinse and treat with neem; raise humidity.
conditions.prevention: Humidity ≥50%; inspect monthly.

conditions.name: Brown lower fronds
conditions.symptoms: Oldest lower fronds yellow and turn brown.
conditions.remedy: Natural aging; trim cleanly at the stem base.
conditions.prevention: Expected — palms shed old fronds continuously.

conditions.name: Stunted growth
conditions.symptoms: Plant stops making new fronds for months.
conditions.remedy: Feed at half strength monthly in growing season; check for root-bound.
conditions.prevention: Repot every 2–3 years; feed lightly in spring and summer.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Water only when the top 3 cm of soil is dry; parlour palms dislike wet feet.
Low to medium light is ideal; avoid direct sun, which scorches the fronds.
Trim off yellow fronds at the base; the plant always keeps replacing them.
Group several young plants together in a single pot — they rarely branch and look lusher in clusters.
Chamaedorea elegans is a true palm in the Arecaceae family — but one of the smallest, rarely exceeding 2 m indoors.
Parlour palms are native to southern Mexico and Guatemala, where they grow as understory plants.
Parlour palms were THE must-have plant for Victorian parlours in the 1870s — hence the common name.
They became a symbol of domestic refinement in 19th-century drawing rooms across Europe and North America.
Parlour palms are on NASA's Clean Air Study list for removing benzene and trichloroethylene from indoor air.
The genus name 'Chamaedorea' comes from the Greek for 'dwarf' and 'a gift' — possibly a reference to the low, easy-to-harvest fruit.
Male and female parlour palms are separate plants; females produce small round yellow fruits after pollination.
A mature parlour palm produces tiny yellow flower sprays that look like delicate strings of beads.
Parlour palms are non-toxic to cats and dogs, a pet-safe tropical option.
They grow extremely slowly — a 40 cm plant may be 5 years old already.
Parlour palms survive low light better than almost any other palm — even surviving in far corners of offices.

care.placement_hints: Classic Victorian parlour palm; slow-growing but long-lived.

common_conditions: brown tips from dry air or fluoride
lore: Native only to Lord Howe Island — a speck of land in the Tasman Sea. The island's economy has relied on exporting Kentia palm seeds for over a century, each painstakingly hand-collected and certified.
etymology: 'Howea' names the island; 'forsteriana' honours German naturalist Johann Reinhold Forster who sailed with Captain Cook's second voyage.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Very slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached patches, brown tips

Slow-growing; patience required
Use rain or filtered water to avoid tip browning

conditions.remedy: Switch to filtered water; raise humidity slightly.
conditions.prevention: Use rain or filtered water; flush pot quarterly.

conditions.remedy: Let soil dry halfway; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Water thoroughly then let partly dry; Kentias are drought-tolerant once settled.

conditions.name: Spider mites

conditions.remedy: Rinse and treat with neem oil weekly.
conditions.prevention: Wipe fronds monthly; inspect undersides.

conditions.name: Slow growth
conditions.symptoms: Very few new fronds each year.
conditions.remedy: Normal — Kentias grow ~1 frond per stem per year.
conditions.prevention: Expected; Kentias are slow but long-lived.

conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect; trim damaged fronds.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect only; avoid south-facing direct sun.

facts: Water when the top 5 cm of soil has dried; Kentia palms tolerate brief drought better than overwatering.
Bright indirect light is ideal — direct noon sun scorches the delicate tips.
Wipe the fronds monthly — dust accumulation cuts light absorption dramatically.
Repot only every 3–4 years; Kentia roots resent disturbance and grow slowly.
Kentia palms are in the Arecaceae family and are endemic to tiny Lord Howe Island, off the coast of Australia.
Lord Howe Island is the only natural habitat of Kentia palms; all Kentia seeds sold worldwide are still harvested there under strict quotas.
Kentia palms became Queen Victoria's favourite palm — they are still used at royal state funerals as a symbol of mourning.
In the 1890s, Kentia palm exports were the economic backbone of Lord Howe Island's economy.
Kentia palms are on NASA's Clean Air Study list for removing formaldehyde.
Mature Kentia palms can exceed 10 m in the wild and live for over 60 years.
Kentia palms are one of the slowest-growing palms — a single frond may take 6 months to unfurl.
A Kentia palm never develops a single trunk until it is about 5 years old.
Kentia palms are entirely non-toxic to humans and pets.
Luxury hotels worldwide used Kentia palms in lobbies throughout the 20th century because of their unmatched elegance.
Unlike most palms, Kentia's roots are relatively shallow, making them surprisingly easy to repot for such a large plant.

care.placement_hints: Thirstier than most palms; wants consistently moist but not soggy soil.

common_conditions: brown tips from underwatering or tap water
lore: Endangered in its Madagascar homeland but one of the most widely cultivated palms on Earth — the houseplant you're watering is far more common than the wild one. Clumping habit makes it look fuller than most indoor palms.
etymology: 'Dypsis' is of uncertain origin, possibly from Greek 'dypto' (to dive); 'lutescens' means 'becoming yellow' after the golden yellow leaf sheaths and stems.

light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin pale fronds, slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached yellow fronds

conditions.prevention: Use rain or filtered water; flush pot quarterly.

conditions.remedy: Repot into a pot 5 cm wider; refresh soil.
conditions.prevention: Repot every 2–3 years; Arecas outgrow quickly.

facts: Water when the top 3 cm of soil feels dry; areca palms like consistent moisture but not soggy roots.
Bright indirect light, or a few hours of gentle morning sun, produces the fullest fronds.
Raise humidity above 50% — dry indoor air turns the frond tips crispy and brown.
Feed with a balanced palm fertiliser every 6 weeks from spring to autumn.
Dypsis lutescens is native to eastern Madagascar and is actually listed as endangered in its wild habitat.
Areca palms are one of the few palms with multiple golden-yellow stems forming a clumping, bamboo-like cluster.
Although endangered in the wild, tens of millions are cultivated worldwide in nurseries — a cultivation success story.
The common name 'butterfly palm' comes from the way the arched fronds flutter in air movement.
Areca palms were introduced to Europe in the 1870s and instantly became a greenhouse staple.
Areca palms are one of the top-scoring plants on NASA's Clean Air Study for removing formaldehyde, xylene, and benzene.
A mature Areca can transpire over 1 litre of water per day — effectively acting as a natural humidifier.
Areca palms are non-toxic to cats and dogs.
Each clump grows from underground suckers; a crowded specimen can be divided into several new plants.
In Madagascar, the soft growing point (palm heart) is traditionally eaten — though harvesting kills the stem.
Areca palms are sometimes confused with the betel-nut palm, which is a completely different genus (Areca catechu).

care.placement_hints: Riverbank palm — wants lots of light and lots of water; struggles in average living rooms.

common_conditions: brown crispy fronds from low humidity
rapid decline in dry heated rooms
lore: Grows wild only along a handful of Madagascan rivers and is classified as Vulnerable in the wild — yet is one of the most commonly bulk-sold palms at big-box stores, where it rarely thrives indoors.
etymology: 'Ravenea' honours French botanist Louis Ravené; 'rivularis' means 'of the streams', after its riverbank habitat.

light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached patches in hottest afternoon sun

Humidity ≥55% — difficult in heated living rooms

conditions.remedy: Raise humidity dramatically; move to a bathroom or add a humidifier.

conditions.remedy: Move to brightest indirect light possible.

conditions.remedy: Shower and treat with neem weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Humidity ≥55%; weekly inspection.

conditions.remedy: Relocate to a humid bright spot; trim dead fronds.
conditions.prevention: Only buy a Majesty palm if you can provide high humidity year-round.

facts: Water deeply and often in summer; majesty palms grow naturally in riverbeds and are thirsty by palm standards.
Keep humidity above 60% — dry indoor air is the #1 killer of majesty palms.
Provide as much bright indirect light as possible; low light rapidly causes leggy, yellowing fronds.
Fertilise monthly during the growing season with a palm-specific fertiliser containing magnesium.
Ravenea rivularis is native to the banks of rivers in southern Madagascar — the species name 'rivularis' literally means 'of the riverside'.
Majesty palms are in the Arecaceae family and are listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss.
In the wild, majesty palms grow alongside lemurs — lemurs eat the fruit and disperse the seeds.
Majesty palms were introduced to the Western horticultural trade in the 1990s and rapidly became a common big-box plant.
Madagascar has hundreds of endemic palm species, many undescribed — Ravenea is one of the most iconic genera.
Majesty palms can grow over 18 m tall in their native habitat, with trunks over 40 cm thick.
Indoors, majesty palms rarely exceed 3 m due to limited root space and humidity.
Majesty palms are completely non-toxic to cats and dogs.
Each frond lives roughly 2 years before yellowing and shedding.
Unlike many palms, majesty palms will tolerate mildly flooded soil, echoing their riverside origins.
A majesty palm that turns orange-yellow all over often needs more magnesium and potassium in its fertiliser.

care.placement_hints: Soak 20-30 min weekly, shake out, dry upside down; never sit in water.

common_conditions: rot from water trapped in base
lore: Blushes bright red or purple when it's about to flower — a single event at the end of a rosette's life, after which it produces pups before dying. Needs no soil at all; absorbs water through microscopic leaf scales called trichomes.
etymology: 'Tillandsia' was dedicated to 17th-century Swedish botanist Elias Tillandz; 'ionantha' means 'violet-flowered'.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Max 2 hours filtered morning sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Faded grey-green colour, no blush before blooming
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached or brown leaf tips

placement_tips: Hang or rest in an airy open dish
Bright spot with good airflow
Soak 20–30 min weekly; dry upside-down
Never pot in soil

conditions.name: Rot from water trapped in base
conditions.symptoms: Base turns brown and mushy.
conditions.remedy: Too late to save if mushy; propagate any pups that have formed.
conditions.prevention: Always dry upside-down within 2 hours of soaking.

conditions.name: Browning tips from dry air
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips curl and brown.
conditions.remedy: Soak weekly for 30 minutes; raise humidity.
conditions.prevention: Weekly 20–30 minute soaks; mist between soaks in dry rooms.

conditions.name: Fading from insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Grey-green colour dulls; no blush before blooming.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect or filtered direct sun.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect with some direct sun triggers the red blush.

conditions.name: Mushy base after soaking
conditions.symptoms: Base feels soft after a recent soak.
conditions.remedy: Increase drying time dramatically; check for hidden water.
conditions.prevention: Shake out thoroughly; dry upside-down in a bright airy spot.

conditions.name: No pups after blooming
conditions.symptoms: After blooming, the plant doesn't produce offsets.
conditions.remedy: Pups can take months; keep up normal care and wait.
conditions.prevention: Feed with bromeliad fertilizer monthly in growing season.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Soak the whole plant in room-temperature water for 20 minutes once a week, then shake it out completely.
Never let water sit in the rosette — it causes rapid rot at the crown.
Provide bright indirect light; 2–4 hours of early morning sun is ideal.
Mist lightly between soaks if the indoor air is bone-dry.
Tillandsia ionantha is an epiphyte — it draws nothing from soil and absorbs everything through tiny scales on its leaves.
The silvery scales, called trichomes, evolved to capture water and nutrients from fog and rain.
Before flowering, Tillandsia ionantha's green leaves blush a brilliant red-pink — a signal to pollinators.
Tillandsias are in the Bromeliaceae family — the same family as pineapples.
Sky plants grow naturally from Mexico down to Nicaragua, often clinging to cactus spines.
The Aztecs reportedly used certain Tillandsias in religious ceremonies because of their ability to live 'on air'.
A sky plant flowers only once in its lifetime — producing a vivid purple tubular bloom — then slowly dies over a year while producing offsets.
Pups (offsets) form around the base after flowering; detach them once they are about 1/3 the size of the mother plant.
Tillandsia ionantha is non-toxic to pets and humans.
Sky plants are one of the few houseplants that can be grown glued or wired to driftwood, stones, or shells.
In its native range, sky plants attract hummingbirds thanks to the tubular violet flowers.

care.placement_hints: Xeric species — prefers misting and short dunks over long soaks.

common_conditions: brown center from water sitting in base
sunburn from direct midday sun through glass
lore: Hailed as the 'queen of air plants' for its size and sculpted silver rosette. Listed under CITES Appendix II due to overcollection in the wild — only nursery-grown specimens may be traded internationally.
etymology: From Greek 'xeros' (dry) and 'graphis' (writing, drawing) — the dry, curling silvery leaves look like calligraphic strokes.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours filtered direct sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin curled leaves that don't plump up
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached or brown patches from unfiltered sun

placement_tips: Display on an open stand with airflow
South or west-facing spot, filtered with a sheer curtain
Mist weekly; dunk for 5 min every 2 weeks
Never soak upright — water in the center rots the core

conditions.name: Rot from over-soaking
conditions.symptoms: Base or core darkens and softens.
conditions.remedy: Cut away rotten tissue; pray.
conditions.prevention: Short dunks (5 minutes) every 2 weeks; mist more than soak.

conditions.name: Curled tips from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Silvery leaves curl tightly inward.
conditions.remedy: Mist or give a 5-minute dunk; uncurls within a day.
conditions.prevention: Mist weekly; dunk every 2 weeks.

conditions.name: Brown center

conditions.remedy: Check for trapped water; dry thoroughly.
conditions.prevention: Never soak upright; always dry upside-down.

conditions.name: Sunburn through glass
conditions.symptoms: Bleached or brown patches on leaves in strong windowsill sun.
conditions.remedy: Move 1m back from the window; filter with a sheer curtain.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect with filtered direct sun is ideal — glass intensifies burn risk.

conditions.name: Thinning leaves
conditions.symptoms: Older outer leaves turn thin and brittle.
conditions.remedy: Normal aging; gently remove dead leaves.
conditions.prevention: Normal lifecycle; feed with bromeliad fertilizer monthly.

facts: Soak for 30 minutes every 2 weeks — xerographica is more drought-tolerant than most air plants.
Dry completely upside down after soaking so water drains from the curled leaves.
Bright indirect light or gentle morning sun produces the fullest silvery rosette.
Unlike many Tillandsias, xerographica tolerates lower humidity and dry room air well.
Tillandsia xerographica is native to the dry forests of southern Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras.
The species name 'xerographica' comes from the Greek for 'dry writing' — referring to the curled, pencil-like leaves.
Xerographica's silvery trichomes give it its near-white appearance and help reflect the harsh tropical dry-forest sun.
It is listed on CITES Appendix II — international trade is regulated to prevent wild poaching.
Xerographica became an interior design icon in the 2010s — used in wedding bouquets and minimalist installations.
In parts of Guatemala, wild xerographicas are still an endangered target for commercial collection despite protections.
Tillandsia xerographica is known as the 'Queen of Air Plants' because of its large, sculptural form.
A mature xerographica can reach 80 cm across and live for more than 20 years.
When it flowers, xerographica sends up a branched spike over 60 cm tall with bright chartreuse-green and red blooms.
Xerographica flowers only once in its lifetime, then produces multiple pups from its base.
Xerographica is non-toxic to pets and humans.

care.placement_hints: Hang with good airflow; mist daily or dunk 2-3 times a week.

common_conditions: rot from staying wet against a surface
lore: Neither Spanish nor moss — it is a bromeliad, and French colonists nicknamed it 'Spanish beard' to mock the Spanish; the Spanish replied with 'French hair'. Used as mattress stuffing across the American South for centuries.
etymology: 'Usneoides' means 'resembling Usnea', the unrelated lichen it mimics in form.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Max 2 hours filtered sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thinning clump, dull grey colour
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached or brown strands in harsh sun

placement_tips: Hang with excellent airflow — never against a wall
Mist daily in dry rooms or dunk 5 min every 3 days
Bright spot away from cold drafts
Humidity ≥50% preferred

conditions.name: Browning from stagnant air
conditions.symptoms: Strands turn brown and brittle where airflow is poor.
conditions.remedy: Relocate to an airier spot; trim dead sections.
conditions.prevention: Hang with good airflow; avoid closed corners.

conditions.name: Thinning from too little water
conditions.symptoms: The clump thins out and loses its lush grey-green.
conditions.remedy: Mist daily or dunk for 5 min every 3 days.
conditions.prevention: Mist daily in dry rooms; more frequent care than ionantha.

conditions.name: Rot against a wet surface
conditions.symptoms: Sections touching a wet wall or soil turn brown.
conditions.remedy: Move off damp surfaces.
conditions.prevention: Hang free; never against anything that holds moisture.

conditions.name: Fading in low light
conditions.symptoms: Loses the silvery-grey colour, turns dull.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect; a little filtered direct sun is fine.

conditions.name: Chunks falling off
conditions.symptoms: Sections of the clump drop to the floor.
conditions.remedy: Re-hang the fallen pieces; they continue growing separately.
conditions.prevention: Natural propagation; every dropped chunk is a new plant.

facts: Mist Spanish moss thoroughly 3 times a week, or dunk it briefly in water once a week.
Hang in bright indirect light with strong air movement; stagnant air causes rot.
Never soak Spanish moss for long periods — it stays wet too long and breaks down.
Trim off any brown or dry strands; healthy tissue is silvery-green and flexible.
Tillandsia usneoides is an epiphyte that hangs from trees — its roots, when present, anchor only, not feed.
Despite the common name, Spanish moss is neither Spanish nor a moss — it is a bromeliad, related to pineapples.
The species name 'usneoides' means 'resembling Usnea' — a reference to the lichen Usnea it looks like.
Native Americans used Spanish moss as bedding, baby padding, and arrow shaft wrapping long before European contact.
In the 19th and early 20th century, Spanish moss was the primary stuffing for Henry Ford's Model T upholstery.
French settlers in Louisiana called it 'Barbe Espagnole' (Spanish beard) — the common name stuck.
Spanish moss produces tiny, pale green-yellow flowers that smell faintly sweet at night.
Spanish moss shrouds entire oak forests in the southern United States, from Texas through Florida.
Spanish moss is non-toxic, but chiggers can sometimes live in freshly-harvested outdoor strands.
Spanish moss can be dried and used indoors as decorative filler around arrangements — but dried strands are not alive.
A healthy hanging strand can grow over 7 m long in wild cypress swamps.

care.placement_hints: Mount with base slightly elevated so water runs off; bulb-base tolerates dry.

common_conditions: rot in the hollow bulb base from trapped water
loss of leaves from extreme dryness
lore: Named for the Greek Gorgon: writhing silver-green tendrils emerge from a bulbous base like snakes from a severed head. The hollow base is often colonised by ants in the wild in a mutually beneficial arrangement.
etymology: 'Caput-medusae' is Latin for 'head of Medusa'.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun tolerated
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale curled leaves, slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached leaf tips in intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: Mount with the base slightly tilted down so water runs off
Bright spot with airflow
Soak 15–20 min weekly; shake out thoroughly
Never mount in moss that holds moisture against the bulb

conditions.name: Rot in the hollow bulb
conditions.symptoms: Bulb base turns soft and discoloured.
conditions.remedy: Too late to save if deep; propagate any pups.
conditions.prevention: Mount with the base tilted down; never soak upright.

conditions.name: Curling leaves from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Tentacle leaves curl tightly and look silvery.
conditions.remedy: Soak for 20 minutes; uncurls within hours.
conditions.prevention: Weekly 15–20 min soaks; shake out thoroughly.

conditions.name: Pale colour in low light
conditions.symptoms: Loses the rich green-grey tone.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright indirect.
conditions.prevention: Bright indirect light; medusae tolerate some direct morning sun.

conditions.name: Leaf loss from extreme dryness
conditions.symptoms: Tentacle leaves break off when very dry.
conditions.remedy: Soak thoroughly; new leaves replace them.
conditions.prevention: Never stretch past 2 weeks without water.

conditions.name: No pups
conditions.symptoms: After blooming, no new pups form.
conditions.remedy: Wait — can take 6 months; keep up normal care.
conditions.prevention: Feed with bromeliad fertilizer monthly during growing season.

facts: Soak for 20 minutes once a week; dry upside down for several hours so water escapes the curled leaves.
Bright indirect light or 2–3 hours of gentle direct sun is ideal.
Provide good air circulation — a stagnant bathroom is the fastest way to rot a caput-medusae.
Avoid tap water with high chlorine; rainwater or filtered water is best.
Tillandsia caput-medusae means 'head of Medusa' — the snaking, twisted leaves resemble the mythical Greek monster's snakes.
This species is a bromeliad in the Bromeliaceae family, just like pineapples and Spanish moss.
It is endemic to Mexico and Central America, where it clings to tree branches in dry tropical forests.
The bulbous base of caput-medusae hides a hollow chamber where ants often make nests — a symbiotic relationship.
These ant colonies aggressively defend the plant from herbivores in exchange for shelter — a classic mutualism.
Caput-medusae was first described scientifically by Edouard Morren in 1880.
Medusa's head blooms only once — producing a spike of tubular pink, purple, or violet flowers before slowly dying.
After flowering, the mother plant produces 2–4 pups from its base that grow into new plants.
Like other air plants, Tillandsia caput-medusae is completely non-toxic to pets and humans.
It can happily live on a seashell, piece of driftwood, or mounted on a bare wall — anywhere with enough light and airflow.
Medusa's head can live over 10 years with proper care, and a mature cluster can hold a dozen plants.

care.placement_hints: Vining habit; prefers to climb or cascade; tolerates low light but grows best in bright indirect.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets and children (calcium oxalate crystals).

common_conditions: loss of variegation in low light
small leaves as a sign of light stress
lore: Known as the 'easiest houseplant' by generations of growers; it originated in the wet rainforests of Mexico and Guatemala, where it climbs tree trunks.
etymology: 'Hederaceum' comes from Latin 'hedera' (ivy), referencing its trailing, ivy-like growth habit.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: No direct sun needed; tolerates 1–2 hours morning sun.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slower growth, leaves become smaller and farther apart, variegation fades to solid green.
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Scorched brown patches on leaves, especially in harsh afternoon sun.

placement_tips: Excellent for hanging baskets or trailing along shelves.
Can be trained to climb a moss pole or stake.
Adapts to low light but grows faster in medium to bright indirect light.
Humidity above 40% promotes faster growth and larger leaves.

conditions.name: Yellowing leaves from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy leaves that turn yellow and translucent; new growth appears weak.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering for 1–2 weeks, improve ventilation, and inspect roots for rot. Trim affected leaves.
conditions.prevention: Allow top inch of soil to dry between waterings; use a pot with drainage holes.

conditions.name: Loss of variegation
conditions.symptoms: Solid green leaves appear; the cream or white variegation disappears.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; variegated leaves will emerge over the next flush of growth.
conditions.prevention: Maintain at least medium light; variegation requires more light than solid green foliage.

conditions.name: Leggy growth
conditions.symptoms: Long stems with few leaves; excessive distance between nodes.
conditions.remedy: Pinch off the growth tip to encourage branching; increase light levels.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light and rotate the pot weekly.

conditions.name: Small leaves
conditions.symptoms: New growth produces noticeably smaller leaves than mature ones.
conditions.remedy: Increase light and feed monthly with balanced fertilizer during growing season.
conditions.prevention: Ensure medium or brighter light; feed every 2–3 weeks spring–fall.

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips turn brown and crispy, often with a yellow halo.
conditions.remedy: Improve humidity by misting or grouping plants; reduce fertilizer strength.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 40%; avoid fertilizer burn by using half-strength solution.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: The common name 'sweetheart plant' refers to the heart-shaped leaves, which are soft and velvety to the touch.
Philodendron hederaceum produces climbing aerial roots even indoors, signaling it wants vertical structure.
It easily propagates from single-node cuttings placed in water; new roots emerge in 7–10 days.
The genus name 'Philodendron' comes from Greek 'philo' (love) and 'dendron' (tree).
In its native rainforest habitat, this species climbs tree trunks to reach better light in the canopy.
Variegated cultivars like 'Brasil' have cream or white sections; they require brighter light than solid green forms.
The plant tolerates low light but will grow much faster and produce larger leaves under medium to bright conditions.
Older specimens often develop long woody stems if not regularly pinched back for bushier growth.
A mature trailing specimen can grow 2–3 meters long; it's ideal for shelves, bookcases, or wall-mounted trellises.
It's one of the few houseplants that adapts equally well to water propagation or soil, staying green indefinitely in vases.
In Feng Shui, trailing plants like this symbolize flowing energy and continuity; they're placed on high shelves.
The petioles (leaf stems) are flexible and allow the leaves to adjust their angle toward light sources.
Misting the leaves weekly will increase humidity and reduce the risk of spider mites and brown tips.
Pest infestations spread quickly in low-light conditions; improving light and air circulation is preventative.
Pet owners should wash hands after handling and keep the plant out of reach, as all parts contain oxalates.

care.placement_hints: Climbs readily; pink coloration intensifies with more light; needs bright indirect to thrive.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets and children (calcium oxalate crystals).

common_conditions: pink color fades to pure green in low light
lore: The 'Pink Princess' cultivar became wildly popular on social media for its dramatic pink variegation; some specimens sell for over $100 due to rarity and slow propagation.
etymology: 'Erubescens' comes from Latin 'erubesco' (to blush), referring to the pinkish-red undersides of new leaves and the pink variegation.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours of morning sun; avoid harsh afternoon rays.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pink variegation disappears entirely; leaves revert to solid green; growth stalls.
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf bleaching or tan spots on sun-exposed leaves; colors fade to dull pink.

placement_tips: Place within 1 meter of an east or west-facing window for best pink coloration.
The pink intensifies with more light, but avoid direct afternoon sun in summer.
Train it to climb a moss pole or stake to encourage fuller, more colorful growth.
Rotate the pot monthly to ensure even light exposure and prevent lopsided growth.

conditions.name: Pink color fades to green
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge solid green with no pink variegation; older pink leaves lose color.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location (1m from a bright window); pink coloration will return in new growth within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; rotate the pot weekly to ensure even exposure.

conditions.name: Slow growth without brightness
conditions.symptoms: Leaves are smaller and farther apart; plant stalls in winter or dim rooms.
conditions.remedy: Increase light; consider grow lights if natural light is insufficient.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light year-round; use supplemental grow lights if needed.

conditions.name: Yellowing leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves turn soft and yellow; stems weaken at the base.
conditions.remedy: Reduce watering frequency; allow soil to dry 1–2 inches between waterings.
conditions.prevention: Improve drainage; use a fast-draining aroid mix; never let the pot sit in water.

conditions.name: Leaning toward light
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows asymmetrically toward the brightest window.
conditions.remedy: Rotate the pot 180° every 3–4 days; use a small stake to guide upright growth.
conditions.prevention: Rotate the pot weekly; maintain even light from multiple directions.

conditions.name: Leaf burn from direct sun
conditions.symptoms: Tan or bleached patches on leaves facing the sun.
conditions.remedy: Move back 30 cm from the window; trim damaged leaves.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light; filter direct afternoon sun with a sheer curtain.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: The variegated pink sections are caused by chimeric tissue, where some cells lack chlorophyll, producing the striking pink hue.
Not all pink is the same: some specimens show deep pink, others pale pink, depending on genetics and light exposure.
The Pink Princess cultivar is so expensive because it does not propagate readily from cuttings; every plant is still relatively rare.
Leaf undersides are often a deep burgundy or red, especially on new growth; this is where the 'blushing' name originates.
In its native Colombian cloud forests, this species climbs tree trunks in dappled, bright filtered sunlight.
The petioles are reddish or pink, adding to the overall aesthetic appeal of the plant.
It grows slower than plain green Philodendron hederaceum but still propagates easily in water.
The more light it receives, the more intensely colored the variegation; some growers use grow lights to maximize pink.
Mature vining specimens can reach 3 meters or more when trained on a trellis; older plants develop thicker, woodier stems.
Single-node cuttings root in 1–2 weeks in water; they transition to soil after roots are 1–2 cm long.
In Feng Shui, pink plants are associated with love and harmony; this is traditionally placed in bedroom areas.
The plant is sensitive to cold; temperatures below 15°C can trigger leaf drop and color loss.
Humidity above 50% encourages faster growth and deeper pink coloration in variegated sections.
Regular feeding (every 2 weeks) during growing season supports pink coloration and robust growth.
This is one of the most photographed houseplants on Instagram, spurring both demand and price speculation.

care.placement_hints: Compact, bushy growth; excellent for desks and shelves; water sparingly to avoid root rot.

lore: Called 'baby rubber plant' because its thick leaves resemble those of the larger rubber tree (Ficus elastica), but in miniature; it's a succulent-leaning tropical.
etymology: 'Peperomia' derives from the Greek 'piperis' (pepper) and '-omia' (suffix denoting resemblance); 'obtusifolia' means 'blunt leaves' in Latin.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours of gentle morning sun; avoid harsh afternoon rays.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Weak pale growth; leaves become thin and smaller; slower overall development.
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Faded color, bleached patches if exposed to intense direct sun.

placement_tips: Ideal for office desks and small spaces due to its compact, mounding habit.
Prefers higher humidity but tolerates dry indoor air; mist if leaves look dull.
Keep away from heating vents and cold drafts; it dislikes temperature swings.
Repot only when roots emerge from the pot; Peperomia prefers slightly snug pots.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy stems at the base; leaves wilt despite wet soil; sudden leaf drop.
conditions.remedy: Remove from pot, trim away black mushy roots, allow stems to dry 2 days, repot in fresh succulent mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when the top inch of soil is dry; reduce watering in winter by 50%.

conditions.name: Wilting despite moist soil
conditions.symptoms: Leaves droop even when soil feels wet; may indicate root decay.
conditions.remedy: Check for root rot; if roots are soft and dark, unpot and repot in dry mix.
conditions.prevention: Ensure excellent drainage; use a pot with large drainage holes; never let water sit in the saucer.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf loss after exposure to temperatures below 13°C.
conditions.remedy: Move to a warmer location; trim off any blackened stems.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 15°C; avoid windowsills in winter; keep away from air conditioning.

conditions.name: Pale weak growth
conditions.symptoms: New leaves are smaller and paler; growth is very slow.
conditions.remedy: Move to medium light; feed monthly with diluted liquid fertilizer.
conditions.prevention: Maintain medium light; feed every 2–3 weeks during growing season.

conditions.name: Leggy sparse stems
conditions.symptoms: Long stems with few leaves; compact bushy form is lost.
conditions.remedy: Pinch off growth tips regularly; increase light exposure.
conditions.prevention: Provide medium to bright indirect light; pinch growing tips monthly to encourage branching.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Peperomia leaves are thick and succulent due to high water storage capacity, reducing the need for frequent watering.
The common name 'Pepper face' refers to the slightly peppery scent of crushed leaves, related to its distant cousin the black pepper plant.
The genus Peperomia contains over 1,500 species, all small tropical epiphytes from Central and South America.
It grows naturally on moss-covered tree bark, explaining its preference for chunky, well-draining soil.
The plant produces tiny flower spikes (inflorescences) that look like miniature white or pale pink rat tails.
Peperomia propagates effortlessly: a single leaf on moist soil will produce roots and a new plant within 3–4 weeks.
Unlike most houseplants, Peperomia has fleshy petioles and leaves that easily release from the plant body when mature.
In tropical nurseries, it's one of the most propagated houseplants due to ease and compact final size.
The thick leaves have a slightly waxy cuticle that helps the plant retain water in dry indoor environments.
Peperomia thrives in terrariums because it tolerates high humidity without becoming susceptible to root rot.
The plant's compact, rounded form means it rarely requires pruning or training; it mounds naturally.
Variegated Peperomia varieties like 'Marble' and 'Watermelon' are available, though all have the same care requirements.
It's popular in office environments because it tolerates air conditioning and artificial light well.
The plant is native to humid rainforest understories, though it adapts surprisingly well to dry indoor air.
Peperomia is non-toxic, making it safe for homes with curious pets and children.

care.placement_hints: Striking patterned foliage; likes humid environments; very sensitive to overwatering.

common_conditions: waterlogged soil leading to stem decay
lore: The watermelon-like pink and green striped pattern on its leaves earned it a place in Victorian parlors as a curiosity plant; it remains a favorite for patterned foliage enthusiasts.
etymology: 'Argyreia' comes from Greek 'argyros' (silver), referencing the silvery-gray striping that contrasts with the green.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: No direct sun; filtered indirect light is ideal.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pattern fades; leaves lose their striped contrast and appear more uniformly dark.
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Color washes out; silver stripes become pale or invisible; leaf may bleach.

placement_tips: Position in a north or east-facing window where light is bright but filtered.
Use a pebble tray with water beneath the pot to increase humidity without waterlogging roots.
Mist the leaves 2–3 times per week to maintain the vibrant pattern and prevent spider mites.
Keep away from heat sources; constant warmth and low humidity dull the pattern.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft blackened stems, mushy roots, sudden leaf collapse.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, remove all rotted tissue, allow stems to air-dry 24–48 hours, repot in fresh gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when top half-inch is dry; reduce frequency in winter by 40%.

conditions.name: Waterlogged soil decay
conditions.symptoms: Stems soften and blacken at the base; leaves wilt despite wet soil.
conditions.remedy: Improve drainage by mixing perlite into soil; water less frequently; ensure pot has adequate drainage.
conditions.prevention: Use a pot with large drainage holes; use a succulent or orchid mix for better air space.

conditions.name: Pattern fading in poor light
conditions.symptoms: Watermelon stripes lose contrast; leaves appear uniformly dark green.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a window or bright area; the pattern will return in new growth within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain medium light; rotate monthly to ensure even exposure.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf loss after exposure to temperatures below 13°C.
conditions.remedy: Move to a warmer location; trim blackened stems.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 15°C; avoid cold drafts and air conditioning vents.

conditions.name: Crispy leaf edges
conditions.symptoms: Leaf margins turn brown and papery; pattern becomes dull.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity by misting; move away from heating vents.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 50%; mist 3 times per week.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.

conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
