conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Senecio rowleyanus earns its common name 'string of pearls' from the distinctive spherical leaves that resemble a strand of beads on a thread.
The spherical shape is an evolutionary adaptation — the rounded form minimises surface area, reducing water loss in arid climates.
Each leaf acts as a water-storage organ; the translucent tissue inside allows light to reach internal photosynthetic cells even when the leaf is embedded in sand.
The plant can produce small white flowers with a distinctive cinnamon-like fragrance, though flowering is rare indoors.
String of Pearls is a fast-growing succulent when light and water are optimised; mature strands can exceed 1 metre in just 2–3 years.
The plant contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, mild toxins that cause GI upset in pets; ingestion is rarely serious but should be avoided.
Unlike some succulents, String of Pearls benefits from more consistent moisture; it prefers drying between waterings rather than bone-dry dormancy.
Propagation is trivial — individual pearls can be removed and pressed into soil where they root within 1–2 weeks.
The plant tolerates a wide range of temperatures but suffers in cold conditions below 10°C and prefers warmth above 15°C.
In South African habitats, String of Pearls grows as a trailing groundcover on rocky, sandy soils with minimal organic matter.
The trailing habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets and mounted planters; the cascading strands can be 60+ cm long.
Regular fertilising (monthly during the growing season) significantly boosts growth rate and pearl size.
The plant is sensitive to overwatering combined with cold — root rot is the most common failure in temperate climates with poor drainage.
Repotting annually encourages vigorous growth and prevents the plant from becoming root-bound.
A mature String of Pearls basket can produce dozens of strands, each draped with hundreds of pearls, creating a visually striking cascading display.

care.placement_hints: Trailing vine with heart-shaped variegated leaves; tubers form along stems for vegetative propagation.

lore: String of hearts is prized by collectors for its trailing habit and ability to produce tiny tubers along the stems, creating a cascade of beads. In Victorian times it was propagated by pinning tubers to moist soil to create new plantlets.
etymology: Ceropegia means 'wax fountain' in Greek, referring to the waxy, tubular flowers; woodii honours John Medley Wood, a South African botanist.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours morning sun tolerated; afternoon sun fades the variegation
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Long gaps between leaf pairs, vines stretch, variegation dulls to uniform green
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Heart leaves bleach pale or develop scorched edges in intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: Hang in a bright window with dappled afternoon shade; east-facing is ideal
Trailing pots work best — allow vines to cascade freely over the edge
Keep above 10°C; sudden cold snaps cause leaf drop within hours
Propagate tubers by pressing into moist soil: each becomes a new plant

conditions.name: Tuber rot
conditions.symptoms: Tubers become mushy, blackened, or smell rotten; vine segments detach at the tuber.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, separate healthy vine sections from rotted tubers, let air-dry for 24h, repot in cactus mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is dry; never leave the pot sitting in a saucer of water.

conditions.name: Stem shrivelling
conditions.symptoms: Stems become thin and papery; leaves shrivel and drop; plant dries out rapidly.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot for 10 minutes, then water deeply. Let drain fully. Resume watering weekly.
conditions.prevention: Don't let soil completely dry for more than 2 weeks; check the soil once weekly.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold
conditions.symptoms: Sudden yellowing and dropping of leaves after exposure to temps below 10°C.
conditions.remedy: Move away from cold window or door; do not water for 48 hours. New growth resumes in warmth.
conditions.prevention: Never place on a cold windowsill; keep away from exterior doors and AC vents in winter.

conditions.name: Sparse growth and weak vines
conditions.symptoms: Vines grow slowly, with large gaps between pairs of leaves; overall vine is thin and lanky.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window; provide 3–4 hours of diffuse sunlight daily.
conditions.prevention: Place within 1m of a bright, indirect light source; rotate quarterly.

conditions.name: Variegation loss
conditions.symptoms: Heart leaves turn uniform dark green; white or pink variegation disappears.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light — variegation usually returns within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; insufficient light causes the plant to revert to solid colour.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: String of hearts produces tiny tubers along its stems — press these into moist soil and they root to form new plants.
The heart-shaped leaves are semi-succulent and store water to survive brief dry spells.
Unlike most vining plants, string of hearts prefers to dry out completely between waterings.
Variegation in the leaves is a stress response to light; bright light maintains the white or pink margins.
The plant can survive on moisture from morning dew in its native habitat, making it extremely drought-tolerant.
String of hearts is one of the few succulents that thrives in trailing pots and hanging baskets.
The vines can grow 2–3 metres long if left unpruned, creating a thick curtain of foliage.
In southern Africa, wild populations grow as epiphytes on trees, using their tubers to anchor to bark.
The plant is completely non-toxic and safe around pets and children.
Overwatering is the number-one killer; the tiny tubers rot in soggy conditions within days.
Each leaf-pair is connected to a small tuber along the stem, acting as a water-storage reservoir.
String of hearts flowers produce waxy, tubular blooms that attract insects — they are rarely seen indoors.
The genus Ceropegia contains over 200 species, most from Africa, but C. woodii is the most common houseplant.
Propagation from tubers is more reliable than leaf cuttings; each tuber becomes a full plant within 8 weeks.
The plant enters semi-dormancy in winter, slowing growth significantly — water less frequently.

care.placement_hints: Leaves stack along the stem like beads on a string; develops reddish tones in bright light.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets (gastrointestinal upset if ingested).

common_conditions: pale stacked leaves indicating light hunger
compact tight growth in strong sunlight
lore: String of buttons is beloved for its mathematical perfection — leaves spiral around the stem in a Fibonacci sequence, creating a mesmerizing three-dimensional bead effect. Gardeners have been fascinated by this arrangement for centuries.
etymology: Crassula means 'thick' in Latin, referring to the succulent leaves; perforata means 'pierced through', describing how the stem passes through the leaf-pairs.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4–6 hours direct sun daily; benefits from morning sun and afternoon shade in extreme heat
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Stems stretch and elongate, leaves spread away from the stem, plant loses the bead-like appearance
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf tips bleach or scorch in intense afternoon sun; usually recovers if moved to bright indirect light

placement_tips: Place in a south or west-facing window with direct morning or afternoon sun
Reddish leaf colouration indicates optimal light; maintain those conditions year-round
Use a shallow pot with excellent drainage to prevent root rot and keep soil drying fast
Repot every 2–3 years; refresh the top layer of soil to maintain nutrient balance

conditions.name: Etiolation and stretching
conditions.symptoms: Stems elongate with large gaps between leaf-pairs; leaves spread away from the stem; plant becomes sparse.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter light immediately; trim the stretched stems and propagate them in bright indirect light.
conditions.prevention: Provide at least 4 hours of direct or bright indirect sun daily; rotate quarterly.

conditions.name: Brittle fragile stems
conditions.symptoms: Stems become thin, brittle, and snap easily even with light handling.
conditions.remedy: Increase watering frequency slightly (every 7–10 days) and provide brighter light to strengthen the plant.
conditions.prevention: Water when the top 1 cm of soil is dry; avoid prolonged drought periods of 3+ weeks.

conditions.name: Pale leaf stacking
conditions.symptoms: Leaves lose their vibrant colour, become pale or yellowish, and lose the tight stacking arrangement.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location; pale colour indicates the plant is not photosynthesizing efficiently.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright direct light; pale growth is an early warning of inadequate illumination.

conditions.name: Leaf discolouration and fading
conditions.symptoms: Leaves turn dull grey or muddy green; the plant loses visual appeal despite being healthy.
conditions.remedy: Gradually increase light exposure over 1–2 weeks; improved colour returns within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide consistent bright light; this succulent's beauty depends on good illumination.

conditions.name: Root crown rot
conditions.symptoms: Base of stems become mushy or black; the plant topples despite firm soil.
conditions.remedy: Cut away rotted sections with a sterile knife, dust with cinnamon, let callus for 2 days, repot in fresh gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Use gritty, fast-draining soil; water at the soil level, never on the foliage or crown.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.

conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: String of buttons leaves grow in opposite pairs that are perfectly stacked, creating a 3D bead effect.
The leaf stacking follows a Fibonacci spiral pattern — a mathematical arrangement found throughout nature.
In bright light, leaves develop reddish or pink edges and tips, indicating peak photosynthesis.
The plant is semi-dormant in winter and requires very little water from October through February.
String of buttons is mildly toxic to cats and dogs, causing gastrointestinal upset if chewed.
The stems are brittle and break easily when underwatered; keeping soil slightly moist prevents snapping.
Crassula perforata produces tiny pale yellow flowers in spring if light conditions are optimal.
The plant grows slowly but steadily, gaining about 2–3 inches per year under good conditions.
Unlike many succulents, string of buttons prefers slightly moist soil during the growing season.
Propagation is easy: snap off a stem and insert into dry soil; roots form within 7–10 days.
The 'perforata' name refers to how the stem appears to pierce through the center of each leaf-pair.
This succulent thrives in small pots because its shallow roots don't need much soil.
String of buttons is native to South Africa's Eastern Cape, a region with Mediterranean-like climate patterns.
The plant will cascade beautifully from a hanging pot or elevated shelf.
Compact tight growth and reddish leaf colour indicate the plant is receiving maximum light and appropriate water.

care.placement_hints: Often mistaken for a cactus but is a euphorbia with caustic white milky sap that irritates skin and eyes.
care.toxicity: Toxic — milky white sap is caustic; causes severe skin and eye irritation. Use gloves when handling.

common_conditions: sap burn on skin from handling without gloves
sap-induced eye irritation if rubbed after contact
root rot from overwatering the thick caudex
leggy growth and branch loss in low light
lore: African milk tree is prized for its striking architectural form, resembling a miniature cathedral with its three-sided branches reaching skyward. Interestingly, it is not a cactus despite its spine-like projections and tree-like growth habit. Traditional healers in central Africa have used the sap (carefully) for various medicinal purposes.
etymology: Euphorbia is named after Euphorbus, a Greek physician; 'trigona' means three-angled, referring to the distinctive three-sided stem.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–4 hours morning sun; afternoon shade prevents scorching and maintains upright form
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Stems lean or stretch toward light, branch dieback occurs, growth becomes sparse
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Stem tips scorch and turn brown; the plant may drop lower branches

placement_tips: Place near a bright window with dappled afternoon shade — avoid harsh south-facing direct sun
ALWAYS wear gloves when handling; the milky sap causes severe skin irritation and persistent rash
If sap contacts eyes, flush immediately with copious amounts of water for at least 15 minutes
Keep away from children and pets; this plant is toxic and the sap injury risk is significant

conditions.name: Caustic sap burn on skin
conditions.symptoms: Itching, redness, blistering, or burning sensation on skin within minutes of contact; may develop into a persistent rash.
conditions.remedy: Wash affected area immediately and thoroughly with soap and water; apply hydrocortisone cream if rash develops.
conditions.prevention: ALWAYS wear latex or nitrile gloves when handling or pruning; wash hands immediately after.

conditions.name: Eye irritation from sap contact
conditions.symptoms: Severe burning, redness, tearing, and light sensitivity if sap contacts the eye.
conditions.remedy: Flush eyes with clean water for 15+ minutes; seek medical attention if irritation persists.
conditions.prevention: Never touch your face after handling; wash gloves before removing them; keep away from young children.

conditions.name: Root rot of the caudex
conditions.symptoms: Base of the plant becomes soft or mushy; the thick trunk loses firmness and may collapse.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, cut away all soft tissue, dust with cinnamon, air-dry for 5 days, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Allow soil to dry completely between waterings; never water in winter; use a pot with excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Branch dieback from cold
conditions.symptoms: Branches blacken, shrivel, or fall off after exposure to temperatures below 10°C.
conditions.remedy: Move to a warm location immediately; do not water for 1 week. Remove dead branches with sterile shears.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 13°C; never expose to drafts or cold windowsills; avoid outdoor placement in winter.

conditions.name: Leaning and sparse branching
conditions.symptoms: The plant loses its upright pyramidal form, leans to one side, and lower branches drop off.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter light; prune the top to encourage branching. The plant may take 6+ months to regain shape.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; rotate the pot quarterly to encourage even growth on all sides.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Euphorbia trigona is NOT a cactus despite its cactus-like appearance; it's a member of the Euphorbia genus.
The milky white sap is highly caustic and can cause severe dermatitis and persistent blistering.
Handling the plant without gloves can result in a painful rash lasting several days to weeks.
If sap enters the eyes, it causes severe burning and temporary vision impairment; medical attention may be needed.
The stem is three-sided (triangular) with small red leaves and thorn-like projections along the ridges.
African milk tree can grow 1.5–2 metres tall indoors under ideal conditions, but growth is slow.
The plant produces small, inconspicuous pink flowers at the stem tips during the growing season.
Unlike cacti, it requires well-draining soil but slightly more frequent watering than desert plants.
The plant stores water in its succulent stems, allowing it to survive brief periods of drought.
Propagation is possible from stem cuttings, but always wear gloves and let the sap dry completely before planting.
African milk tree is sensitive to cold and will drop leaves or branches if exposed to temperatures below 10°C.
It is completely non-edible and should be kept far away from pets and children due to toxicity.
The common name 'cathedral cactus' refers to its tall, columnar form with branches reaching upward.
Central African folklore attributes protective properties to this plant, though this is superstition.
Repotting should be done every 2–3 years; the plant becomes root-bound very slowly.

care.placement_hints: Caudex stores water; can survive months without watering. Not actually a palm.

common_conditions: yellow wilting leaves from root issues
sparse crown growth in insufficient light
yellowing lower leaves from aging and low light
lore: Ponytail palm is a modern houseplant icon, appearing in countless interiors for its sculptural beauty. The swollen base reminded early gardeners of an elephant's foot, while the crown of long strappy leaves resembles a ponytail. It can live for decades in the same pot, growing only a few inches per year.
etymology: Beaucarnea honours Jean Beaumont de Beaumont, a Mexican botanist; recurvata means 'recurved', describing the gracefully arching leaves.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours direct sun tolerated; morning sun is ideal; afternoon heat is tolerated
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin, sparse crown; leaves become pale and lanky; growth slows dramatically
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf tips bleach or scorch in harsh afternoon sun; colour fades slightly

placement_tips: Place in a bright window where it receives at least 3 hours of indirect sunlight daily
The caudex can survive 2–3 months without water if left in moderate temperatures
Repotting is rarely necessary — the plant thrives in the same pot for 5+ years
Avoid overwatering in winter; the plant is nearly dormant and needs minimal water

conditions.name: Caudex rot and softening
conditions.symptoms: The swollen base becomes mushy, soft, or blackened; the plant may collapse.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, cut away all soft tissue with a sterile knife, dust with cinnamon, air-dry for 7+ days.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is completely dry; never water in winter; use a pot with drainage holes.

conditions.name: Yellowing wilting leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves turn yellow and droop despite the soil appearing moist; the plant loses turgidity.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering for 2 weeks; move to a brighter location. Check for root rot when repotting.
conditions.prevention: Allow soil to dry between waterings; check soil moisture with your finger before watering.

conditions.name: Sparse weak crown
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge thin, pale, and far apart; the crown looks sparse and unhealthy.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; provide 4+ hours of daily light. New growth improves within weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; the crown requires good illumination to produce healthy foliage.

conditions.name: Lower leaf yellowing and drop
conditions.symptoms: Older leaves at the base of the crown gradually turn yellow and drop over weeks.
conditions.remedy: This is normal aging; remove yellowed leaves with clean shears. Ensure the plant has adequate light.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light to slow the aging process; feed monthly during the growing season.

conditions.name: Caudex scale formation
conditions.symptoms: The base of the caudex develops a papery, cracked texture or rough scaling.
conditions.remedy: This is normal aging and protective. Do not attempt to remove it; it protects the plant.
conditions.prevention: This is a cosmetic change and indicates the plant is establishing its protective corky layer.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Ponytail palm is not a palm at all — it belongs to the Agave family and is more closely related to yuccas.
The swollen caudex at the base is a water-storage organ that allows the plant to survive months without watering.
The plant can live for 200+ years in its native Mexican habitat, making it one of the longest-lived houseplants.
Indoors, ponytail palms grow very slowly, gaining only 1–2 inches per year even with optimal care.
The leaves are long, thin, and arch gracefully downward — they should never be cut or trimmed.
This plant is completely non-toxic and safe around cats, dogs, and children.
Ponytail palms require very little fertilizer; over-feeding causes soft growth and caudex rot.
The plant produces small, cream-coloured flowers in spring if mature enough — this is rare indoors.
Repotting is rarely needed; the plant thrives in the same pot for 5–10 years without stress.
Use gritty, fast-draining cactus soil; regular potting soil holds too much moisture and causes rot.
The caudex develops a corky, scaled texture as it ages — this is normal and indicates maturity.
Overwatering is the leading cause of death; the caudex cannot tolerate sitting in wet soil.
The plant is native to the Mexican states of Tamaulipas and Veracruz, in dry tropical regions.
In Mexican folklore, the plant was called the 'elephant's foot' due to its trunk-like caudex.
Propagation from seed is possible but extremely slow; mature plants are propagated by division.

care.placement_hints: Spineless 5-ribbed star shape covered in white flecks (trichomes); needs very little water in winter.

common_conditions: sunburn after sudden intense light exposure
lore: Bishop's cap cactus is prized by collectors for its spineless form and distinctive white-speckled appearance. In its natural habitat, the tiny white hairs (trichomes) cover the plant like flour, reflecting intense desert sunlight and reducing water loss. The five-ribbed star shape is so perfect it seems almost artificial.
etymology: Astrophytum means 'star plant' in Greek, referring to the five-ribbed star shape when viewed from above; myriostigma means 'many-spotted', describing the white trichomes.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun daily; needs intense light to maintain compact form and white speckling
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Plant etiolates (stretches), loses white trichomes, becomes pale and weak, may split or crack
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sunburn appears as brown patches after sudden exposure; move to bright indirect light to recover

placement_tips: Place in a south-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun; this cactus thrives in bright light
Acclimate gradually if moving from indoor shade to direct sun to prevent sunburn
Use a shallow terracotta pot with a drainage hole; terracotta dries faster than plastic
Water sparingly October–March (every 6–8 weeks); some growers stop watering entirely during dormancy

conditions.name: Winter root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy softened body, blackened roots, foul smell; plant collapses despite dormancy.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, remove all rotted tissue, dust with cinnamon, air-dry 7+ days, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Stop watering from October through March; the plant is dormant and needs almost no water.

conditions.name: Shrivelling from drought
conditions.symptoms: Body becomes thin and wrinkled; the plant loses turgidity and becomes limp.
conditions.remedy: Water once deeply; let the pot drain fully. The plant should plump up within 24 hours.
conditions.prevention: Water every 3–4 weeks in summer; once every 6–8 weeks in winter if at all.

conditions.name: Etiolation from low light
conditions.symptoms: Body stretches upward, becomes pale, white spots fade, plant loses its compact form.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright direct light immediately; the plant will regain colour within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide 6+ hours of direct sun daily; rotate quarterly to ensure even light exposure.

conditions.name: Sunburn from sudden light exposure
conditions.symptoms: Brown or orange patches appear on the body after moving to direct sun; may develop cracks.
conditions.remedy: Move back to bright indirect light for 1–2 weeks, then gradually reintroduce direct sun.
conditions.prevention: Acclimate slowly over 2–3 weeks when moving from shade to direct light.

conditions.name: Corking of lower body
conditions.symptoms: Lower portion develops a tan, woody, cork-like texture; this is normal aging.
conditions.remedy: No action needed; this is natural maturation. The corky layer protects the cactus.
conditions.prevention: This is a natural process that occurs as the plant matures over several years.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Bishop's cap cactus is completely spineless, making it one of the safest cacti to handle without gloves.
The white trichomes (tiny hairs) that cover the body are not dust — they are living cells that reflect sunlight.
Viewed from above, the five ribs form a perfect star shape, which inspired the scientific name Astrophytum.
The cactus produces small yellow flowers with red-edged petals in late spring if light is excellent.
In its native Mexican habitat, it grows on rocky hillsides in intense, unfiltered sunlight.
Bishop's cap cactus needs almost NO water from October through March — many growers water only 2–3 times total.
The plant grows very slowly, typically gaining 1–2 centimetres per year in diameter.
Terracotta pots are ideal because they breathe and dry out faster than plastic, reducing rot risk.
The species is sometimes called 'star cactus' due to its five-ribbed star form and stellar appearance.
Overwatering is the number-one killer; this cactus evolved in arid regions and cannot tolerate constantly moist soil.
Mature plants may produce 50–100 small yellow flowers during a good bloom season.
The white flour-like covering gives the plant a distinctive appearance and helps it survive in the hot sun.
Astrophytum myriostigma is native to northeastern Mexico, where it grows on limestone and rocky slopes.
A 20-year-old plant may be only 20–30 centimetres tall, demonstrating its extremely slow growth rate.
This cactus is ideal for sunny windowsills and does not require special care beyond minimal water and bright light.

care.placement_hints: Glochids (tiny barbed bristles) lodge in skin and are nearly impossible to remove — extreme hazard.
care.toxicity: Toxic — glochids (tiny barbed bristles) lodge in skin and cause severe itching; handle with thick gloves or long sleeves.

common_conditions: glochid injury from handling without protection
spider mites under the woolly bristles
lore: Bunny ear cactus is infamous among collectors for its 'cute' appearance hiding a vicious defense mechanism. The tiny glochids — nearly invisible barbed spines — lodge deep in skin and resist removal. Despite this hazard, it remains popular for its flat, segmented form and woolly appearance.
etymology: Opuntia is named after a city in ancient Greece; microdasys means 'small hairy', describing the fine glochids covering the surface.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4–6 hours of direct sun daily; benefits from morning sun and afternoon shade
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pads become pale and weak, growth stretches and becomes lean, bristles fade
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Pads may scorch in extreme heat; colour fades slightly; move to bright indirect light

placement_tips: ALWAYS wear thick gloves or long sleeves when handling; glochids are nearly invisible and resist removal
If glochids contact skin, use tape (duct tape works best) to pull out embedded spines before they irritate further
Do NOT place where children or pets can touch the plant — the injury risk is serious
Place in a dedicated cactus corner away from high-traffic areas to prevent accidental contact

conditions.name: Glochid injury from handling
conditions.symptoms: Tiny barbed spines embed in skin, causing itching, redness, and an irritating rash that persists for days.
conditions.remedy: Use tape (duct or painter's tape) pressed firmly over the area and peeled away to remove spines; repeat as needed.
conditions.prevention: ALWAYS wear thick latex gloves, nitrile gloves, or long sleeves; never touch with bare skin.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Segments become soft and mushy; the cactus loses firmness and may collapse.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, remove soft tissue with a sterile knife, dust with cinnamon, air-dry 5–7 days, repot in dry cactus mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is completely dry; wait at least 2 weeks between waterings.

conditions.name: Etiolation from low light
conditions.symptoms: Pads become pale, thin, and stretch upward; growth becomes weak and lanky.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright direct light immediately; the plant should regain vigour within 2–3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide 4+ hours of direct sun daily; rotate quarterly to ensure even growth.

conditions.name: Segment blackening from cold
conditions.symptoms: Pads turn black or dark brown after exposure to cold (below 5°C); affected segments die.
conditions.remedy: Move to warm location; do not water for 2 weeks. Remove dead segments cleanly with sterile shears.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 10°C; never place near cold windows or exterior doors in winter.

conditions.name: Spider mites under glochids
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing among the woolly bristles; stippled discolouration on segments; leaves dull.
conditions.remedy: Use neem oil or insecticidal soap; the bristles make treatment difficult — apply thoroughly and repeat weekly.
conditions.prevention: Inspect regularly; keep humidity below 40% to discourage spider mite colonization.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.

conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: The 'cute' fuzzy bristles are glochids — barbed microscopic spines that lodge deeply in skin and resist removal.
Glochids are nearly invisible to the naked eye but cause severe itching and irritation for days after contact.
Duct tape is the most effective tool for removing glochids — press and peel repeatedly over the affected area.
Bunny ear cactus is native to the arid scrublands of central and northern Mexico.
The plant produces small yellow flowers in spring if light conditions are excellent.
Despite its innocent appearance, this cactus is dangerous; it should never be placed where children or pets can reach it.
The flat, ear-shaped segments are called cladodes and are actually modified stems, not leaves.
New pads develop from the edges of mature segments and grow rapidly during the warm season.
The woolly appearance comes from the dense covering of glochids, which evolved as a sun-reflecting defense.
Bunny ear cactus produces a toxic sap that can cause gastrointestinal upset if ingested.
The plant is extremely drought-tolerant and can survive months without water in its dormant state.
In warm climates, it spreads aggressively and is considered invasive in Mediterranean regions.
Propagation is easy: detach a healthy pad, let it dry for 5 days, and press into dry soil.
The plant thrives on neglect — overwatering is the most common cause of failure.
A mature plant can reach 60–90 centimetres tall over 5–7 years with minimal care.

care.placement_hints: Epiphytic trailing spineless cactus. Only cactus native to Old World (Africa, Sri Lanka). Needs more water than desert cacti.

origin: Tropical Americas, Africa, Sri Lanka (only cactus native to the Old World)
lore: Mistletoe cactus is unique among cacti — it is the only member of the family native to Africa, Sri Lanka, and other parts of the Old World. Botanists believe its seeds travelled on migratory birds from Central America millions of years ago. In its native tropical rainforests, it grows as an epiphyte on tree branches, creating cascading green chains.
etymology: Rhipsalis comes from the Greek 'rhips' (wicker basket), referring to its woven appearance; baccifera means 'berry-bearing', describing the white berries.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 1–2 hours morning sun tolerated; afternoon sun causes bleaching and leaf drop
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Stems become thin and pale, growth slows dramatically, plant becomes sparse and leggy
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaves bleach to pale green or yellow, plant loses colour, may develop sunburn

placement_tips: Hang in a bright window with dappled afternoon shade; east-facing is perfect for morning sun
Never use direct afternoon sun — unlike desert cacti, mistletoe cactus evolved in shade
Use a hanging pot or elevated position to showcase the cascading stems
Maintain humidity between 40–60% by grouping with other plants or misting weekly

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Stems become mushy and blackened; the plant collapses despite appearing wet.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, remove all rotted roots, dust with cinnamon, air-dry 3–5 days, repot in orchid mix.
conditions.prevention: Water when top 1 cm is dry; never leave sitting in water. Use a pot with excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold
conditions.symptoms: Sudden yellowing and dropping of segments after exposure to temperatures below 10°C.
conditions.remedy: Move away from cold source; do not water for 1 week. New growth resumes in warmth.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 10°C; never expose to cold windows, drafts, or AC vents in winter.

conditions.name: Sparse thin growth
conditions.symptoms: Stems become thin, weak, and widely spaced; the plant loses its dense cascading appearance.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; the plant should produce thicker stems within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; rotate the pot monthly to encourage even growth.

conditions.name: Pale discolouration from shade
conditions.symptoms: Stems turn pale yellow-green; the plant loses vibrancy and visual appeal.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window; colour should return within 3–4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light; avoid deep shade or low-light corners.

conditions.name: Brittle stem breakage
conditions.symptoms: Stems become brittle and snap easily; the plant fragments with minimal handling.
conditions.remedy: Increase watering frequency slightly; water when top 1 cm is dry, not when bone-dry.
conditions.prevention: Keep the plant slightly more moist than desert cacti; mistletoe cactus prefers humid conditions.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.

facts: Rhipsalis baccifera is the only cactus genus native to the Old World (Africa and Sri Lanka).
Unlike desert cacti, mistletoe cactus evolved in tropical rainforests and grows on trees as an epiphyte.
The plant is completely spineless and harmless, making it safe for homes with children and pets.
It produces small white flowers followed by translucent white berries that resemble mistletoe.
Mistletoe cactus needs considerably more water than desert cacti because it evolved in humid rainforests.
The trailing stems can reach 1–2 metres long in a hanging pot over several years.
Propagation is easy: cut 5–10 cm stem segments, let air-dry 1 day, then insert into orchid mix.
The plant is non-toxic and completely safe around cats, dogs, and children.
Mistletoe cactus produces berries that were historically used to make bird attractants and sticky traps.
In tropical regions where it has naturalized, the plant is considered mildly invasive on native trees.
The scientific name 'baccifera' refers to the berry-bearing habit, unusual among cacti.
This species is sometimes confused with Selenicereus species, but lacks that genus's nocturnal blooming.
Unlike most cacti, mistletoe cactus does not enter dormancy and can flower year-round indoors.
The plant thrives in humidity and benefits from misting or grouping with other plants.
In its native rainforest habitat, it hangs from tree branches, creating natural green curtains.

care.placement_hints: Flowers around Easter (April-May) with distinct blooms. Needs cool nights to trigger flowering.

common_conditions: failure to bloom from lack of cool dormancy
lore: Easter cactus is a beloved spring bloomer in temperate gardens. Unlike its Christmas cactus cousin, it flowers in spring and requires different care — cooler nights in winter are essential to trigger the spectacular cascade of bright red, pink, or orange flowers. Gardeners eagerly await the Easter display each year.
etymology: Hatiora honors Thomas Harriot, an English mathematician; gaertneri honors the Gaertner family of botanists.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun ideal; afternoon shade prevents scorching
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Plant becomes pale, growth slows, buds fail to form, stems stretch and weaken
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf tips scorch or bleach; very intense afternoon sun stresses the plant

placement_tips: Place in a bright window with cool nights (10–15°C from October-December) to trigger flowering
Move away from heating vents in winter — artificial warmth prevents the cold dormancy needed for buds
Water consistently during growing season, but reduce water slightly in winter to encourage blooming
Expect vibrant red, pink, or orange flowers in April-May if dormancy conditions are met

conditions.name: Bud drop from inconsistent watering
conditions.symptoms: Flower buds form, then shrivel and drop before opening.
conditions.remedy: Maintain consistent moisture; water when top 1 cm is dry. Do not let the soil dry completely.
conditions.prevention: Water regularly during the growing season; bud drop is often caused by erratic watering patterns.

conditions.name: Failure to bloom
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows vigorously but produces no buds or flowers.
conditions.remedy: Provide cool nights (10–15°C) for 8 weeks from October-December; reduce watering slightly.
conditions.prevention: Easter cactus must experience a cool dormant period to set buds; warm winters prevent blooming.

conditions.name: Root rot from winter overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Stems become mushy; plant collapses despite appearing wet during dormancy.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, remove rotted roots, dust with cinnamon, air-dry 5–7 days, repot in fresh orchid mix.
conditions.prevention: Reduce watering significantly from November-January; the plant is dormant and needs minimal water.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold drafts
conditions.symptoms: Sudden yellowing and shedding of leaf segments after exposure to cold or drafts.
conditions.remedy: Move away from drafts and cold windows; do not water for 1 week. Growth resumes in warmth.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 10°C; avoid cold windowsills and exterior doors, especially in winter.

conditions.name: Sparse pale growth
conditions.symptoms: Stems become thin and weak; plant loses its lush appearance; growth becomes limited.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter indirect light; provide 4+ hours of daily illumination.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light year-round; pale growth indicates insufficient illumination.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Easter cactus flowers in April-May, distinct from Christmas cactus which blooms in December.
The flowering is triggered by cool nights (10–15°C) and reduced daylight in autumn — warmth prevents blooming.
Flowers are bright red, pink, or orange and last 1–2 weeks if conditions remain cool.
Unlike Christmas cactus (Schlumbergera), Easter cactus has a different leaf-segment shape — segments are more rounded.
The plant is native to the Atlantic forest of Brazil, a tropical rainforest habitat.
Easter cactus is completely non-toxic and safe around pets and children.
It grows as an epiphyte on tree branches in its native habitat, explaining its preference for moisture.
The plant produces hundreds of flowers if dormancy conditions are perfect — a mature plant may display 200+ blooms.
Propagation is easy: detach leaf segments, let air-dry 1 day, then insert into orchid mix.
Unlike desert cacti, Easter cactus needs consistently moist (not wet) soil during the growing season.
Repotting should be done after flowering, when new growth begins, not during dormancy.
The plant can live for 20+ years in the same pot with minimal repotting.
Buds will drop if the plant is moved during bud formation — keep it in one location from January-April.
A cool windowsill is ideal — the plant uses the temperature drop as a 'signal' to prepare buds.
Feeding is not necessary if the plant is repotted every 2–3 years with fresh orchid mix.

care.placement_hints: Critically endangered in wild due to habitat flooding in 1990s. Slow-growing sphere.

origin: Central Mexico (critically endangered in the wild)
common_conditions: slow growth and etiolation in low light
spider mites under the golden spines
crown rot from water pooling on top
lore: Golden barrel cactus is one of the world's most critically endangered plants. In 1994, a dam flooded 95% of its native habitat in Mexico. Today, wild populations are nearly extinct, but horticulturists have preserved the species through cultivation. Each living specimen represents a genetic victory against extinction.
etymology: Echinocactus means 'hedgehog cactus'; grusonii honours Hermann Gruson, a German cactus collector who pioneered its cultivation.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun daily; benefits from intense light and heat
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Body stretches or becomes distorted, spines weaken, plant loses golden colour and becomes pale
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sunburn appears as brown patches after sudden exposure to intense light; move to bright indirect light

placement_tips: Place in a south or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun daily
Acclimate gradually if moving from indoor shade to direct sun to prevent sunburn
Never allow water to pool on the crown — it causes rot; always water at the soil level
This species grows extremely slowly — expect only 1–2 centimetres of growth per year

conditions.name: Winter rot from watering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy crown or base; the plant may collapse despite being healthy-looking.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away all soft tissue, dust with cinnamon, air-dry 10+ days, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Stop watering completely from November-March; the plant is dormant and needs zero water.

conditions.name: Crown rot from water pooling
conditions.symptoms: Soft wet spot develops at the crown or top centre; may spread downward.
conditions.remedy: Tip the pot immediately to drain water; cut away affected tissue; dust with cinnamon; air-dry.
conditions.prevention: NEVER water from the top; always water at soil level only; avoid overhead watering.

conditions.name: Etiolation and colour loss
conditions.symptoms: Body becomes pale or grey instead of golden; growth stretches or becomes distorted.
conditions.remedy: Move to intense direct sunlight; the plant should regain golden colour within 4–8 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide 6+ hours of direct sun daily; maintain intense light year-round.

conditions.name: Sunburn from sudden light exposure
conditions.symptoms: Brown or orange patches appear on the body after moving to direct sun.
conditions.remedy: Move back to bright indirect light for 2–3 weeks, then gradually reintroduce direct sun.
conditions.prevention: Acclimate slowly over 3–4 weeks when moving from shade to direct light.

conditions.name: Spider mites under spines
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing among the spines; stippled discolouration on the body; growth slows.
conditions.remedy: Spray with neem oil or insecticidal soap; the dense spines make treatment difficult — apply thoroughly.
conditions.prevention: Inspect monthly; maintain low humidity (30–40%) to discourage spider mite colonization.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Golden barrel cactus is one of the world's most critically endangered plants.
In 1994, a dam flooded 95% of its native habitat in Veracruz, Mexico, nearly causing extinction.
Today, most wild populations exist only in botanical gardens and private collections.
The species was saved from extinction by horticulturists who propagated specimens worldwide.
Each golden barrel cactus is genetically valuable — horticulturists carefully track lineages.
The plant grows extremely slowly, gaining only 1–2 centimetres per year even with optimal care.
Mature specimens in botanical gardens are 50–100+ years old and still only 1–1.5 metres tall.
The golden-yellow spines give the plant its common name and reflect intense desert sunlight.
It produces small yellow flowers at the crown in spring if mature and well-established.
Golden barrel cactus is completely non-toxic and safe around pets and children.
The plant requires almost no water from November-March — most growers water zero times during this period.
Overwatering is the number-one killer; this cactus evolved in one of Mexico's driest regions.
Terracotta pots are essential because they allow the soil to dry quickly.
A 20-year-old specimen may be only 20–30 centimetres tall, demonstrating its ultra-slow growth.
Propagation from seed takes 10+ years to produce a specimen worth viewing.

care.placement_hints: Thrives in bright indirect light; needs a warm location. Root-bound plants flower earlier.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets (seeds and flowers contain toxins).

common_conditions: Leaf splitting and yellowing from low humidity
lore: Named for Queen Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, wife of George III. Charlotte, North Carolina is also named in her honour. The exotic flower resembles a bird in flight — the orange sepals are the body and head, while the blue petals form the wings.
etymology: Genus honours the Queen; 'reginae' means 'of the queen.' The flower's shape inspired the common name 'crane flower' in some regions.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun; afternoon shade in hot climates
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leaves smaller, stems elongate, no flowers, pale green foliage
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf tips scorch in intense afternoon sun; flowers fade faster

placement_tips: Position 1–2m from a south-facing window for optimal light and flowering.
Keep the plant slightly root-bound to encourage blooming (repot every 2–3 years only).
Maintain 50–70% humidity around the foliage; mist weekly or group with humidifiers.
Protect flowers from cold drafts — they last 2–3 weeks if undisturbed.

conditions.name: No flowers after 4 years
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows vigorously but never produces the iconic orange and blue blooms.
conditions.remedy: Ensure bright light (8+ hours daily). Wait for root-bound state (do not repot for 1–2 years). Feed with high-phosphorus fertilizer monthly. Flower induction takes 5–7 years in indoor conditions.
conditions.prevention: Start with a mature propagation (2–3 years old at purchase). Avoid overwatering and overfeeding nitrogen.

conditions.name: Leaf splitting and tattered margins
conditions.symptoms: Leaf edges split, turn brown, or appear tattered; new leaves emerge smaller.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity by grouping plants, using a pebble tray, or daily misting. Trim damaged leaf tips with clean scissors.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%. Avoid letting the plant dry out completely in summer.

conditions.name: Mealybug colonies in leaf bases
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters at leaf petioles; sticky residue on lower leaves.
conditions.remedy: Spray with 70% isopropyl alcohol on a cotton swab; follow up every 7 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Inspect new plants before bringing home. Quarantine for 2 weeks.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Mushy petioles, yellowing leaves, foul smell, plant wilts despite wet soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, remove all rotted roots with clean secateurs, dust with cinnamon. Repot in gritty mix with excellent drainage.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 3 cm of soil dry between waterings. Use pots with large drainage holes.

conditions.name: Leaf yellowing from low light
conditions.symptoms: Entire plant turns pale yellow-green; new leaves are small and pale.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window. Prune yellow leaves to redirect energy to new growth.
conditions.prevention: Provide at least bright indirect light (lux > 3000). Rotate quarterly toward the light source.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Strelitzia flowers take 4–7 years to appear indoors, but once established, a mature plant blooms for 2–3 weeks per flower.
The flower structure is precisely engineered: a perch forms when a bird lands, triggering pollen sacs to dust its body.
Root-bound plants flower more readily — resist the urge to repot every year.
The plant's thick orange sepals and blue petals evolved to attract sunbirds in South African coastal scrub.
A single mature plant can produce 5–10 blooms per season over many years.
Strelitzia grows from a rhizome and spreads slowly via underground shoots.
The plant tolerates salt spray and sandy soils — its native habitat is exposed coastal cliffs.
Humidity above 60% accelerates leaf unfurling and reduces edge-browning.
New leaves take 6–8 weeks to fully expand after emergence.
The name 'crane flower' reflects the beak-like orange sepals and curved blue petals.
Seeds germinate in 30–60 days when bottom-heated to 24–27°C.
A mature plant stands 1–1.5 m tall; pot size influences final height.
Strelitzia flowers remain vivid for 2–3 weeks indoors if humidity is maintained.
The plant is evergreen and requires no dormancy period — feed year-round with diluted fertilizer.
Charlotte, North Carolina (founded 1768) honours Queen Charlotte, the same woman who inspired this plant's genus name.

care.placement_hints: Needs full bright direct light; drought-tolerant; water sparingly. Swollen base stores water.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets (saponins in foliage).

common_conditions: Leaf tip browning from low humidity or water salts
Trunk rot if soil stays wet
lore: The 'elephantipes' epithet refers to the plant's swollen caudex base, which resembles an elephant's foot. In its native range (Mexico and Guatemala), this species grows as a small understory tree in dry tropical deciduous forests, thriving in intense heat and occasional drought.
etymology: 'Yucca' comes from the Taíno (Caribbean indigenous) word for cassava; 'elephantipes' means 'elephant foot,' describing the thick basal trunk.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours daily; tolerates full afternoon sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leaves pale and spindly; lower leaves yellow; stem etiolates (stretches)
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — this plant is sun-loving and tolerates intense rays without scorching

placement_tips: Place in a south or west-facing window for maximum direct sun exposure.
Use a heavy, wide pot to counterbalance the tall canopy and prevent tipping.
Allow the pot to dry out completely between waterings — the thick base stores water reserves.
Trim brown leaf tips with clean scissors; remove dead lower leaves to promote air circulation.

conditions.name: Overwatering and root rot
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy stem at the base, yellowing leaves, soil stays wet >10 days, foul smell.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering immediately. Unpot and inspect the caudex (swollen base). Cut away blackened tissue. Repot in very gritty, fast-draining cactus mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when the soil is bone-dry. Err on the side of underwatering — yuccas store water in their trunk.

conditions.name: Leaf tip browning
conditions.symptoms: Brown or tan tips on leaves; sometimes affecting up to 25% of the leaf margin.
conditions.remedy: Trim affected tips with clean scissors at a 45-degree angle. Increase humidity by misting or grouping plants. Consider using filtered or rainwater.
conditions.prevention: Yuccas are sensitive to salt and chlorine. Use distilled water if tap water is mineral-heavy.

conditions.name: Spider mites in dry air
conditions.symptoms: Fine pale webbing on stems and undersides of leaves; stippled yellow appearance.
conditions.remedy: Rinse the plant thoroughly under a shower. Wipe leaves with neem oil weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Regular misting to 30–40% humidity deters mites. Inspect new plants before bringing home.

conditions.name: Trunk rot from poor drainage
conditions.symptoms: Soft blackened areas at the base of the stem; may smell like compost or decay.
conditions.remedy: Immediately cut back all affected tissue with a clean knife. Let the cut dry for 3 days. Repot in bone-dry cactus soil.
conditions.prevention: Never use regular potting mix — always use fast-draining cactus or succulent soil.

conditions.name: Slow growth in low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge pale and small; stem becomes thin and weak; no new growth for months.
conditions.remedy: Move to a sunnier location. Prune the top 10–15 cm to encourage branching from lower nodes.
conditions.prevention: Provide at least 6 hours of direct sun daily. South or west windows are ideal.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.
