facts: Spearmint is the mildest and sweetest of the Mentha species — commonly used in teas, cocktails, and Middle Eastern dishes; peppermint is a hybrid with a sharper bite.
Mint's invasive nature is legendary: a single runner can spread 1 metre in a season if unchecked, making it a banned plant in some gardens.
The flavour compound (carvone) in spearmint is distinct from peppermint's menthol; peppermint feels cooling while spearmint tastes sweet and fruity.
Mint has been used medicinally for 4,000+ years to treat digestive discomfort, headaches, and respiratory issues — modern science confirms antimicrobial properties.
A mature mint plant can yield 500+ grams of fresh leaves per season if harvested frequently and properly.
Mint prefers consistent moisture (like basil) but tolerates partial shade better than most herbs — making it ideal for east-facing windows or filtered light.
Dried spearmint loses much of its delicate sweetness within 3 months — fresh mint is far superior in flavour and aroma.
Mint roots are so vigorous they can crack terracotta pots from the inside out over 6–12 months; plastic pots are more durable for long-term growth.
The plant's aromatic oils repel insects naturally; traditional gardeners planted mint around crops to deter aphids and mosquitoes.
Mint flowers are small and pale purple or white, arranged in spikes; they attract bees and butterflies but signal the end of peak leaf production.
In the kitchen, mint leaves should be torn by hand rather than cut with a blade — cutting oxidises the edges and dulls the flavour within minutes.
Mint can be frozen in ice cubes with water for cocktails, or dried by hanging stems upside down in a dark, breezy location for 1–2 weeks.
The plant's essential oils have a mild numbing effect on the tongue due to menthol's interaction with cold-sensing nerve fibres.
Historically, mint was rubbed on tables at Roman banquets to stimulate appetite and aid digestion — a tradition that persists in modern Mediterranean cuisine.
Mint cuttings root so readily in water that a single glass of water with 5 stems will produce a rooted propagule in 10 days.

care.placement_hints: Drought-tolerant woody shrub; prefers dry, gritty soil and minimal fussing. Hates wet feet and cold damp winters.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic to pets; mild essential oils are safe.

common_conditions: woody stem dieback from cold wet feet
root rot in winter dormancy if overwatered
lore: Rosemary's Latin name 'Rosmarinus officinalis' literally means 'sea dew' (ros = dew, marinus = sea); ancient Mediterranean sailors planted it on cliffsides. A symbol of remembrance, fidelity, and remembrance of the dead — rosemary sprigs were traditionally carried at funerals.
etymology: 'Salvia' is the Latin for 'to save' or 'healing' — rosemary was reclassified into genus Salvia in 2017, splitting from Rosmarinus. 'Officinalis' means 'medicinal' or 'of the apothecary'.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6–8 hours minimum; essential oil concentration increases with full sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Lanky weak growth, pale needles, poor flavour, susceptibility to mildew
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — rosemary thrives in full Mediterranean sun; only extreme heat (>35°C) with drought stress causes needle scorch

placement_tips: Place on a south or west-facing windowsill with 6+ hours of direct sunlight daily; windowsill-grown plants produce more robust flavour
Allow the soil to dry completely between waterings — treat rosemary like a cactus, not a leafy herb; overwatering is the #1 killer
Trim woody stems in spring to shape the plant and remove any frost-blackened tips; rosemary responds well to hard pruning
In winter, move the pot away from cold windowsills (below 10°C) — rosemary resents cold wet drafts; a warm bright kitchen shelf is ideal

conditions.name: Woody stem dieback from cold wet feet
conditions.symptoms: Brown or black patches spread up stems; leaves drop; base of plant becomes hollow and rotten.
conditions.remedy: Cut away all blackened tissue back to living green wood; repot in completely dry, gritty cactus mix; move to a warm, airy spot.
conditions.prevention: Never overwater; allow soil to dry fully between waterings; move the pot away from cold windows in winter; use a pot with excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Needle browning from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Needles turn brown (starting at the base of stems) and drop; foliage becomes sparse over 2–3 weeks.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering immediately; trim brown stems; increase airflow with a small fan; leave the pot dry for 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Rosemary is a Mediterranean drought plant — water only when soil is bone-dry; check moisture with your finger; once every 10–14 days is typical.

conditions.name: Powdery mildew
conditions.symptoms: Fine white powder on leaf surfaces and stems; leaves curl and yellow; growth slows.
conditions.remedy: Isolate the plant; spray with a sulphur-based fungicide or 50:50 milk-water solution every 7 days; remove heavily infected stems.
conditions.prevention: Ensure excellent airflow; avoid high humidity (keep below 60%); water at soil level only; don't mist rosemary.

conditions.name: Winter root rot in dormancy
conditions.symptoms: Sudden wilting in January–February despite moist soil; mushy roots; foul smell from the pot.
conditions.remedy: Dry out the pot completely; cut away rotten roots; repot in fresh gritty mix; move to a warm, bright, airy location.
conditions.prevention: In winter, rosemary enters dormancy — reduce watering by 75%; allow soil to dry fully between rare waterings.

conditions.name: Leggy sparse growth indoors
conditions.symptoms: Plant becomes tall and thin with long gaps between leaf clusters; lower stem is bare wood.
conditions.remedy: Prune hard (cut back by 50%) in spring to force bushy branching; provide 6+ hours of direct sunlight.
conditions.prevention: Rosemary needs intense light to stay compact — windowsill or grow light is essential; rotate the pot weekly for even growth.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Rosemary was reclassified from genus Rosmarinus to Salvia in 2017 based on genetic analysis; most gardeners still use the old name Rosmarinus officinalis.
The plant is native to rocky Mediterranean cliffs where rainfall is minimal — rosemary's narrow waxy needles are adaptations to drought, not weakness.
Rosemary's essential oils (camphor, 1,8-cineol, α-pinene) have been shown in studies to enhance memory and concentration — hence the medieval tradition of wearing sprigs while studying.
A single mature rosemary plant in ideal conditions lives 15–20 years, making it far longer-lived than basil or mint; it's a perennial woody shrub, not a tender annual.
Rosemary needles harvested in late morning (after dew dries) are more aromatic than morning-harvested foliage due to essential oil concentration by mid-day.
Ancient Romans burned rosemary as incense and placed sprigs in tombs as a symbol of remembrance — the tradition persists in some Mediterranean funeral customs.
Greek students were rosemary sprigs to exams; medieval knights embroidered rosemary sprigs on their garments as a symbol of courage and fidelity.
The plant tolerates poor, sandy soil and rocky terrain; rich organic soil often causes leggy weak growth and increased disease susceptibility.
Rosemary flowers are small and pale blue, purple, pink, or white depending on variety; they attract bees and butterflies throughout the growing season.
Indoor rosemary grows slowly compared to outdoor plants — expect 2–3 cm of growth per month in bright light, half that in medium light.
The plant responds extremely well to hard pruning in spring; cutting back by 50% will trigger dense bushy regrowth and prevent woody bare-stemmed plants.
Rosemary pairs chemically with lamb (both have thymol and camphor notes) — a culinary tradition backed by modern flavour chemistry.
Dried rosemary retains its potency for 12+ months (unlike basil or mint) — freezing in oil is the best preservation method for full flavour.
A 5-year-old rosemary plant can yield 500+ grams of fresh needles per year with proper pruning and care.
Rosemary oil (hydrosol) leftover from distillation makes an excellent rinse for hair health and is used in traditional Mediterranean beauty routines.

care.placement_hints: Extremely drought-tolerant like rosemary; prefers dry gritty soil. Mediterranean origin makes it a 'set and forget' herb for sunny windowsills.

common_conditions: woody dieback from winter cold and wet
root rot in consistently moist soil
lore: In medieval Europe, thyme symbolised courage; knights embroidered thyme sprigs on their clothing and gifts of thyme sprigs were tokens of respect. The ancient Greeks burned thyme as incense in temples, believing it inspired bravery in battle.
etymology: 'Thymus' comes from the Greek 'thymon' meaning 'to fumigate' — ancient peoples burned thyme for its aromatic smoke. Medieval herbalists linked the plant's courage symbolism to the word 'thumos' (Greek for spirit or passion).

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours required; full Mediterranean sun optimal for essential oil development
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Lanky weak growth, pale foliage, poor flavour, susceptibility to mildew
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — thyme loves intense sun; only extreme heat (>38°C) with severe drought causes leaf scorch

placement_tips: Place on a hot south or west-facing windowsill with 6+ hours of direct sunlight; thyme is one of the most light-hungry culinary herbs
Water sparingly — thyme hates wet feet; allow soil to dry completely between waterings (every 2–3 weeks is typical)
Pinch the top growth in spring and early summer to encourage bushy branching; do not let the plant become tall and sparse
Trim back by 30–50% in late spring to rejuvenate old woody plants and prevent them from becoming bare at the base

conditions.name: Woody dieback from cold wet roots
conditions.symptoms: Stems blacken from the base upward; leaves drop; foliage becomes sparse and weak despite appearing alive.
conditions.remedy: Prune away all blackened stems back to living tissue; repot in a very gritty, fast-draining mix; reduce watering to minimal levels.
conditions.prevention: Never water in winter; thyme is dormant and requires almost no moisture from November to March. Always use a pot with excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Sparse foliage from low light
conditions.symptoms: Plant produces long thin stems with gaps between leaf clusters; lower stem is bare; growth slows dramatically.
conditions.remedy: Move to a much brighter location (south-facing windowsill or grow light); prune hard in spring to force bushy regrowth.
conditions.prevention: Thyme needs 6+ hours of direct sunlight — without it, the plant will never thrive indoors. Grow lights are essential for winter light.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Sudden wilting despite wet soil; roots smell foul and are black/mushy; plant dies within days.
conditions.remedy: Immediately stop all watering; repot in completely dry gritty mix; place in a warm, breezy location.
conditions.prevention: Thyme is a drought plant — water only when soil is bone-dry. When in doubt, don't water. Check soil with your finger before watering.

conditions.name: Powdery mildew
conditions.symptoms: Fine white powdery coating on leaf surfaces; leaves curl; foliage becomes yellow and drops.
conditions.remedy: Isolate the plant; spray with sulphur dust or 50:50 milk-water solution every 7 days; improve airflow.
conditions.prevention: Thyme hates humidity — keep below 50%; ensure excellent airflow; water only at the soil, never on foliage.

conditions.name: Slow winter dormancy
conditions.symptoms: Little to no growth from November to February; plant appears brown and lifeless.
conditions.remedy: This is normal dormancy — don't water, don't worry. Growth resumes in spring. Trim dead-looking stems in April.
conditions.prevention: Thyme naturally rests in winter. Provide as much light as possible; keep it above 5°C; don't attempt to feed or stimulate growth.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Thymus vulgaris is the culinary thyme used in French bouquet garni (herb bundle) and Italian seasoning blends — it's the standard thyme in commercial cooking.
Thyme essential oil (thymol) has demonstrated antimicrobial and antioxidant properties; medieval herbalists used it to treat coughs and respiratory infections.
Medieval knights wore thyme sprigs as tokens of courage before battle; the symbolism likely stems from the plant's robust survival in harsh Mediterranean terrain.
A mature thyme plant produces hundreds of tiny pale pink, purple, or white flowers in late spring and early summer — highly attractive to bees.
Thyme is one of the most drought-tolerant culinary herbs; a well-established plant can survive 3–4 weeks without water in summer heat.
The plant's essential oils intensify with sunlight — windowsill-grown thyme has stronger flavour than shade-grown plants due to increased oil concentration.
Thyme leaves are tiny (2–4 mm) and densely packed on wiry stems — harvesting is labour-intensive compared to larger-leafed herbs like basil.
Fresh thyme sprigs can be frozen in ice cubes with water or oil for long-term storage; frozen thyme retains flavour better than dried for 6+ months.
A 3-year-old thyme plant can yield 200–300 grams of fresh leaves per season with proper management and hard spring pruning.
Thyme is a low-growing (15–30 cm) perennial that can live 10+ years in ideal conditions — far more durable than tender annuals like basil.
The plant's creeping varieties (Thymus serpyllum, creeping thyme) spread horizontally; upright types like T. vulgaris grow in a compact mound.
In the kitchen, thyme pairs with olive oil, garlic, rosemary, and oregano — classic Mediterranean flavour combinations share similar essential oil profiles.
Thyme is one of the few herbs that improves in flavour when dried; dried thyme concentrates the essential oils and develops deeper, more complex notes.
The plant responds extremely well to hard pruning — cutting back by 50% in spring forces dense new growth and prevents woody bare-stemmed plants.
Thyme thrives in poor, well-drained soil; rich organic soil promotes weak growth and disease; gritty cactus mix is ideal for indoor cultivation.

care.placement_hints: Biennial herb that flowers and dies in year 2; tolerates partial shade better than most culinary herbs. Prefers consistent moisture without waterlogging.

common_conditions: root rot in winter dormancy if overwatered
early bolting to flower in year 2
lore: Parsley was sacred to ancient Greeks, who wove it into victory crowns; it symbolised death and rebirth in classical mythology. Medieval herbalists believed parsley root could cure baldness, and pregnant women were cautioned against eating it (a myth with no basis).
etymology: 'Petroselinum' comes from Greek 'petros' (rock) and 'selinon' (celery) — 'rock celery', referencing its rocky Mediterranean habitat. 'Crispum' means 'curled'.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours acceptable; prefers dappled light rather than intense afternoon sun
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale foliage, weak stems, loss of curled texture on leaves
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf edges brown in intense afternoon sun; plant bolts earlier with excessive heat

placement_tips: Place 1–2 metres from a south or west-facing window; parsley tolerates partial shade better than most herbs and actually prefers dappled light in summer
Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged — parsley hates both extremes; check soil daily and water when the top 1 cm feels dry
Pinch off any flower buds as soon as they appear to extend leaf production; once the plant bolts to flower, leaf production stops and quality declines rapidly
Harvest outer stems first, working inward; a mature parsley plant can be harvested every 3–4 days if managed carefully

conditions.name: Split stems from inconsistent watering
conditions.symptoms: Stems and leaf ribs crack and split; leaves yellow and drop; plant wilts between waterings.
conditions.remedy: Trim split stems; increase watering frequency to keep soil consistently (but not soggily) moist; use a self-watering pot if you travel.
conditions.prevention: Parsley hates drought followed by wet soil — maintain consistent moisture. Set a daily watering reminder; check soil with your finger each morning.

conditions.name: Leaf yellowing from nitrogen hunger
conditions.symptoms: Older outer leaves turn yellow first; new leaves are pale; growth slows despite adequate light.
conditions.remedy: Feed with a balanced liquid fertiliser (10-10-10 or similar) every 2 weeks; or compost a thin layer of compost to refresh nutrients.
conditions.prevention: Parsley is a heavy feeder — fertilise every 2–3 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 1–2 cm of soil every spring.

conditions.name: Root rot in winter dormancy if overwatered
conditions.symptoms: Plant suddenly wilts in late autumn/winter despite wet soil; roots smell foul; foliage blackens.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering immediately; repot in fresh mix; trim away any blackened roots; place in a cool, bright, airy location.
conditions.prevention: In winter (Nov–Feb), parsley slows growth — reduce watering by 50%. Don't let it sit in wet soil for more than 2 days.

conditions.name: Early bolting to flower
conditions.symptoms: Tall flower stalks emerge from the centre; leaf production stops; leaves become bitter and thin.
conditions.remedy: Pinch off flower buds immediately; trim the central stalk back to the leaf crown. Leaf production may resume for 2–3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Parsley is a biennial — it naturally flowers in year 2. Pinch buds daily to extend year 1 leaf production. Plan to compost and replant in year 2.

conditions.name: Biennial lifecycle — expected decline in year 2
conditions.symptoms: Plant flowers prolifically in spring of year 2; leaf production stops; foliage becomes tough and bitter.
conditions.remedy: Accept the plant's lifecycle — it's normal. Compost it and start fresh from seed or a new plant.
conditions.prevention: Treat parsley as a 1-year annual indoors. Sow seed every spring for continuous fresh plants. Year 2 plants are typically composted.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats

facts: Petroselinum crispum is a biennial — it produces only leaves (rosette) in year 1, then flowers and seeds in year 2 before dying naturally.
Parsley seed germinates slowly (2–3 weeks) and unevenly; soaking seeds overnight before sowing speeds germination by 30–50%.
The curly varieties are ornamental as well as edible; flat-leaf (Italian) parsley has more flavour but less visual appeal on the plate.
Parsley is exceptionally nutritious — high in vitamins C, K, and A; a single large sprig provides 20% of daily vitamin K.
Fresh parsley leaves harvested in the morning (after dew dries) have maximum flavour and aromatic compounds.
Parsley can be frozen whole in small bags, dried by hanging stems upside-down, or frozen in water/oil cubes for 6+ months of use.
In the kitchen, parsley is a workhorse herb used to garnish, brighten sauces, or form the base of chimichurri and salsa verde.
Ancient Greeks used parsley wreaths to crown victors in athletic competitions and as an aromatic ceremonial herb.
A mature parsley plant in year 1 can yield 50–80 grams of fresh leaves per week with regular harvesting.
Parsley roots are edible and aromatic — Hamburg parsley is a variety bred for its thick, carrot-like roots used in soups.
The plant tolerates partial shade (3–4 hours of sun) better than basil, mint, or rosemary — making it ideal for north-facing windowsills.
Parsley has a natural affinity for tomato-based dishes and Mediterranean cuisine; paired with garlic, lemon, and olive oil.
The plant's biennial nature means indoor growers should accept a 1-year lifespan and plan to replant; forcing year-2 growth results in bitter leaves.
Parsley seeds are slow and temperamental — many growers buy young plants from nurseries rather than starting from seed.
The plant's curled foliage is a genetic mutation of wild parsley (Petroselinum crispum var. crispum); flat-leaf varieties are closer to the wild type.

care.placement_hints: Extremely cold-hardy perennial with delicate onion flavour. Tolerates partial shade and poor soil; produces pretty purple flowers in spring.
care.toxicity: TOXIC TO CATS AND DOGS — Allium family causes hemolytic anemia and organ damage. Avoid.

common_conditions: bulb rot in winter dormancy if overwatered
mite damage when air is too dry
lore: Chives are the mildest member of the Allium family (onions, garlic, leeks); they've been cultivated in Asia for over 5,000 years. Medieval European monks grew chives in monastery gardens for both culinary and medicinal use — the flowers were used in salads and as decorative garnishes.
etymology: 'Allium' is the Latin name for the garlic plant; the genus includes 900+ species. 'Schoenoprasum' comes from Greek 'schoinon' (rush, referring to the grass-like leaves) and 'prasum' (leek).

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4–6 hours; partial shade tolerated better than basil or rosemary
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Weak spindly growth, pale foliage, poor flavour, few flowers
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — chives love sun; only extreme heat (>35°C) with drought causes leaf tip browning

placement_tips: Place on a bright windowsill with at least 4 hours of direct sun; east-facing windows are ideal as they provide morning light without harsh afternoon heat
Keep soil consistently moist during the growing season (April–September); reduce watering in winter as the plant naturally slows
Pinch off flower buds early in the season if you want maximum leaf production; allow a few flowers to bloom for their delicate onion flavour and ornamental appeal
Harvest outer tubes first, working inward; a mature clump can be harvested every 3–5 days during peak growth

conditions.name: Wilting from underwatering during growth
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips brown and curl; plant droops despite firm soil; growth slows dramatically.
conditions.remedy: Water immediately and deeply; soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the bulbs.
conditions.prevention: During the April–September growing season, keep soil consistently moist. Check daily; chives' shallow bulbs dry out quickly.

conditions.name: Yellowing from nitrogen depletion
conditions.symptoms: Leaves turn pale yellow starting from the tips; growth slows; foliage becomes thin and weak.
conditions.remedy: Feed with a balanced liquid fertiliser (10-10-10) every 2 weeks; or layer 1 cm of compost on top of the soil.
conditions.prevention: Chives are moderate feeders — fertilise every 2–3 weeks during the growing season (April–September).

conditions.name: Bulb rot in winter dormancy if overwatered
conditions.symptoms: Plant suddenly collapses in November–February; base of plant is mushy; foul smell from soil.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering immediately; repot in fresh, well-draining mix; trim away any blackened or mushy bulbs.
conditions.prevention: In winter, chives go dormant — reduce watering by 75%. The soil should be barely moist, almost dry.

conditions.name: Mite damage and stippling in dry air
conditions.symptoms: Tiny yellow stipples appear on leaf surfaces; fine webbing under the tubes; leaves lose sheen.
conditions.remedy: Mist the foliage daily with water; spray with neem oil every 5 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep indoor humidity above 50% — chives prefer moderate humidity. Mist weekly even when no mites are visible.

conditions.name: Overcrowding and congestion
conditions.symptoms: Bulbs push up out of the soil; growth slows; leaves compete for light and become weak.
conditions.remedy: Divide the clump in early spring — gently pry apart clusters of bulbs and repot into separate containers or larger pot with fresh soil.
conditions.prevention: Divide chives every 2–3 years in spring to rejuvenate the clump and maintain vigour.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Chives (Allium schoenoprasum) are the mildest onion-family herb; their flavour is delicate, grassy, and slightly sweet — nothing like the bite of raw garlic or onions.
WARNING: Chives are TOXIC to cats and dogs due to compounds in the Allium genus (thiosulfate) that damage red blood cells — cause hemolytic anemia. Keep away from pets.
Chives are a perennial herb — they die back to the bulbs each winter and regrow reliably every spring for 5+ years with minimal care.
The delicate purple or white flowers (edible and mildly onion-flavoured) bloom in spring and early summer — highly ornamental in both kitchen and garden.
Fresh chives are best used within 2–3 hours of cutting as they begin to lose volatile compounds; dried chives lose 70% of flavour and are poor substitutes.
Chives can be frozen chopped in water/oil cubes for 3–4 months of storage; frozen chives work better in cooked dishes than fresh.
A mature chive clump can yield 30–50 grams of fresh leaves per week during peak growth (April–September).
In medieval European monasteries, chives were called 'rush leeks' and used as a garnish, condiment, and mild medicine for digestive issues.
Chives are one of the easiest herbs to grow indoors — they tolerate partial shade, poor soil, and neglect better than basil or mint.
The plant prefers cool to moderate temperatures (12–20°C) and actually appreciates winter dormancy; forcing growth year-round weakens the bulbs.
Chive bulbs can be divided in early spring or autumn; gently pry apart clusters and repot — each small bulb will root and become a new plant.
Chives prefer slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6.0–7.0); they dislike very alkaline soils and may yellowing in pH >8.0.
The flavour is concentrated in the base of each tube (near the bulb); the pale inner leaves are milder than the dark outer leaves.
Chives pair chemically with potatoes, eggs, cream sauces, and cheese; classical French cooking uses them heavily in fines herbes blends.
Unlike most herbs, chives can be harvested year-round with daily care — even a small plant provides continuous garnish if leaves are pinched gently rather than torn.

care.placement_hints: Heat-loving tropical plant; needs 8+ hours of direct sunlight and warm air (20–28°C). Requires hand-pollination or air circulation for fruit set indoors.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic but capsaicin oil (hot compound) is irritant to pets and eyes; avoid contact.

common_conditions: blossom-end rot from calcium deficiency or inconsistent watering
flower drop from low light or temperature stress
spider mites and thrips in dry air
lore: Chili peppers were unknown to Europe, Asia, and Africa until Columbus arrived in the Americas in 1493; within a century, chili cultivation had spread globally. In Mexico, chilis are sacred — the Aztecs used them in ceremonies and as currency. Today, the world grows 4+ million tonnes of chilis annually.
etymology: 'Capsicum' comes from the Greek 'kapto' meaning 'to bite'; the genus name reflects the pungent bite of hot varieties. 'Annuum' means 'annual' — though the plant is technically a short-lived perennial.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 8+ hours required for flowering and fruit production; less light produces leaves only
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Leggy weak growth, no flowers, no fruit, poor colour in ripening peppers
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare in indoor settings; only extreme heat (>35°C) with dry soil causes leaf scorch

placement_tips: Place on a south or southwest-facing windowsill with 8+ hours of direct sunlight; grow lights (LED 12–14 hours daily) are essential for indoor fruit set
Pollinate flowers by hand using a small brush — gently brush inside each flower to transfer pollen between flowers, or flick stems daily to shake pollen
Keep air temperature between 20–28°C; chilis stall below 16°C and drop flowers in cold conditions. Night temperatures below 15°C cause blossom end rot.
Water deeply when the top 1 cm of soil feels dry; provide consistent moisture during flowering and fruiting to prevent calcium deficiency (blossom-end rot)

conditions.name: Blossom-end rot (calcium deficiency)
conditions.symptoms: Dark sunken lesions appear on the bottom (blossom end) of developing fruits; fruits are inedible.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected fruits; water deeply and consistently to improve calcium uptake; add a calcium supplement (crushed eggshells, liquid calcium) to the soil.
conditions.prevention: Maintain consistent soil moisture; never let the plant dry out completely during flowering/fruiting. Feed with a balanced NPK + calcium supplement.

conditions.name: Flower drop from temperature stress or low light
conditions.symptoms: Flowers develop but fall off before setting fruit; no peppers form.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter, warmer location; ensure 8+ hours of direct sun and night temperatures above 15°C.
conditions.prevention: Chilis need heat and light to set fruit — windowsill growing often fails. Use grow lights; maintain warm air temperature (20–28°C).

conditions.name: Spider mites and thrips
conditions.symptoms: Tiny stipples on leaves; fine webbing under foliage; small insects visible; leaves curl and lose colour.
conditions.remedy: Mist foliage daily with water; spray with neem oil every 5 days for 3 weeks. Increase humidity to 60%+.
conditions.prevention: Spider mites thrive in dry air — mist regularly and maintain humidity above 50%. Inspect new plants before bringing indoors.

conditions.name: Stunted growth from cold roots
conditions.symptoms: Plant stops growing despite bright light; leaves pale and weak; roots are slow to develop.
conditions.remedy: Move the pot to a warm location (above a heating vent or on a warm shelf); use a heat mat under the pot to maintain soil temperature at 20°C+.
conditions.prevention: Chilis are tropical — keep soil warm (20–25°C minimum). Place the pot away from cold windows and exterior walls.

conditions.name: Poor pollination — no fruit set despite flowers
conditions.symptoms: Abundant flowers bloom but fall off without forming fruit; plant looks healthy but barren.
conditions.remedy: Hand-pollinate every other day — use a small brush to transfer pollen inside each flower, or flick stems vigorously to shake pollen.
conditions.prevention: Indoor chilis lack bees — hand-pollination is essential. Alternatively, place a small oscillating fan next to the plant to simulate wind pollination.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Capsicum annuum includes all sweet bell peppers and many chili varieties — the difference is genetics, not species (some are hot, some mild).
Chilis were unknown to Europe, Asia, and Africa until Columbus's 1493 voyage to the Caribbean — today they're eaten on every continent except Antarctica.
The heat in chilis comes from capsaicin, an alkaloid that triggers pain receptors in the mouth; birds are immune to capsaicin and disperse seeds; mammals feel the burn.
Chilis pair with tomatoes due to shared volatile compounds (mainly terpenes) — a modern flavour pairing with deep evolutionary roots in Central American cuisine.
A single chili plant can produce 50+ fruits per season in ideal conditions (full sun, 8+ hours daily, 20–28°C, hand-pollination).
Dried chilis are far more potent than fresh; drying concentrates capsaicin and other flavour compounds. A dried chili is 3–4 times hotter than the fresh equivalent.
Chili peppers turn colour as they ripen — green indicates immature fruit, yellow/orange/red indicates progressive ripeness and heat intensity.
The Scoville scale measures chili heat; bell peppers rank 0 Scoville units; jalapeños 2,500–8,000; habaneros 100,000–350,000; Carolina Reaper 2.2 million.
Capsaicin is poorly soluble in water but dissolves in fat — milk, oil, or fat-based dishes neutralise chili heat; water makes it worse.
Indoor chili plants require hand-pollination or air movement — bees don't live in your kitchen. A small oscillating fan or gentle brush-painting of flowers works.
Chilis are short-lived perennials (live 2–3 years) but are typically grown as annuals indoors due to declining productivity and size.
The plant is native to Mexico and Central America, where it grows in warm, sunny tropical climates — replicating this indoors requires grow lights and heat mats.
Chili peppers are rich in vitamin C (more per gram than citrus), vitamin A, and potassium — fresh chilis are highly nutritious.
In the kitchen, green (immature) chilis are milder and grassy; ripe red/yellow chilis are sweeter and more complex due to sugar accumulation.
Capsaicin has antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic properties — chilis have been used medicinally in Aztec and traditional Asian medicine for millennia.

care.placement_hints: Long-lived woody shrub (15–30 years indoors); slow-growing, low-maintenance, and drought-tolerant once established. Iconic for its glossy leaves.

common_conditions: scale insects — bay laurel's signature pest
yellowing from nitrogen hunger over time
slow growth indoors from low light
lore: Bay laurel holds deep significance in Western culture — ancient Greeks and Romans crowned victors (emperors, poets, athletes) with laurel wreaths. The word 'laureate' (as in poet laureate) derives from Laurus nobilis. In mythology, Daphne, a nymph, was transformed into a bay laurel by the gods to escape Apollo's pursuit.
etymology: 'Laurus' is the Latin name for bay; 'nobilis' means 'noble' — reflecting its esteemed place in classical culture. The myth of Daphne transformed into laurel is the genus's namesake story.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours acceptable; prefers dappled light; tolerates partial shade better than most herbs
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale foliage, sparse branching, weak flavour in leaves
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare — bay laurel tolerates full sun; intense afternoon heat may scorch leaves only in extreme conditions (>35°C)

placement_tips: Place 1–2 metres from a south or east-facing window; bay laurel tolerates bright indirect light and partial shade better than sun-demanding herbs
Water moderately — the soil should dry between waterings. Check moisture with your finger; water when the top 2–3 cm feels dry.
Prune gently in spring to shape the plant and remove any scale-infested branches; bay laurel responds well to clipping and can be trained into a topiary form
Feed lightly every 3–4 months during the growing season; bay laurel is a slow feeder and doesn't need intensive nutrition like basil or mint

conditions.name: Scale insects — bay laurel's signature pest
conditions.symptoms: Small hard brown bumps cluster on stems and leaf undersides; sticky honeydew residue; sooty mould on leaves below infested branches.
conditions.remedy: Isolate the plant; scrape off scale insects by hand using a soft brush or cloth; spray with neem oil weekly for 4–6 weeks. Prune heavily infested branches.
conditions.prevention: Bay laurel is highly susceptible to scale — inspect leaf undersides monthly. Quarantine new plants for 3 weeks. Treat at first sign of brown bumps.

conditions.name: Yellowing from nitrogen depletion over years
conditions.symptoms: Older leaves turn pale yellow; new growth slows; overall vigour declines after 2–3 years indoors.
conditions.remedy: Feed with a balanced liquid fertiliser (10-10-10) every 3–4 months; or top-dress with compost in spring.
conditions.prevention: Bay laurel is a light feeder but benefits from annual refreshing. Feed quarterly during the growing season.

conditions.name: Slow growth indoors from low light
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows only 2–3 cm per year despite good care; branching is sparse.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location (1–2 metres from a window); ensure at least 4+ hours of bright indirect light daily.
conditions.prevention: Bay laurel grows slowly indoors even with good care — this is normal. Patience is required; accept 5–10 cm growth per year.

conditions.name: Stem dieback from cold exposure
conditions.symptoms: Stems blacken from the tip downward; leaves drop; growth halts.
conditions.remedy: Trim away blackened stems back to healthy green wood; move the pot away from cold windows; maintain temperature above 5°C.
conditions.prevention: Bay laurel dislikes cold drafts below 5°C — keep away from uninsulated windows in winter. It's cold-hardy outdoors but tender indoors.

conditions.name: Long-lived perennial nature — multi-decade commitment
conditions.symptoms: Plant becomes large and woody over 5–10 years; eventually reaches ceiling height or becomes unwieldy.
conditions.remedy: Prune regularly to control size; consider air-layering to propagate or replanting the mature plant outdoors in a mild climate.
conditions.prevention: Bay laurel can live 15–30+ years indoors — plan for long-term care. Annual pruning keeps size manageable.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.

facts: Laurus nobilis is a long-lived perennial woody shrub — plants can live 15–30+ years indoors with minimal care, making it a legacy herb.
Ancient Greeks and Romans crowned victors, emperors, and celebrated poets with laurel wreaths (corona laurea) — the tradition lingers in the term 'laureate'.
In Ovid's Metamorphoses, the nymph Daphne was transformed into a bay laurel to escape Apollo's advances — the myth is the genus's namesake.
Bay laurel grows slowly indoors (2–5 cm per year) but is nearly immortal — patience is rewarded with a handsome, multi-decade plant.
The leaves have a subtle sweet, slightly peppery flavour; they're used whole in soups, stews, and braises, then removed before serving (the leaves are tough and inedible).
Bay leaf flavour develops during slow drying — fresh leaves are mild, but dried leaves (2–3 weeks) become more aromatic and complex.
A single bay leaf infuses a pot of soup for hours; the flavour compounds are fat-soluble and distribute throughout liquids slowly.
Bay laurel essential oil contains eugenol (clove-like), linalool (floral), and geranyl acetate (fruity) — the interplay creates its subtle complexity.
The plant is highly susceptible to scale insects — bay laurel growers must inspect leaf undersides monthly to catch infestations early.
Bay laurel tolerates partial shade and bright indirect light better than sun-demanding herbs like basil or rosemary.
In Mediterranean regions, bay laurel grows as an outdoor shrub 3–5 metres tall; indoor cultivation keeps it compact via pruning.
The plant was sacred to Apollo in classical Greek culture — temples planted bay laurels, and priestesses chewed bay leaves before prophecies.
Bay laurel leaves can be air-dried by hanging stems upside-down in a dark, breezy location for 2–3 weeks; dried leaves store for 1+ year.
The plant's tough, waxy leaves are naturally antimicrobial — they repel some insect pests, though scale insects are an exception.
A 10-year-old bay laurel plant indoors will reach 60–90 cm height, making it an impressive focal point and a reliable source of culinary leaves.

care.placement_hints: Fast-growing annual that bolts quickly in heat; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for continuous harvest. Both seeds and leaves are edible.

common_conditions: rapid bolting to flower in heat and long days
lore: Coriander seeds were found in Tutankhamun's tomb (1300s BCE), making it one of the oldest known cultivated herbs. The plant was used in ancient Egyptian, Greek, and Roman medicine. Today, coriander is essential to Indian, Mexican, and Middle Eastern cuisine — both seeds and fresh leaves are used extensively.
etymology: 'Coriandrum' comes from Greek 'koriannon', possibly from 'kore' (maiden), though the origin is disputed. 'Sativum' means 'cultivated'. The English name 'coriander' is used for the seeds; 'cilantro' (from Spanish) for the fresh leaves.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 4–6 hours; long days (>14 hours) trigger bolting
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Weak spindly growth, pale foliage, early bolting, poor leaf production
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rapid flowering (bolting) in heat above 25°C; leaf production stops

placement_tips: Place in a bright window with 4–6 hours of direct sun, but avoid hot afternoon sun (>25°C) which triggers bolting — east-facing windows are ideal
Sow new seeds every 2–3 weeks for continuous leaf harvest; a single plant flowers within 6–8 weeks, ending leaf production.
Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged; coriander's fine roots dry out quickly and cause wilting.
Harvest outer leaves first; a young plant yields leaves for 4–6 weeks before bolting. Once flowers form, collect seeds by hanging the plant upside-down in a paper bag

conditions.name: Rapid bolting to flower in heat and long days
conditions.symptoms: Plant suddenly sends up tall flower stalks; leaf production stops abruptly; remaining leaves become thin and bitter.
conditions.remedy: Once bolting begins, it's irreversible — compost the plant or harvest the developing seeds. Flowers (small white or pale pink) are edible and mildly coriander-flavoured.
conditions.prevention: Succession sow every 2–3 weeks for continuous supply. Grow in cool locations (15–20°C); avoid windowsills above 25°C. Provide afternoon shade in summer.

conditions.name: Weak growth from insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Plant becomes tall and spindly with sparse foliage; leaf production is slow; growth is delayed.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location; add a grow light if necessary. Coriander benefits from 4–6 hours of direct sun daily.
conditions.prevention: Coriander needs good light for robust foliage production — bright indirect or 4+ hours of direct sun is essential.

conditions.name: Wilting and leaf scorch from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Plant droops suddenly despite apparent moisture; leaf edges brown and curl; soil surface feels dry.
conditions.remedy: Water immediately and deeply; soak the pot in a basin for 10 minutes. Resume consistent watering schedule.
conditions.prevention: Coriander's fine roots dry quickly — water when the top 1 cm of soil feels dry. Check daily; avoid drought stress.

conditions.name: Splitting and cracking stems
conditions.symptoms: Stems develop vertical cracks and splits; foliage wilts intermittently.
conditions.remedy: Water consistently to prevent soil from drying out completely. Trim split stems; they won't recover.
conditions.prevention: Maintain consistent, moderate moisture — neither dry nor waterlogged. Avoid feast-famine watering cycles.

conditions.name: Short productive window (4–6 weeks)
conditions.symptoms: Plant produces leaves briefly, then bolts; window for harvesting is short.
conditions.remedy: Accept coriander's short lifespan and plan succession sowings every 2–3 weeks for continuous supply.
conditions.prevention: Sow new seeds frequently; stagger plantings by 2–3 weeks. Once a plant bolts, compost it and start a fresh one.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Coriandrum sativum is unique — both the fresh leaves (cilantro) and dried seeds (coriander) are used in cooking with entirely different flavour profiles.
Coriander seeds were found in Tutankhamun's tomb in the 14th century BCE, making it one of the earliest cultivated herbs in recorded history.
The fresh leaves (cilantro) taste lemony and herbal; the dried seeds taste warm, nutty, and slightly sweet — no overlap. Both are essential to different cuisines.
Coriander is a cool-season crop that bolts (flowers) rapidly in heat (>25°C) and long days (>14 hours) — succession sowing every 2–3 weeks is essential for continuous supply.
The plant produces leaf for only 4–6 weeks before flowering; once flowers form, leaf production stops and quality declines dramatically.
Coriander seeds are round and ribbed, about 3–4 mm in diameter; they're harvested by cutting the entire plant upside-down into a paper bag when seeds turn tan (about 3 weeks after flowering).
The plant's aroma comes from linalool and geranyl acetate — compounds that are volatile and diminish with drying (unlike bay or thyme).
In Indian cuisine, roasted coriander seeds are a fundamental spice in garam masala and curries; ground coriander powder is warm and slightly floral.
A genetic marker affects perception of cilantro (fresh leaves) — about 14% of people perceive a soapy flavour due to the olfactory compound aldehyde C10.
Coriander was used as a spice in ancient Egypt, Rome, and Greece; medieval herbalists prescribed it for digestive complaints (some evidence supports this).
Fresh coriander leaves are highly perishable — they lose flavour within 2–3 days in the fridge. Freezing in water/oil cubes preserves flavour for 3–4 months.
Coriander seeds store for years in airtight containers; they remain potent for 12+ months if kept dry and cool, making them ideal for long-term storage.
The plant's fine roots are shallow and sensitive to drought — inconsistent watering causes stress and early bolting.
In Mexican cuisine, fresh cilantro is essential to salsas and guacamole; in Indian cuisine, it's a garnish on curries, chutneys, and breads.
Coriander is one of the oldest known medicinal herbs — ancient texts describe its use for digestive issues, inflammation, and anxiety (modern research is ongoing).

care.placement_hints: Use bark-based orchid mix; water by submerging pot weekly; ensure cool night temps (10°C drop) to trigger blooming.

common_conditions: root rot from soggy bark mix
failure to rebloom without winter dormancy
lore: Sympodial orchids with canes that flower along their entire length, sometimes producing delicate pink, white, or purple flowers. In the wild, they are epiphytes clinging to Himalayan oak trees, surviving monsoons and cool winters.
etymology: From the Greek 'dendron' (tree) and 'bios' (life), referring to their tree-dwelling habit. 'Nobile' means noble or distinguished.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun; avoid hot afternoon rays
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Spindly canes, few flowers, small pale leaves
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Leaf scorch, bleached foliage, canes sunburned

placement_tips: East-facing windowsill with diffused morning sun
Slatted shelving allows air circulation under the canes
Winter: move to cooler location (60–65°F nights) for 4–6 weeks to initiate flower spikes
Hang from a moss pole or stake to mimic natural tree-clinging growth

conditions.name: Bud blast
conditions.symptoms: Flower buds fall off unopened; shriveled buds before opening.
conditions.remedy: Maintain stable temperature; avoid drafts and sudden cold drops. Most fallen buds cannot be recovered.
conditions.prevention: Keep nights 10°C cooler than days; stable 60°F overnight in winter triggers bloom set.

conditions.name: Root rot from soggy bark
conditions.symptoms: Mushy roots, black root tips, foul smell, wilting despite frequent watering.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim rotten roots with sterile scissors, repot in fresh orchid bark mix with drainage.
conditions.prevention: Let bark dry between waterings; never water daily; use pot with air holes.

conditions.name: Failure to rebloom
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows vegetatively but produces no flower spikes season after season.
conditions.remedy: Ensure 4–6 weeks of cool dormancy (60°F nights); reduce watering during this period.
conditions.prevention: Cool winter rest is non-negotiable for Dendrobium nobile; many indoor growers skip this step.

conditions.name: Keiki formation
conditions.symptoms: Small plantlets with roots sprouting from old canes; can break off canes with weight.
conditions.remedy: Once keikis have 2–3 roots, cut below the keiki and pot in orchid bark; mother plant continues blooming.
conditions.prevention: Keikis are normal; remove if you want all energy into flowering, or propagate if you want new plants.

conditions.name: Pseudobulb shrivel
conditions.symptoms: Canes look wrinkled and thin; stored water depleted.
conditions.remedy: Soak pot in water for 15 minutes weekly; increase humidity with a humidifier or pebble tray.
conditions.prevention: Water thoroughly until water drains; pseudobulbs need full hydration to store moisture.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Dendrobium nobile is an epiphyte native to cool Himalayan oak forests, requiring a winter rest to bloom reliably.
The name 'dendrobium' comes from Greek 'dendron' (tree) and 'bios' (life), reflecting their tree-clinging nature.
Noble dendrobiums flower along the entire length of mature canes, creating cascading columns of blooms.
In cultivation, a 4–6 week winter dormancy with nights at 60°F is essential to initiate flower spike formation.
The bark-based orchid medium mimics their natural epiphytic habitat, allowing roots to dry rapidly between waterings.
Keikis (vegetative offshoots) often form on old canes when dormancy conditions are ideal; these can be rooted and potted separately.
The sympodial growth pattern means new canes emerge from the rhizome base rather than a single growing shoot.
Clear plastic orchid pots help monitor root health; the silvery velamen (root sheath) is photosynthetically active.
Watering by submersion (dunking the pot in a basin) is more reliable than top-watering for orchid bark mixes.
Humidity above 50% keeps pseudobulbs plump and maintains moisture reserves for flowering and growth.
Temperature drops at night trigger flower initiation; indoors, moving plants to a cooler room in autumn works well.
Bud blast (unopened flowers dropping) is nearly always caused by temperature fluctuations or cold drafts.
Many dendrobium hybrids are more forgiving than pure D. nobile, but still require cooler dormancy periods.
The velamen root covering is silvery because it reflects heat and is semi-transparent, allowing photosynthesis.
Overwatering is the #1 killer of Dendrobium nobile; bark must dry significantly between waterings.

care.placement_hints: Monopodial growth; use medium orchid bark; water weekly by submersion; bright light crucial for blooming.

common_conditions: crown rot if water sits on growing tips
lore: Named for William Cattley, an English plantsman who first successfully cultivated it indoors in 1818—a horticultural triumph. Nearly extinct in the wild from over-collection by Victorian orchid hunters.
etymology: Genus 'Cattleya' honors William Cattley, an English merchant and horticulturist. 'Labiata' refers to the showy labellum (lower petal).

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours morning sun ideal; scorch risk with afternoon rays
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin leaves, weak new pseudobulbs, flower spikes fail to form
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow burnt patches, bleached foliage, excessively hard leaves

placement_tips: Bright south or east window (3–4 hours morning sun minimum for blooming)
Support with a stake as new pseudobulbs develop; they can reach 60 cm tall
Repot only after blooming and when new growth emerges (usually spring)
Space plants to allow air circulation; poor air flow encourages fungal diseases

conditions.name: Pseudobulb shrivel
conditions.symptoms: Canes lose turgidity and look wrinkled; tips turn brown; stored water depleted.
conditions.remedy: Increase watering frequency to twice weekly; use a pebble tray or room humidifier.
conditions.prevention: Water thoroughly; pseudobulbs serve as water reserves—keep them plump for blooming.

conditions.name: No blooms on mature plant
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows large pseudobulbs and leaves but produces no flower spikes.
conditions.remedy: Increase light dramatically; move to brightest window. Reduce watering slightly in winter.
conditions.prevention: Light is the #1 trigger for Cattleya blooming; low light = vegetative growth only.

conditions.name: Root rot from soggy bark
conditions.symptoms: Blackened roots, mushy feel, foul odor; leaf yellowing despite watering.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim all dark/soft roots, spray with cinnamon, repot in fresh bark mix.
conditions.prevention: Allow bark to dry between waterings; orchid bark drains faster than soil.

conditions.name: Crown rot from water pooling
conditions.symptoms: New growth rots at the base; dark mushy tissue at the crown where new leaves emerge.
conditions.remedy: Remove all water from the growing tip; improve air flow; do not water the crown directly.
conditions.prevention: Water only the medium or base; never spray or pour water into the crown.

conditions.name: Scale insects on foliage
conditions.symptoms: Small hard brown bumps on leaf undersides and along pseudobulbs; sticky honeydew.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand with a soft brush; treat with neem oil weekly for 4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Monthly leaf inspection catches early infestations; isolation of new plants prevents spread.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

facts: Cattleya labiata is the type species of the genus Cattleya, named after William Cattley who cultivated it first in 1818.
Nearly extinct in the wild from 19th-century orchid hunters who stripped Brazilian rainforests, now protected.
The showy labellum (lower petal) is typically deep purple-red, earning the common name 'Ruby-lipped cattleya'.
Cattleyas grow as sympodial orchids, developing pseudobulbs that alternate along the rhizome base.
Light is the primary trigger for Cattleya blooming; low-light collections rarely flower despite reaching maturity.
The Atlantic forest habitat experiences distinct wet and dry seasons, shaping their dormancy needs in cultivation.
Cattleya roots are velamen-covered and photosynthetically active; clear pots let you monitor root color (silvery = healthy).
Blooming usually occurs once yearly in spring after a winter rest period with slightly reduced watering.
The genus Cattleya revolutionized Victorian horticulture and remains one of the most coveted orchids for cut flowers.
Crown rot is a major problem; water applied to the growing shoot tip during winter can trigger fungal death.
Monopodial growth means the plant has a single main stem that elongates year-round without dormancy.
Pseudobulbs serve as water and nutrient storage; shriveled bulbs indicate insufficient hydration.
Hybrid Cattleyas often tolerate broader light ranges than pure C. labiata, making them more beginner-friendly.
Repotting should happen only after blooming finishes and new growth begins, reducing transplant shock.
The fragrance of Cattleya blooms is highly variable between clones, from subtle to intensely sweet.

care.placement_hints: Warm-growing epiphyte; medium to fine bark mix; weekly submersion watering; minimal dry period.

common_conditions: failure to spike from insufficient light
webbing spider mites in low humidity
lore: The dancing-lady orchids are prized for prolific sprays of small yellow flowers that resemble tiny jumping figures. In the wild, they hang from tree limbs in Mexican cloud forests, swaying with tropical breezes.
etymology: From the Greek 'onkos' (swelling) and 'idion' (small), referring to the warty/swollen lip. 'Sphacelatum' means spotted or marked.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun; filter afternoon rays
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: No flower spikes, thin leaves, pseudobulbs fail to develop
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow leaf edges, burnt leaf tips, bleached foliage

placement_tips: Bright windowsill or shelving under grow lights (14 hours daily helps reliability)
Warm-growing: keep above 65°F year-round; brief 50°F dips can trigger blooming
Use misting bottle or pebble tray to maintain 60%+ humidity in dry climates
Hang or mount on bark slabs to mimic natural tree-clinging habit

conditions.name: Root rot from wet bark
conditions.symptoms: Black mushy roots, foul smell, yellowing new growth despite watering.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, prune all dark roots, dust with cinnamon, pot in fresh orchid bark mix.
conditions.prevention: Allow bark to dry between waterings; use fine-grade bark for faster drainage.

conditions.name: Failure to produce flower spikes
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows vigorously but never forms a spray of flowers.
conditions.remedy: Ensure bright indirect light (medium-bright range); a brief cool period helps.
conditions.prevention: Oncidium sphacelatum needs strong light to initiate spikes; low light = foliage only.

conditions.name: Pseudobulb dehydration
conditions.symptoms: Pseudobulbs wrinkle and shrink; leaves lose turgor.
conditions.remedy: Increase watering to twice weekly; boost humidity with a humidifier or humidity tent.
conditions.prevention: Orchid bark must not dry out completely; it should feel damp but not soggy.

conditions.name: Spider mite infestation in dry air
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing, stippled yellow leaves, foliage loses shine and vigor.
conditions.remedy: Mist foliage daily; apply neem oil weekly; increase humidity aggressively.
conditions.prevention: Humidity above 60% suppresses spider mites; misting also disrupts their lifecycle.

conditions.name: Crown rot from poor air flow
conditions.symptoms: New growth blackens at the base; growing tip becomes mushy and dark.
conditions.remedy: Improve air circulation with a fan; remove the affected new growth; stop misting the crown.
conditions.prevention: Never spray water on the growing tip; ensure strong air movement around plants.

facts: Oncidium sphacelatum produces long branching sprays with dozens of small yellow flowers resembling dancing figures.
The swollen lip and warty texture gave rise to the genus name from Greek 'onkos' (swelling).
Native to Mexican cloud forests, they epiphytically perch on tree limbs and grow in humid, warm conditions year-round.
The flowers are pollinated in nature by male euglossine bees attracted to their scent.
A single mature plant can produce 50+ tiny yellow blooms on a single spray, creating an explosion of color.
Oncidium sphacelatum is warm-growing and dislikes cool dormancy, unlike many other orchid types.
The dancing-lady common name refers to the uncanny resemblance of the flowers to figures in motion.
Fine-grade orchid bark drains quickly, suiting their epiphytic roots that cannot tolerate soggy conditions.
Humidity is critical; dry air rapidly shrivels pseudobulbs and invites spider mites.
Blooming typically occurs in spring after a brief warm dry period of slightly reduced watering.
The widespread genus Oncidium includes hundreds of species with similar dancing-lady flower structure.
Oncidium sphacelatum is one of the smallest-flowered species in the genus, yet the spikes are prolific.
Pseudobulbs are small and compact; the plant takes up little horizontal space when mounted.
Clear potting allows monitoring of the silvery velamen, which turns green when actively absorbing water.
The genus name origin relates to the biological structure, not any cultural or historical figure.

care.placement_hints: Cool-growing; medium orchid bark; weekly submersion watering; requires cool autumn nights (50°F) to bloom.

common_conditions: failure to bloom without cool dormancy
fungal leaf spot in crowded conditions
lore: The aloe-like strapped leaves give this Cymbidium its common name. In the wild, they hang from moss-covered tree branches in Southeast Asian highlands, where cool autumn nights trigger stunning pale-green or cream flower spikes.
etymology: 'Cymbidium' from Greek 'kymbe' (boat), referring to the boat-shaped labellum. 'Aloifolium' means aloe-like leaves.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 2–3 hours morning sun; protect from harsh afternoon rays
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin strappy leaves, weak new growth, no flower spikes
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Yellow burnt patches on leaves, hardened foliage, color bleaching

placement_tips: Cool-growing orchid: needs outdoor summer placement or bright, cool windowsill
Essential: 6–8 weeks of cool nights (50–55°F) in autumn to trigger flower buds
Space plants with space for air circulation; crowding promotes fungal diseases
Repot spring after blooming; large plants can stay in the same pot 3–4 years

conditions.name: Failure to bloom despite maturity
conditions.symptoms: Large pseudobulbs and healthy leaves, but no flower spikes form.
conditions.remedy: Move to a cooler location in autumn; provide 6–8 weeks of 50–55°F nights.
conditions.prevention: Cool dormancy is non-negotiable for Cymbidium aloifolium; indoor heat prevents blooming.

conditions.name: Pseudobulb dehydration
conditions.symptoms: Bulbs wrinkle and shrink; new growth emerges weakly.
conditions.remedy: Increase watering to twice weekly; improve humidity with a humidifier.
conditions.prevention: Orchid bark must stay evenly moist; pseudobulbs need good hydration for vigor.

conditions.name: Root rot from soggy medium
conditions.symptoms: Black mushy roots, foul odor, yellowing leaves despite watering.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim rotten roots, repot in fresh orchid bark with excellent drainage.
conditions.prevention: Medium-grade bark drains well; allow slight drying between waterings.

conditions.name: Scale insects on pseudobulbs
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps on canes and leaf bases; honeydew stickiness.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand; treat weekly with neem oil for 4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Monthly inspection of pseudobulbs catches scale early; isolate infested plants.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot in crowding
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-ringed spots on leaves; rapid spread in poor air flow.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves; space plants for air movement; apply copper fungicide.
conditions.prevention: Thin spacing, avoid overhead watering, and good ventilation prevent fungal issues.
