conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Episcia cupreata is a close relative of African violets (genus Saintpaulia) but diverged millions of years ago and evolved trailing rather than upright growth.
The delicate orange-red trumpet flowers are pollinated by hummingbirds in the rainforest canopy—they are a remarkable adaptation to attract hummingbird vision.
Episcia spreads via horizontal stolons (runners) much like strawberries; plantlets form at each node along the runner, creating new rosettes.
Some cultivars of Episcia cupreata have coppery-bronze foliage (hence 'cupreata') while others are deep green—the color depends on light intensity and cultivar genetics.
The plant is native to the humid Colombian rainforest, where it grows as an epiphytic creeper on fallen logs and tree bases, not in soil.
Episcia is extremely sensitive to water quality; chlorine and fluoride in tap water cause immediate cell damage and leaf splitting—this is perhaps the plant's pickiest requirement.
Unlike African violets, which dislike water on their foliage, Episcia tolerates (even prefers) misting and high humidity without developing fungal issues.
Episcia flowers are produced intermittently throughout the year in optimal conditions (18–24°C, high humidity, bright indirect light).
The plant's stolons can grow 2–3 feet long in a single season, making it ideal for hanging baskets or cascading from terrarium edges.
Episcia is non-toxic to cats and dogs, making it a safe choice for pet households.
Propagation is trivial: plantlets forming along stolons will root in 1–2 weeks when pinned into moist soil or water.
The genus Episcia contains 7 species, all from South American rainforests; E. cupreata is the most commonly cultivated.
In very high humidity (85%+), Episcia can produce flowers continuously with minimal dormancy.
The plant's sensitivity to cold water and temperature is thought to be an adaptation to the stable, warm rainforest climate where it evolved.
Episcia flowers are produced from leaf axils along the stems; removing flowers does not prevent further flowering—the plant will flower multiple times per year.

care.placement_hints: Must maintain 70%+ humidity; forms dense pale-green hummocks; ideal for closed terrariums or shade.

origin: Cool-temperate Northern Hemisphere forests (Europe, North America, Asia)
common_conditions: decay from poor drainage in sealed terrariums
loss of structure from crushing or overhandling
algae overgrowth in stagnant humid air
lore: Leucobryum glaucum is a true moss—the only species in this catalog that is not vascular. It lacks roots entirely, instead anchoring with rhizoids (hair-like structures that do not transport water). Mosses have no vascular system and absorb water directly through their cell walls. This makes Leucobryum uniquely suited to terrariums: it thrives in high humidity and never dries out. The pale bluish-green color comes from air-filled hyaline cells that reflect light. Collectors prize these 'cushion mosses' for their sculptural hummocks, which can persist unchanged for 5+ years.
etymology: 'Leucobryum' derives from Greek 'leuco' (white) + 'bryon' (moss), referring to the pale color. 'Glaucum' means 'grayish' or 'blue-green,' describing the hue. 'Cushion moss' and 'pincushion moss' refer to the dense rounded hummocks.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None—direct sun scorches the delicate tissues
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Very slow growth, pale color, becomes translucent
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Brown discoloration, bleached washed-out color, hummocks flatten or collapse

placement_tips: Position 80–120 cm from a window (medium light, mostly shade-tolerant)
Ideal for the base layer of closed glass terrariums, where it creates a living landscape
Avoid placing under grow lights—even low-intensity LEDs can scorch the delicate tissues
Maintain 16–20°C and 70%+ humidity; this is a cool-climate species and struggles above 22°C

conditions.name: Browning from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Hummocks turn brown and papery; the pale bluish-green color fades.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity to 70%+ immediately; in closed terrariums, ensure adequate condensation; mist daily in open dishes.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 70%; closed terrariums are essential for this species.

conditions.name: Browning from too-bright light
conditions.symptoms: Hummocks develop brown patches; pale color bleaches out; tissue becomes papery.
conditions.remedy: Move further from the light source or shade with a semi-transparent cloth; recover may take 2–3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: This is a shade-loving forest moss; provide medium light at most, never direct or bright light.

conditions.name: Decay and fungal growth in sealed terrariums
conditions.symptoms: Hummocks develop black or gray slimy coating; musty smell; tissue becomes soft and collapses.
conditions.remedy: Improve drainage by ensuring a gravel layer at the terrarium base; open the lid for 30 minutes daily to reduce stagnancy.
conditions.prevention: Mosses do not require much water; in sealed terrariums, the base should have gravel for drainage, not pooled water.

conditions.name: Loss of structure from crushing
conditions.symptoms: Hummocks flatten or collapse when stepped on or crushed; they do not recover their original shape.
conditions.remedy: Accept the new flat form; new growth will eventually restore structure over months.
conditions.prevention: Mosses are fragile; avoid placing where they will be touched or walked on.

conditions.name: Algae overgrowth in stagnant air
conditions.symptoms: Bright green algal mat forms on the moss surface, obscuring the pale hummocks.
conditions.remedy: Increase airflow by opening the terrarium lid; algae prefers still, humid air and disappears with better ventilation.
conditions.prevention: In closed terrariums, open briefly (10–15 minutes) every 3 days to prevent algae and bacterial overgrowth.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.

conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Leucobryum glaucum is a true moss, lacking roots entirely—it anchors with rhizoids (hair-like structures) and absorbs water directly through cell walls.
Unlike vascular plants, mosses have no internal water-conducting tissue; every cell must be within 1 cm of a water source to survive.
The pale bluish-green hue comes from hyaline (transparent) air-filled cells that reflect light and help regulate moisture.
Leucobryum glaucum does not photosynthesize as efficiently as vascular plants—it grows very slowly, increasing in size only 1–2 cm per year.
These 'cushion mosses' form hummocks because they grow in a compact branching pattern, creating dense 3D domes rather than flat mats.
Leucobryum glaucum can persist unchanged in a terrarium for 5+ years without growing or shrinking, making it a permanent landscape element.
Mosses reproduce via spores, not seeds; spores are microscopic and dispersed by air currents in their native forests.
In old-growth temperate forests, thick cushion-moss carpets cover the ground, protecting tree seedlings and creating microhabitats for insects.
Leucobryum species are more primitive than ferns but have survived for 400+ million years, indicating remarkable evolutionary stability.
The moss is non-toxic to all animals and is sometimes harvested from wild forests for floristry and terrarium use.
Leucobryum glaucum prefers cool climates (10–18°C) and weakens if exposed to warm dry conditions (above 22°C with low humidity).
In closed terrariums, this moss thrives in the shadiest, most humid corners, where it forms a stable 'canopy understory' beneath vascular plants.
Mosses can survive complete desiccation and revive within hours of rehydration—a trait called 'poikilohydry' found nowhere else in the plant kingdom.
Leucobryum is almost never bothered by pests; its lack of vascular tissue and dense structure make it inhospitable to insects.
The species has been used in traditional Japanese moss gardens for centuries, where hummocks are cultivated as living sculpture for decades or centuries.

care.placement_hints: Prefers 60%+ humidity; compact rosette with corrugated leaves; produces white flower spikes.

common_conditions: brown leaf edges from low humidity
weak pale growth from insufficient light
lore: Peperomia caperata is prized for its deeply corrugated or 'rippled' leaves, which create a textured rosette that catches light beautifully. The common name 'Emerald ripple' refers to the rich green corrugated foliage. The plant sends up delicate white flower spikes ('rat tails') that are attractive but insignificant—the real appeal is the leaf structure. Unlike many terrarium plants, Peperomia prefers slightly drier conditions (though still humid) and is prone to root rot in waterlogged soil.
etymology: 'Peperomia' derives from Greek 'peperi' (pepper) + '-omia' (resemblance), as the plant is a relative of black pepper (though not edible). 'Caperata' means 'wrinkled' or 'corrugated,' referring to the distinctive leaf texture.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: None—direct sun scorches leaves
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale weak growth, corrugations become less pronounced, leaves become thin
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached washed-out color, brown leaf edges, growth halts

placement_tips: Position 50–80 cm from an east or north-facing window (bright indirect light)
Ideal for the mid-height layer in open terrariums; avoid placing in the most humid spot (Peperomia prefers 60% humidity, not 80%+)
Use only distilled or room-temperature water; cold tap water causes leaf splitting
Maintain 18–24°C; avoid cold drafts which stall growth and flowering

conditions.name: Leaf split from cold tap water
conditions.symptoms: Leaves develop translucent split lines or appear to burst after watering; damage appears within 24 hours.
conditions.remedy: Use only room-temperature distilled water; trim damaged areas; affected leaves will eventually drop.
conditions.prevention: Never use cold tap water—the combination of temperature shock and chlorine causes cell rupture.

conditions.name: Brown leaf edges from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips and margins turn brown and papery; spreads inward from edges.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity to 60%+; mist with distilled water or place on a pebble tray; trim brown edges.
conditions.prevention: Maintain 60%+ humidity; avoid very dry rooms (below 50%).

conditions.name: Weak pale growth from insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Plant is pale and thin; corrugations become less pronounced; growth is nearly imperceptible.
conditions.remedy: Move 20 cm closer to the light source; within 2 weeks, color and texture should improve.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light; unlike shade-obligate plants, Peperomia requires moderate brightness.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soil smells musty; plant wilts despite constant moisture; leaves yellow and fall.
conditions.remedy: Allow soil to dry partially between waterings; ensure drainage exists; if rot is advanced, repot in fresh soil.
conditions.prevention: Water only when the top 1 cm of soil feels dry; Peperomia stores water in its leaves and prefers drying cycles.

conditions.name: Sparse weak growth from nutrient starvation
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows very slowly; leaves are small and pale; overall size barely increases.
conditions.remedy: Apply a dilute liquid fertilizer (1/4 strength) every 3 weeks; refresh soil with compost.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 4–6 weeks during the growing season; use nutrient-rich terrarium soil.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.

facts: Peperomia caperata's 'rippled' or 'corrugated' leaf texture creates maximum surface area for photosynthesis while adding remarkable aesthetic appeal.
The corrugations also serve a functional purpose: they increase leaf strength and reduce water loss by creating microclimates within the surface texture.
Peperomia is a succulent-leaved plant—it stores water in its fleshy leaves and stems, explaining its tolerance for drying out between waterings.
The white flower spikes ('rat tails') are the plant's actual inflorescence; they are wind-pollinated (not showy like Episcia flowers).
Peperomia caperata is one of 1,600+ species in the Peperomia genus, making it one of the largest genera of tropical plants.
The genus Peperomia is related to black pepper (Piper nigrum) but is not edible—the name 'Peperomia' means 'pepper-like.'
Peperomia has a compact root system adapted to epiphytic growth (on logs and trees) rather than soil; it prefers pots that are wider than tall.
Unlike most terrarium plants, Peperomia prefers 60% humidity rather than 70%+ and is prone to rot in very humid sealed terrariums.
The plant propagates easily from leaf cuttings: a single leaf placed on moist soil will develop roots and a new rosette within 3–4 weeks.
Peperomia is non-toxic to cats, dogs, and humans, making it a safe choice for all households.
The corrugated leaf surface has a velvety texture due to fine transparent hairs that reduce water loss—a feature of many rainforest understory plants.
In its native Brazilian habitat, Peperomia grows on mossy logs and tree trunks in the darkest forest understory (5–10% of full sunlight).
Peperomia caperata is compact and slow-growing (2–3 cm per year), making it ideal for small terrariums and remaining in bounds for years.
The plant's sensitivity to cold water is thought to be an adaptation to the stable, warm, humid rainforest environment where it evolved.
Peperomia caperata's compact rosette habit and slow growth make it perfect for placing in the center of a terrarium, where it becomes a long-lived focal point.

care.placement_hints: Keep in bright indirect light to prevent reversion to green; avoid dark corners.

common_conditions: Reversion to green in low light
Brown leaf tips from low humidity
lore: A recent cultivar bred for its striking silvery-pale foliage, popular in modern minimalist interiors. The silvery color is recessive and requires consistent bright light to maintain.
etymology: Named for its moonlight-like silvery sheen; 'trifasciata' refers to the three-banded cross-striping pattern.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours morning sun tolerated; reversion risk in afternoon direct sun.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Silvery leaves fade to green, slow growth, pale new leaves
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached washed-out appearance if exposed to intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: Place 0.5–1m from a bright east-facing window
Keep away from dim hallways — silver color fades quickly without bright light
Rotate quarterly to maintain even coloration across all leaves
Winter light is often insufficient — move closer to a window in December–February

conditions.name: Silver-to-green reversion
conditions.symptoms: New growth emerges green instead of silver; existing pale leaves retain color but no longer brighten.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter spot (2+ meters from a south or west window). New leaves emerging after 4–6 weeks will show silver if light is sufficient.
conditions.prevention: Maintain medium-to-bright indirect light; winter light drops 40% — adjust placement seasonally.

conditions.name: Wrinkled curled leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves develop vertical creases and curl inward; silvery surface dulls.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once, then let dry fully. Most cases recover within a week if light improves.
conditions.prevention: Don't push waterings past 3 weeks indoors; check soil before watering.

conditions.name: Bleached patchy silver
conditions.symptoms: Some leaves develop white or cream patches; others fade unevenly.
conditions.remedy: Move away from direct afternoon sun; trim severely bleached leaves. Recovery takes 2–3 months.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light, not direct sun. Filter harsh afternoon rays through a sheer curtain.

conditions.name: Black stem base
conditions.symptoms: Blackening starts at soil line; affected leaves wilt despite appearing dry.
conditions.remedy: Cut off affected leaves at the soil line; reduce water drastically; repot in fresh dry mix if spread.
conditions.prevention: Always use a pot with drainage; never top-water heavily; let soil dry between waterings.

conditions.name: Soft mushy base
conditions.symptoms: Base of plant becomes squishy; foul smell; leaves separate easily from rhizome.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away all rotten tissue with a clean knife, dust with cinnamon, let callus 2 days, repot in dry gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Never overwater; check soil is bone-dry before watering; winter watering should be minimal.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

facts: Moonshine snake plant can revert to solid green in low-light conditions due to a recessive chlorophyll-modulating mutation.
Tissue-culture propagation ensures genetic stability of the silvery coloration in commercial Moonshine cultivars.
The pale color originates from reduced chlorophyll content, requiring bright light to prevent gradual darkening.
NASA's Clean Air Study (1989) listed Sansevieria among top air-purifying houseplants, removing formaldehyde and benzene.
Sansevieria was reclassified to the genus Dracaena in 2012 by botanists, though common names and horticulture still use the old name.
Moonshine cultivars were first commercially released in the early 2000s by Dutch propagators.
The silvery sheen reflects light more efficiently than green foliage, making it appear luminous in moderate indoor lighting.
Reversion in low light occurs within 2-3 growth cycles if ambient light drops below 1000 lux.
Unlike most variegated plants, Moonshine's silver coloration is stable once established in proper light conditions.
The cultivar thrives in USDA zones 10-12 outdoors but is primarily grown as a houseplant in temperate regions.
Moonshine produces inconspicuous white flowers with pale yellow stamens when mature and well-established.
The three-banded striping pattern (trifasciata) becomes more visible as silver leaves age, typically over 6-9 months.
Commercial growers maintain Moonshine mother plants under 12-16 hour daily lighting to prevent accidental reversion.
Moonshine's silvery leaves contain less lignin than green varieties, making them slightly softer and more prone to physical damage.
The cultivar gained popularity in Scandinavian minimalist interior design during the 2010s, driving global demand.

care.placement_hints: Compact rosette form tolerates low light; excellent for desks and shelves.

origin: Sport discovered in 1939 in New Orleans by nurseryman William Hahn
common_conditions: Overwatering in compact rosette form — slow drainage
Water pooling at leaf bases causing rot
lore: The first patented Sansevieria cultivar (1941). Its compact growth habit and decorative rosette form made it a mainstay of 1970s terrariums and modern minimalist decor.
etymology: Named for William Hahn, the American hybridizer who discovered the sport; 'Hahnii' is the diminutive form of his surname.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours morning sun tolerated; no afternoon sun needed.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale new leaves in the rosette center
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached patchy white appearance if exposed to intense sun

placement_tips: Ideal for desks and side tables with medium ambient light
Fits well on shelves away from heating vents
Avoid enclosed terrariums — the dense rosette traps moisture
Water carefully around the base; avoid pooling in the center

conditions.name: Water pooling in the rosette center
conditions.symptoms: Water collects in the tight leaf whorl; foul smell develops; center leaves become translucent.
conditions.remedy: Tilt the plant to drain trapped water; use a paper towel to wick moisture from the center. Let dry fully before watering again.
conditions.prevention: Water only at the soil line, never from above. Use a narrow-spout watering can. Bottom-watering is ideal for this form.

conditions.name: Compact rosette overwatering risk
conditions.symptoms: Mushy soft leaves in the center; foul smell; slow drainage through the compact soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, inspect the rhizome for rot, cut away blackened tissue, repot in very gritty (50% perlite) mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only every 16–20 days indoors. Check soil is completely dry before watering. 'Hahnii' needs drier conditions than tall Sansevieria.

conditions.name: Mealybugs in the dense center
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps hidden in the tight leaf joints; hard to access with a cotton swab.
conditions.remedy: Spray the center with 70% isopropyl alcohol; use a syringe to inject into gaps. Repeat every 7 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Inspect the center monthly. Improve air circulation; avoid misting.

conditions.name: Slow-draining compact soil
conditions.symptoms: Soil stays wet for 10+ days; roots begin to rot; leaves become soft.
conditions.remedy: Repot in a much grittier mix (equal parts potting soil, perlite, and coarse sand). Use a small terracotta pot for faster drying.
conditions.prevention: Use only fast-draining cactus or succulent mix. Terracotta pots dry faster than plastic.

conditions.name: Wrinkled curled leaves in rosette
conditions.symptoms: Leaves curl inward at edges; the rosette appears wilted and creased.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once by soaking the pot base for 10 minutes; then let dry completely. Recovery is slower in compact forms — expect 10–14 days.
conditions.prevention: Establish a regular watering schedule; don't skip more than 20 days. Water from below when possible.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Sansevieria hahnii was the first patented Sansevieria cultivar, granted U.S. Patent 2,195,066 in 1941 by William Hahn of New Orleans.
William Hahn discovered this compact sport (genetic mutation) while growing Sansevieria trifasciata in his New Orleans nursery.
The 'bird's nest' common name derives from the tight rosette formation of leaves, resembling a small bird's nest structure.
Unlike parent species that grow 60-100+ cm tall, Hahnii stays under 30 cm, making it ideal for desktop and shelf placement.
Hahnii produces shorter flower spikes than Sansevieria trifasciata, with cream-colored fragrant blooms appearing in spring.
This cultivar was originally marketed as 'Bird's Nest Snake Plant' by Hahn's nursery in the 1940s.
Leaf arrangement is more densely packed than the parent species, with each rosette containing 8-12 leaves instead of 6-8.
Hahnii's compact size made it commercially viable for office desk culture and modern minimalist interiors from the 1960s onward.
The cultivar exhibits the same CAM photosynthesis and drought tolerance as its parent species, requiring minimal water.
Tissue-culture propagation of Hahnii was perfected in the 1980s, dramatically reducing nursery propagation time from 18 to 4 months.
Hahn's original patent expired in 1961, allowing other growers to commercially propagate and sell the cultivar freely.
Hahnii's compact growth habit is controlled by a recessive dwarfing allele that slows cell elongation in the petiole (leaf stem).
The cultivar was heavily marketed in Japanese plant culture during the 1970s, becoming a status symbol for bonsai enthusiasts.
Mature Hahnii rosettes can be separated and rooted as individual plantlets, each developing into a new compact plant within 4-6 weeks.
Hahnii remains one of the best-selling compact Sansevieria varieties globally, accounting for roughly 15% of commercial Sansevieria sales.

care.placement_hints: Tall cylindrical form suits corners and hallways; thrives with bright light.

origin: Angola, native to savannas and rocky outcrops
common_conditions: Brown cylindrical leaf tips from dry air
lore: In Angola, cylindrica leaves were historically braided into rope and used as bowstring material for hunting. The commercial trade often artificially braids young plants into spirals — a labor-intensive process that marks premium specimens.
etymology: Named for its distinctive cylindrical tubular leaves; 'cylindrica' is Latin for tube-shaped. The species originates from Angola in south-central Africa.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 3 hours morning sun tolerated; afternoon rays risk browning tube tips.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, thin weak new leaves, plant leans toward light
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached washed-out color; brown tips on tube edges in intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: Place 1–1.5m from a bright north or east-facing window
Excellent as a tall upright specimen in corners — fills vertical space with minimal footprint
Avoid dark interiors — cylindrica needs more light than flat-leafed Sansevieria
Rotate monthly to prevent leaning toward the light source

conditions.name: Leaf-tip browning from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Tube tips develop dry brown patches; edges crisp and papery; plant otherwise healthy.
conditions.remedy: Trim brown tips at an angle with clean scissors. Mist weekly or place on a pebble tray with water (not touching the pot).
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 40%; group plants together; avoid heating vents and AC ducts directly on the plant.

conditions.name: Brown cylindrical tube tips in dry climates
conditions.symptoms: The rounded tube ends develop drying brown bands; tips curl inward slightly.
conditions.remedy: Trim off the brown section cleanly with a sharp knife. The plant continues growing new leaves from below.
conditions.prevention: Mist the plant 2–3 times weekly during winter heating season. Pebble tray with water underneath helps.

conditions.name: Sparse weak growth in medium light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge thin and short; gaps between leaves increase; plant looks sparse.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window (within 1m of north or east glass). Growth accelerates within 3–4 weeks with better light.
conditions.prevention: Cylindrica needs more light than standard Sansevieria. Place in bright indirect light, not medium shade.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy base; foul smell; tubes become translucent and collapse.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, cut away all black rotten roots and tissue, dust with cinnamon, let callus 2 days, repot in very gritty mix.
conditions.prevention: Water even less frequently than other Sansevieria — every 18–21 days. Let soil dry completely.

conditions.name: Wrinkled papery tubes from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Cylindrical leaves shrivel and develop lengthwise creases; the whole plant looks desiccated.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot base for 15 minutes; water from below to rehydrate. Recovery is fast — expect plumped tubes within 3 days.
conditions.prevention: Don't let the soil dry out for longer than 20–21 days indoors. Set a watering reminder.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

facts: Sansevieria cylindrica is native to Angola in southwestern Africa, where it grows in rocky, semi-arid savanna ecosystems.
Historically, leaves of S. cylindrica were twisted and braided into bowstrings, fishing lines, and rope by Angolan herders.
The cylindrical leaf structure allows the plant to minimize water loss through its reduced surface area in arid climates.
In traditional Angolan rope-making, S. cylindrica leaves were harvested at 2-3 meters length, dried, and twisted into cordage.
The cultivar 'Boncel' is a dwarf form with tightly bunched cylindrical leaves, reaching only 20-30 cm tall.
S. cylindrica employs CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) photosynthesis, opening stomata at night to minimize water loss during the day.
Commercial decorative braiding of S. cylindrica leaves is still practiced in East Africa, producing basket-weave cylinder arrangements.
The plant's cylindrical leaves contain thick-walled cells and a spongy water-storage parenchyma, enabling 3-4 month drought survival.
In horticultural cultivation, S. cylindrica is occasionally braided into spiral or circular forms during active growth.
The cylindrical morphology is controlled by genes affecting blade width and margin fusion during leaf development.
Leaf turgidity in S. cylindrica depends on nightly stomatal opening and nighttime CO₂ fixation into malic acid.
Propagation from leaf cuttings takes 6-8 weeks longer in S. cylindrica than in flat-leaved Sansevieria species due to meristem location.
The species thrives in gritty, extremely well-draining soil with perlite or pumice comprising at least 50% by volume.
S. cylindrica's cultural significance in Angola is documented in traditional ethnobotany texts from the early 20th century.
Modern tissue-culture labs in South Africa and Kenya now propagate S. cylindrica commercially for the indoor plant trade.

care.placement_hints: Striking dark foliage suits modern interiors; shade-tolerant.

common_conditions: Dust accumulation hiding the black color
lore: A relatively new cultivar bred for its deep black-green foliage with subtle silver banding. It adds dramatic contrast to minimalist interiors and is popular in contemporary Scandinavian-style plantscaping.
etymology: Named for the coral-black color of its leaves, combining the deep marine hue of black coral (Leiopathes) with the plant's architectural form.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours morning sun tolerated; no harsh afternoon sun required.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale new leaves, black color remains but doesn't deepen
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached grayish cast on leaf tips in intense direct sun

placement_tips: Display 1–2m from a north or east-facing window
Excellent for low-light corners; dark color suits moody interiors
Dust dark leaves monthly to reveal their black luster
Pair with bright accent plants to create visual contrast

conditions.name: Dust accumulation obscuring black color
conditions.symptoms: Black leaves appear gray and dull; silver bands become invisible under a dusty film.
conditions.remedy: Wipe each leaf with a soft damp cloth monthly. For stubborn dust, use distilled water to avoid mineral spots.
conditions.prevention: Dust monthly to maintain the plant's dramatic appearance. Grouped plants reduce dust exposure.

conditions.name: Pale black color in low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge with a greenish tint; the black coloration is muted rather than deep.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a window with bright ambient light (within 1.5m). Black deepens within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide consistent medium light; rotate quarterly for even coloration.

conditions.name: Wrinkled curled leaves
conditions.symptoms: Leaves develop vertical creases and curl inward; black color dulls slightly.
conditions.remedy: Deep-water once, then let dry fully. Most cases recover within a week.
conditions.prevention: Don't push waterings past 3 weeks indoors; check soil before watering.

conditions.name: Black stem base and soft rot
conditions.symptoms: Blackening starts at soil line and moves up the leaf; base becomes mushy.
conditions.remedy: Cut off affected leaves at the soil line; reduce water drastically. If rot spreads, unpot and repot in fresh dry mix.
conditions.prevention: Always use a pot with drainage; never top-water heavily; let soil dry between waterings.

conditions.name: Mealybugs harder to spot on dark leaves
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps visible only in bright light; discovered late in the infestation.
conditions.remedy: Inspect under bright light weekly. Dab each bug with 70% isopropyl alcohol on a cotton swab.
conditions.prevention: Shine a flashlight into leaf joints weekly; quarantine new plants; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

facts: Sansevieria 'Black Coral' is named for its very dark green-black leaf coloration, resembling deep-sea coral specimens.
The dark green color in 'Black Coral' results from extremely high chlorophyll concentration and deep anthocyanin layering.
This cultivar is one of the few Sansevieria varieties that remains visibly dark even in lower light conditions.
'Black Coral' maintains low maintenance requirements identical to other Sansevieria varieties, thriving on neglect.
The cultivar's leaf edges often develop burgundy or near-black margins, intensifying with age and maturity.
Tissue-culture propagation of 'Black Coral' became widely available commercially in the 2010s.
Unlike 'Moonshine', 'Black Coral' shows minimal reversion risk in moderately low light, making it forgiving for darker spaces.
The dark coloration provides excellent camouflage in its native semi-arid environments, reducing herbivore pressure.
Mature 'Black Coral' plants can reach 60-90 cm tall with 6-8 leaves per rosette, similar to Sansevieria trifasciata.
'Black Coral' produces the same cream and white fragrant flower spikes as other Sansevieria varieties in spring.
The cultivar's dark coloration makes it popular in modern Scandinavian and Japanese interior design aesthetics.
Propagation from leaf cuttings of 'Black Coral' yields plantlets within 6-8 weeks, with coloration stable from emergence.
The high anthocyanin content in 'Black Coral' leaves may provide additional UV-protective and antioxidant functions.
Commercial growers select for the darkest offspring during 'Black Coral' breeding, intensifying the black-green phenotype.
'Black Coral' is notably pest-resistant and rarely affected by spider mites, likely due to its thick waxy leaf coating.

care.placement_hints: Cream-and-green variegation shows best in bright light; excellent statement plant.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets (ficin in sap can irritate skin and GI tract).

origin: Tissue-culture cultivar developed by Dutch nurseries
common_conditions: Variegation scorch in direct afternoon sun
Brown leaf tips from low humidity
lore: A Dutch tissue-culture cultivar whose name is a Dutch female given name. 'Tineke' was developed to maintain variegation consistency in propagation. The sectoral cream-and-green pattern and pink flushed new growth make it one of the most visually striking houseplants.
etymology: Named 'Tineke' (diminutive of 'Tine'), a Dutch feminine given name. The cultivar represents Dutch horticultural innovation in tissue-culture propagation.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 1 hour morning sun; harsh afternoon rays scorch variegated leaves.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Variegation fades to solid green; new leaves lose pink flush; slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Cream patches burn white or papery; leaves bleach and drop prematurely

placement_tips: Place 1–1.5m from an east-facing window for morning light
Filter harsh afternoon sun with a sheer curtain
Rotate monthly to prevent lopsided growth toward light
New growth emerges pink-flushed — brighten placement slightly in winter to maintain variegation

conditions.name: Variegation scorch in direct sun
conditions.symptoms: Cream sections develop papery white patches; leaves bleach and become brittle.
conditions.remedy: Move away from direct afternoon sun immediately. Trim burned leaves. New growth will restore color in 3–4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light, not direct sun. Filter afternoon rays with a sheer curtain. South-facing windowsills burn 'Tineke' quickly.

conditions.name: Variegation fading to green in low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge solid green without cream sectoring; existing variegated leaves retain color.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window (within 1m of east or north glass). Variegation usually returns in new growth within 3–4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light. Winter light is insufficient — move closer to the window December–February.

conditions.name: Pink new growth from flushing
conditions.symptoms: Emerging leaves have pink-red flush; older leaves green. Some growers find this beautiful; others prefer solid green.
conditions.remedy: No action needed. Pink flushing is normal and indicates healthy new growth. It fades to green within 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Brighter light and warmer conditions enhance pink coloration. Cooler temperatures reduce it slightly.

conditions.name: Sticky gummosis from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Sap weeps from leaf bases and stems; sticky amber droplets on foliage and beneath the plant.
conditions.remedy: Reduce watering frequency; improve drainage; wipe away sticky residue with a damp cloth. Don't plant on light-colored surfaces.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings. Use a pot with drainage; never bottom-water 'Tineke'.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold drafts or temperature swings
conditions.symptoms: Leaves fall suddenly near windows, doors, or heating vents in winter. Plant otherwise healthy.
conditions.remedy: Move away from the draft source. Prune any remaining bare stems to promote branching.
conditions.prevention: Keep 'Tineke' at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows and 50 cm from AC/heating vents. Avoid temperature swings below 13°C.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: 'Tineke' is a Dutch female given name, a diminutive of Catharina, reflecting the cultivar's Dutch tissue-culture origins.
Ficus elastica 'Tineke' was developed through tissue-culture propagation at a Dutch nursery in the 1980s for stable variegation.
The variegation pattern (cream and pink striping) is stable in 'Tineke', unlike some variegated Ficus cultivars prone to sectoring.
New growth of 'Tineke' exhibits pink-tinted leaves due to high anthocyanin pigment concentrations in young tissue.
Anthocyanin pigments in young Tineke leaves likely provide UV protection during the critical early growth phase.
The cream and pink variegation requires bright indirect light to maintain vibrancy; low light can reduce color contrast by 30-40%.
Tissue-culture multiplication of 'Tineke' allows propagators to produce 50-100 plantlets from a single mother plant in 6-8 weeks.
The cultivar's leaf size typically ranges from 15-20 cm long and 8-12 cm wide, larger than some variegated rubber plant cultivars.
'Tineke' is more compact than green-leaved Ficus elastica, rarely exceeding 1.5 meters indoors without aggressive pruning.
The pink flush in new growth fades to cream-and-white striping after 3-4 weeks as anthocyanin production ceases.
Ficin (a proteolytic enzyme) in 'Tineke' sap can cause mild skin irritation and latex sensitivity in some individuals.
Variegation in 'Tineke' is genetically stable due to tissue-culture origin, unlike hand-propagated variegated specimens.
'Tineke' propagates readily from semi-hardwood cuttings, rooting within 3-4 weeks in humid conditions.
The cultivar became popular in Scandinavian plant design from 2015 onward, particularly in minimalist modern interiors.
'Tineke' requires air layering or cutting-propagation for size control, as pinching alone cannot effectively shape the plant.

care.placement_hints: Deep maroon-black leaves create dramatic focal points; one of the darkest houseplants.
care.toxicity: Toxic to pets (ficin in sap can irritate skin and GI tract).

common_conditions: Dust accumulation hiding the burgundy color
Brown leaf tips from low humidity
Leaf drop from cold or low light
lore: Sometimes called 'Decora' in European trade. 'Burgundy' is sometimes confused with 'Black Prince,' though both refer to the same deep maroon-black cultivar. It represents one of the darkest foliage colors among commonly available houseplants.
etymology: Named for its deep burgundy-red coloration; the 'Black Prince' alias references its dark lordly presence in interior plantscaping.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: Up to 2 hours morning sun tolerated; afternoon rays are too intense for sustained exposure.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Burgundy color fades to greenish-brown; slow growth; light color doesn't deepen
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached washed-out appearance; leaves lose the rich maroon tone

placement_tips: Display 1–1.5m from an east-facing window for morning light
Excellent as a dramatic statement plant in entryways or living rooms
Dust dark leaves monthly to reveal their deep burgundy luster
Winter placement: move closer to windows to maintain color; light drops 40% in winter

conditions.name: Dust accumulation hiding burgundy color
conditions.symptoms: Burgundy-black leaves appear gray and dull; the rich color fades under accumulated dust.
conditions.remedy: Wipe each leaf with a soft damp cloth monthly. For stubborn dust, use distilled water to avoid mineral spots.
conditions.prevention: Dust monthly to maintain the plant's dramatic appearance. A clean 'Burgundy' is visibly darker and more striking.

conditions.name: Greenish-brown color in low light
conditions.symptoms: Burgundy color fades toward olive or green; leaves lose their deep maroon tone.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window (within 1m of east or north glass). Rich burgundy tone usually returns in 3–4 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light. Rotate monthly for even coloration. Winter light is often insufficient.

conditions.name: Sticky gummosis from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Sap weeps from leaf bases and stems; sticky amber droplets collect on foliage and surfaces below.
conditions.remedy: Reduce watering frequency; improve drainage; wipe away sticky residue. Don't position on light-colored furniture.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings. Use a pot with drainage holes.

conditions.name: Leaf drop from cold or insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Leaves fall suddenly in winter near windows or in dimly lit areas. Plant otherwise healthy.
conditions.remedy: Move to a warmer, brighter location away from drafts. Prune bare stems to encourage branching.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 13°C; maintain bright light year-round. Move away from cold windows and heating vents.

conditions.name: Brown leaf-tip edges from low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Leaf tips develop dry crispy brown margins; otherwise healthy-looking foliage.
conditions.remedy: Trim brown edges with clean scissors. Mist weekly or place on a pebble tray with water.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 45%; group plants together; avoid heating vents and AC directly on the plant.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Ficus elastica 'Burgundy' is sometimes called 'Black Prince' or 'Decora' by nurseries, though 'Burgundy' is the accepted cultivar name.
The maroon-black leaf coloration in 'Burgundy' results from excessive anthocyanin overexpression, a dominant trait in this cultivar.
Unlike 'Tineke's' pink flush, 'Burgundy' new growth emerges deep burgundy and only gradually darkens to near-black with age.
Anthocyanin overexpression in 'Burgundy' may provide enhanced photodamage protection but also reduces overall photosynthetic efficiency by 10-15%.
The ficin sap of 'Burgundy' is mildly irritating to skin and can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
'Burgundy' is more shade-tolerant than the green-leaved species, performing acceptably in medium indirect light.
The cultivar's dark coloration makes it visually striking in modern dark-themed or gothic-inspired interior designs.
'Burgundy' leaves often display subtle burgundy veining alongside the deep maroon blade, adding textural interest.
The dark pigmentation in 'Burgundy' intensifies with age; young plants appear more reddish-burgundy than mature specimens.
Tissue-culture propagation of 'Burgundy' became commercial in the 1990s, making the cultivar widely accessible.
'Burgundy' grows slightly slower than green-leaved Ficus elastica, likely due to reduced photosynthetic capacity from pigment density.
Propagation from semi-hardwood cuttings of 'Burgundy' produces reliable rooting within 3-5 weeks in humid conditions.
The cultivar is popular in Japanese and Scandinavian interior design, symbolizing elegance and moody sophistication.
Mature 'Burgundy' plants often develop a narrow branching habit, requiring less pruning than green-leaved varieties for shape maintenance.
'Burgundy' air roots readily along the stem when humidity exceeds 65%, creating an interesting aerial rooting structure.

care.placement_hints: Shade-tolerant with heavy silver-green marbling; thrives in offices and dim corners.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets (oxalate crystals in sap).

origin: Modern cultivar from Thai breeding programs
common_conditions: Brown leaf tips from tap water chlorine
Sparse growth in very low light
lore: Part of the Aglaonema hybrid breeding revolution from Thailand in the 1990s onward. 'Silver Bay' represents the push for more silver variegation and heat tolerance in Aglaonema cultivars popular in Southeast Asian horticulture.
etymology: Named for its prominent silver marbling that creates a 'bay' or section of silvery color across each leaf.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: No direct sun; bright indirect light only.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale new leaves, marbling fades toward solid green
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Scorched leaf patches; severe direct sun bleaches variegation

placement_tips: Ideal for office desks and dim hallways; one of the most shade-tolerant Aglaonema cultivars
Mist weekly or use a pebble tray to maintain humidity around 60%
Keep away from cold windows and heating vents
Rotate monthly for even growth; new leaves emerge from the center

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from tap water chlorine
conditions.symptoms: Brown or tan crispy tips on new leaves; otherwise healthy growth.
conditions.remedy: Switch to distilled or filtered water. Brown tips don't recover but don't spread. Trim tips at an angle for appearance.
conditions.prevention: Use distilled, filtered, or rainwater. Let tap water sit 24 hours to allow chlorine to evaporate before watering.

conditions.name: Marbling fades in very low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge nearly solid green; silver marbling is minimal or absent.
conditions.remedy: Move to a space with more ambient light. Silver variegation usually returns in new growth within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain at least medium light — 'Silver Bay' needs slightly brighter conditions than solid-green Aglaonema to maintain marbling.

conditions.name: Sparse weak growth in low light
conditions.symptoms: Long gaps between leaves; new growth is small and pale; plant looks thin.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location (within 1m of a window with bright indirect light). Growth accelerates within 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: While 'Silver Bay' is shade-tolerant, consistent growth requires medium to bright indirect light.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy stems; foul smell; leaves yellow and wilt despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away rotten roots and stems, repot in fresh fast-draining mix. Hold water for 2 weeks to recover.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2 cm of soil dry between waterings. Use a pot with drainage holes. Reduce watering in winter.

conditions.name: Brown spots from cold or drafts
conditions.symptoms: Sudden dark brown patches on leaf tips and edges; appears 24–48 hours after cold exposure.
conditions.remedy: Move away from the cold source. Trim affected leaves once damage stabilizes. New growth will be healthy.
conditions.prevention: Keep Aglaonema above 13°C. Avoid single-glazed windows, AC ducts, and doors in winter.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Aglaonema 'Silver Bay' is a 1990s-era hybrid output from intensive Thai Aglaonema breeding programs led by commercial nurseries.
The silver-marbled leaf pattern in 'Silver Bay' results from stable genetic variegation, remaining consistent through vegetative propagation.
Unlike many variegated houseplants, 'Silver Bay' tolerates fluorescent office lighting exceptionally well, thriving under 40-50 micromoles PAR.
'Silver Bay' exhibits very slow growth even under optimal conditions, gaining only 2-3 new leaves per year in mature plants.
The cultivar prefers warm, humid conditions (20-25°C, 60%+ humidity), typical of Southeast Asian greenhouse cultivation.
Aglaonema 'Silver Bay' produces small white or pale yellow spathes (flowers) sporadically during spring, rarely flowering indoors.
Propagation from stem cuttings of 'Silver Bay' roots readily in water or moist soil within 2-3 weeks.
The leaf pattern in 'Silver Bay' can lighten in very low light, though the plant survives and grows minimally.
This cultivar was specifically bred for corporate office environments where low light and artificial lighting predominate.
'Silver Bay' is moderately toxic to cats and dogs, containing insoluble calcium oxalate crystals in leaf tissue.
The silver variegation in 'Silver Bay' becomes more pronounced on mature leaves compared to juvenile foliage.
Tissue-culture propagation of 'Silver Bay' was perfected in the late 1990s, enabling mass-market distribution.
'Silver Bay' rarely exceeds 60-80 cm in height, making it suitable for desktop and enclosed office spaces.
The cultivar is one of the few Aglaonema varieties tolerant of consistent low humidity typical in modern office buildings.
'Silver Bay' root systems are relatively shallow, preferring containers no deeper than 15-20 cm.

care.placement_hints: Striking pink-red leaf margins fade in low light; thrives in bright shade.
care.toxicity: Mildly toxic to pets (oxalate crystals in sap).

origin: Thai cultivar from 1990s breeding programs; modern hybrid from Aglaonema rotundum
common_conditions: Pink-red color fades in low light
Brown leaf tips from tap water chlorine
lore: Developed by Thai breeders (notably Greg Hambali) in the 1990s, 'Red Siam' revolutionized Aglaonema breeding by introducing vivid pink-red variegation through crosses with Aglaonema rotundum. This cultivar represents the peak of modern Aglaonema color breeding.
etymology: 'Siam' refers to the historical name for Thailand, the origin of the cultivar. 'Red' describes the prominent red-pink variegation.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: No direct sun; bright indirect light enhances red coloration.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pink-red margins fade to greenish; leaves become nearly solid green
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached pale patches; severe direct sun scorches variegation

placement_tips: Place within 1m of a bright north or east-facing window for best red coloration
Keep away from dark corners — red fades quickly in low light
Mist weekly to maintain humidity and enhance color vibrancy
Rotate monthly for even variegation development across all sides

conditions.name: Pink-red color fades in low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge nearly solid green; pink-red margins are minimal or absent.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location (within 1m of a window). Red coloration usually returns in new growth within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light. 'Red Siam' needs more light than other Aglaonema to display its signature red variegation.

conditions.name: Brown leaf tips from tap water chlorine
conditions.symptoms: Brown or tan crispy tips on new leaves; otherwise healthy growth.
conditions.remedy: Switch to distilled or filtered water. Brown tips don't recover but don't spread. Trim tips at an angle.
conditions.prevention: Use distilled, filtered, or rainwater. Let tap water sit 24 hours to allow chlorine to evaporate.

conditions.name: Red margins pale in medium light
conditions.symptoms: Red margins are present but pale pink rather than vibrant; red fades with time.
conditions.remedy: Increase light gradually by moving closer to a window. Brighter light deepens the red tone over time.
conditions.prevention: Provide consistent bright indirect light. Winter placement should move 1m closer to windows.

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy stems; foul smell; leaves yellow and wilt despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away rotten roots and stems, repot in fresh fast-draining mix. Hold water for 2 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2 cm of soil dry between waterings. Use a pot with drainage. Reduce watering in winter.

conditions.name: Sparse weak growth from insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Long gaps between leaves; new growth is small, pale, and slow to emerge.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter location (1–2m from a window with bright indirect light). Growth should accelerate within 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: 'Red Siam' needs light to thrive; low-light placement results in slow sparse growth and color loss.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

facts: Greg Hambali, an Indonesian Aglaonema breeder, revolutionized red Aglaonema breeding through selective crossing in the 1990s.
Aglaonema 'Red Siam' was specifically developed from Aglaonema rotundum parentage, incorporating red anthocyanin expression.
Commercial red Aglaonema varieties first appeared in Thai nurseries in the early 1990s, largely due to Hambali's breeding work.
'Red Siam' exhibits deeply pigmented pink-red variegation on mature leaves, with green margins forming a striking contrast.
The red coloration in 'Red Siam' intensifies on mature leaves, typically reaching full intensity after 8-12 weeks of growth.
Unlike 'Silver Bay', 'Red Siam' prefers bright indirect light to maintain vibrant red coloration, fading in low-light conditions.
'Red Siam' grows at a moderate pace, approximately 1.5-2 times faster than 'Silver Bay' under equivalent conditions.
The cultivar was originally exported from Thailand to international markets starting in the late 1990s, gaining popularity by 2005.
A. rotundum, the ancestral species, is native to Southeast Asia and naturally exhibits warmer color tones than other Aglaonema species.
'Red Siam' prefers warm, humid tropical conditions (22-28°C, 60-70% humidity) for optimal growth and color expression.
Propagation from cuttings of 'Red Siam' produces reliably colored plantlets within 3-4 weeks in humid propagation conditions.
The anthocyanin pigments in 'Red Siam' leaves increase significantly during cooler seasons (15-20°C), deepening the red tone.
'Red Siam' is toxic to cats and dogs due to insoluble calcium oxalate crystals, similar to other Aglaonema species.
Tissue-culture propagation of 'Red Siam' became standard by 2005, making the cultivar commercially competitive at nurseries worldwide.
Greg Hambali's breeding lineage led to over 20 subsequent red Aglaonema cultivars, establishing Thailand as a red Aglaonema hub.

care.placement_hints: Demands bright indirect light and humidity; dramatic pink stripes require consistent care.

origin: Northwestern South America (Colombia, Venezuela); now classified in genus Goeppertia
common_conditions: Crispy edges from tap water minerals (chlorine, calcium)
Brown curled edges from low humidity
Black leaf spots from cold drafts
lore: Originally classified as Calathea, Pinstripe was reclassified to the genus Goeppertia in 2012 along with other Calathea species. Despite the name change, it remains widely sold as 'Calathea ornata.' The plant is nyctinastic — its leaves fold dramatically at night, a behavior that inspired the 'prayer plant' common name.
etymology: 'Ornata' means ornamental or decorated, referring to the fine pink stripes. 'Pinstripe' describes the delicate parallel line pattern.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: No direct sun; filtered morning light only.
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pink stripes fade to white; leaves pale; growth slows; leaf movement weakens
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Bleached washed-out appearance; brown scorch patches; stripes become invisible

placement_tips: Place 0.5–1.5m from a bright east-facing window for morning light and high humidity
Use a pebble tray with water underneath; mist twice weekly to maintain 60–80% humidity
Rotate monthly to prevent one-sided growth toward the light
Avoid direct sun, cold drafts, and heating vents — all cause leaf damage

conditions.name: Crispy brown edges from tap water chlorine and calcium
conditions.symptoms: Brown papery edges on leaf tips; sometimes yellow halos. Plant otherwise healthy.
conditions.remedy: Switch to distilled, filtered, or rainwater. Brown edges don't recover but don't spread. Trim edges at an angle.
conditions.prevention: Use only distilled or filtered water. Tap water minerals accumulate in leaf tips. Let tap water sit 24 hours if distilled unavailable.

conditions.name: Leaf edges curl and crisp in low humidity
conditions.symptoms: Leaf edges become papery and curl inward; tips brown and crisp despite adequate watering.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity immediately: mist twice daily, place on a pebble tray with water, or use a humidifier.
conditions.prevention: Maintain humidity above 60%. Pebble tray is essential. Group plants together. Avoid heating vents.

conditions.name: Pink stripes fade in low light
conditions.symptoms: New leaves emerge with pale or white stripes instead of vibrant pink; overall leaf color pales.
conditions.remedy: Move closer to a bright window (within 1m of east glass). Pink coloration usually returns in new growth within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light year-round. Winter light is often insufficient — move closer to windows.

conditions.name: Leaf curl from underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Leaves roll inward; tips curl and crisp; plant looks wilted despite moist-looking soil.
conditions.remedy: Water thoroughly until water drains from the bottom. Check that the soil is consistently moist (not wet), and increase humidity.
conditions.prevention: Pinstripe prefers consistently moist (not wet) soil. Water more frequently than other houseplants — every 6–8 days.

conditions.name: Black spots from cold exposure or water contact
conditions.symptoms: Small black-brown spots appear on leaves 24–48 hours after cold wind exposure or watering with cold water.
conditions.remedy: Move away from cold drafts. Use room-temperature distilled water. Trim affected leaves once damage stabilizes.
conditions.prevention: Keep above 16°C. Avoid single-glazed windows and AC drafts. Use warm distilled water for watering.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.
