facts: San Pedro cactus from the Andes of Peru, Ecuador, and Bolivia.
Contains mescaline at 1.0–2.5% concentration in dry weight.
Ceremonial use documented by Chavín culture in northern Peru around 1000 BCE.
Named 'San Pedro' because Saint Peter holds the keys to heaven and spiritual experience.
The common name references opening spiritual doors through visionary experience.
Archaeological evidence shows pre-Columbian use in shamanic rituals.
Echinopsis is derived from Greek 'echinos' (hedgehog) and 'opsis' (appearance).
The columnar growth form can reach 3–6 meters tall in native habitat.
Pachanoi derives from the Quechua word 'pachamama' (Earth Mother).
Used ceremonially by indigenous Peruvian cultures for centuries.
Mescaline is a controlled substance in most modern jurisdictions worldwide.
The cactus is also cultivated ornamentally as a drought-tolerant landscape plant.
White flowers bloom at night, measuring 2–3 inches across.
Long-spined variants exist, differing from the typical short-spined form.
The plant exhibits remarkable cold tolerance despite its tropical origins.

care.placement_hints: South or southwest window with 6+ hours direct sun. Fast-growing columnar cactus—expect rapid vertical growth.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic to pets; fruit is edible and sweet.

origin: South America (Argentina, Paraguay, Brazil); grows on rocky outcrops and in disturbed areas.
common_conditions: rib stretching and etiolation in low light
crown rot from overwatering at the soil line
slow flowering or no fruiting in insufficient light
need for staking as plant grows tall
lore: Cereus repandus produces large fragrant white flowers that open at night and close by morning, followed by sweet red edible fruits known as 'Peruvian apples.' In South America it is cultivated for both ornament and fruit production. The name 'Peruvian apple' refers to the fruit's appearance and flavor, which is sweet and slightly floral.
etymology: Cereus: from Latin 'cereus' (wax candle), referring to the tall columnar form; 'repandus' means 'wavy' or 'undulating,' referring to the wavy rib edges.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6–8 hours daily for optimal growth and flowering
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Ribs elongate and thin, plant becomes pale, growth slows, flowering fails
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare; epidermis may bronze slightly in extreme heat but remains healthy

placement_tips: South or southwest window for maximum light and heat
Plant is a fast grower and will reach 2m+ within 10 years; allow ample space
Stake early (at 60cm) and tie loosely to support the tall columnar form
Can produce flowers indoors if given 6+ hours of daily direct sun; night-blooming

conditions.name: Rib stretching and etiolation from insufficient light
conditions.symptoms: Ribs become thin and elongated, plant becomes pale and slow-growing, internodes widen.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright direct light immediately. Etiolated segments will not reshape; propagate from healthy portions.
conditions.prevention: Maintain 6–8 hours of direct sun daily; use a grow light if natural sun is inadequate.

conditions.name: Crown rot from overwatering at the base
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy tissue develops at the soil line and spreads upward; entire crown becomes translucent and rotten.
conditions.remedy: Cut away all rotted tissue with a sterile knife, allow the cut surface to dry 1 week, then propagate from healthy cuttings.
conditions.prevention: Never water in winter; water only when soil is bone-dry; ensure excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Mealy infestation in areoles and along ribs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters appear in spine clusters and between ribs; sticky honeydew may be visible.
conditions.remedy: Spray with neem oil or horticultural oil every 7 days for 3–4 weeks. Treatment must be thorough to reach mealies deep in areoles.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect regularly; maintain good air circulation.

conditions.name: Failure to flower or fruit indoors
conditions.symptoms: Plant grows but does not produce the characteristic white night-blooming flowers or red fruits.
conditions.remedy: Increase light exposure to 6+ hours of direct sun daily. Ensure adequate water during growing season. Mature plants (3+ years) are more likely to flower.
conditions.prevention: Provide maximum light and allow the plant to reach maturity (3+ years).

conditions.name: Plant lean or toppling from rapid height growth
conditions.symptoms: As the plant grows quickly to 1–2m, it may lean or bend if not staked; risk of toppling.
conditions.remedy: Install a sturdy stake early (at 60cm height) and tie loosely with soft cloth. Increase stake height as the plant grows.
conditions.prevention: Stake preemptively at 60cm; use a sturdy wooden or metal stake; allow for continued growth.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

facts: Peruvian apple cactus native to tropical South America.
Nighttime-flowering producing one fragrant flower per stem per year.
Produces edible red fruit sometimes called 'pitaya' in regional Spanish.
Named 'repandus' from Latin meaning 'waved' or 'undulating,' referring to the wavy stem ridges.
Flowers are white, large, and sweetly fragrant, blooming in summer.
The red fruit is up to 3–4 inches in diameter and smooth-skinned.
Fruit ripens 4–6 weeks after flowering, turning deep red when mature.
Nighttime blooming is an adaptation to nocturnal pollinators in native habitat.
Can grow 15–40 feet tall in ideal conditions; columnar and tree-like growth.
The stem is blue-green with 4–6 prominent undulating ridges.
Fruit flavor is sweet and slightly tart, with small black seeds throughout.
Spines are relatively short and sparse compared to other Cereus species.
This species is hardier than many tropical cacti, tolerating brief frosts.
The fruit is nutritious and rich in vitamin C and antioxidants.
Traditional medicine in South America uses the fruit for various remedies.

care.placement_hints: South or southwest window with 5+ hours direct sun. Compact globose form; does well on windowsills.

origin: Mexico (Guanajuato state); endemic to semi-arid scrublands at 1200–2000m elevation.
common_conditions: mealy infestation in the white woolly hair covering
rot at the base from overwatering or poor drainage
woolly hair matting and loss in high humidity
lore: Mammillaria hahniana is named after German chemist and naturalist Wilhelm Hahn (1808–1889), a collector of Mexican plants. The common name 'Old lady cactus' refers to the wispy white hair that covers the plant, resembling gray hair on an elderly woman's head. In Mexico, it is known as 'Biznagita de la abuelita' (little grandmother's cactus).
etymology: Mammillaria: from Latin 'mammilla' (breast or nipple), referring to the tubercles (nipple-like bumps) covering the stem; 'hahniana' honors Wilhelm Hahn.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 5–8 hours daily for optimal growth and flowering
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Plant becomes pale, growth slows or stalls, may fail to produce flower buds
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Rare; slight browning of spine tips in extreme heat (>40°C) is not harmful

placement_tips: South or southwest windowsill is ideal for reliable flowering
Keep away from humidity-producing areas (bathrooms, kitchens) to prevent woolly hair matting
Rotate 90° every 2 weeks to ensure even light exposure
Excellent candidate for a small collection on a sunny shelf or windowsill

conditions.name: Mealy infestation in the woolly hair
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters (mealies) nestle within and hide among the fine white hair; sticky residue and plant decline.
conditions.remedy: Spray with neem oil or horticultural oil every 7 days for 4 weeks. The white woolly hair makes identification difficult; monitor closely.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect the woolly hair closely; maintain air circulation.

conditions.name: Base rot from overwatering or poor drainage
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy tissue develops at the base where the plant contacts soil; tissue becomes translucent and rotten.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, cut away all rotted tissue, dust with cinnamon, let dry 3 days, repot in completely dry gritty cactus mix.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is bone-dry; never allow standing water; use a pot with drainage holes.

conditions.name: Woolly hair matting and loss in high humidity
conditions.symptoms: Fine white hair becomes matted, clumped, or moldy in damp conditions; hair falls out or loses its delicate appearance.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant to a drier location with better air circulation. Do not mist or increase humidity. Lost hair will not regenerate.
conditions.prevention: Keep away from bathrooms and kitchens; maintain dry ambient conditions; do not mist.

conditions.name: Slow growth in low light
conditions.symptoms: New growth becomes minimal or ceases; plant remains small and compact without normal seasonal growth.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright direct light. Growth rate will increase noticeably within 4–6 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Maintain 5–8 hours of direct sun daily; use a grow light if natural light is insufficient.

conditions.name: Bud drop from temperature stress
conditions.symptoms: Flower buds form on the areoles but dry out and fall before opening; plant fails to bloom despite good light.
conditions.remedy: Maintain stable temperatures (18–25°C) during bud development. Avoid drafts and sudden temperature swings >10°C.
conditions.prevention: Keep away from cold windows and heating vents; maintain consistent warmth during bud season.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

facts: Old lady cactus from Mexico's Guanajuato region, famous for woolly white spines.
Named for German chemist Wilhelm Hahn in the 19th century.
Ring of vibrant pink flowers surrounds the apex when the plant matures.
The white spines are soft and hair-like, covering the entire plant.
Mammilla derives from Latin 'mamma' (breast), referencing the nipple-like tubercles.
The woolly spines protect against intense desert sun and reduce water loss.
Flowers appear in spring and early summer, typically in a ring around the crown.
This species is slow-growing, reaching only 4–6 inches tall at maturity.
The pink flowers contrast dramatically against the white spine covering.
Hahniana honors Wilhelm Hahn's contributions to succulent horticulture.
The plant clusters freely, forming mounds of pink-flowered rosettes.
White hair-like spines make this one of the most ornamental Mammillaria species.
Water sparingly in winter; the plant requires a dry dormancy period.
This species is easily propagated from offsets, which root readily.
The combination of white spines and pink flowers makes it highly decorative.

care.placement_hints: Bright indirect light 1–2m from a window. Must be grafted onto a green rootstock; the colored tissue lacks chlorophyll.

origin: Cultivated mutant developed in Japan in 1941 by Eiji Watanabe; not found in nature. Comes in red, pink, yellow, and orange varieties.
common_conditions: graft union failure—rootstock dies, colored top cannot survive independently
rot at the graft point from overwatering or poor hygiene
loss of color intensity in low light
rootstock overgrowth if the graft is weak
lore: The Moon cactus (Hibotan) was created in 1941 by Japanese cactus breeder Eiji Watanabe as a bold horticultural innovation. The bright red, pink, yellow, and orange color varieties are all achlorophyllous mutants—they lack chlorophyll entirely and cannot photosynthesize on their own. They must be grafted onto a healthy green rootstock (usually Hylocereus or Cereus) to survive. This cactus became iconic in the 1970s–1980s succulent boom.
etymology: Gymnocalycium: from Greek 'gymnos' (naked) + 'calyx' (cup), referring to the smooth flower cup without prominent scales; 'Hibotan' is the Japanese name, meaning 'firefly ball,' referring to the bright glowing colors.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 0–1 hour (early morning only); direct afternoon sun can fade the vivid color
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Color becomes pale or dull; growth slows; the colored top may deteriorate faster
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Direct afternoon sun fades the vibrant color and may stress the graft union

placement_tips: North-facing windowsill or 1–2m from an east-facing window
Bright indirect light is mandatory; the colored tissue is sensitive to direct sun stress
Never place in a dark corner; minimum 8–10 hours of ambient light daily
Keep the graft union dry; water only at the rootstock base

conditions.name: Graft union failure and rootstock collapse
conditions.symptoms: The green rootstock softens, rots, or separates from the colored top; the colored tissue wilts immediately as it loses life support.
conditions.remedy: Once the rootstock dies, the colored top cannot survive—it has no way to photosynthesize or absorb water. Unfortunately, most Hibotan specimens cannot survive regrafting.
conditions.prevention: Maintain excellent drainage; never water the graft union; keep temps stable; select a plant with a thick healthy rootstock when purchasing.

conditions.name: Rot at the graft point from overwatering or moisture
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy tissue develops at the junction where the colored top meets the rootstock; tissue becomes translucent.
conditions.remedy: If caught early, allow the graft area to dry completely and reduce watering drastically. If rot is advanced, the plant is likely lost.
conditions.prevention: Never water the graft union itself; water only at the rootstock base and allow to dry; maintain excellent air circulation.

conditions.name: Color fading or loss of intensity
conditions.symptoms: The bright red, pink, yellow, or orange color becomes pale or dull; the plant loses its aesthetic appeal.
conditions.remedy: Increase light exposure gradually (move closer to a bright window). Some color recovery may occur, but faded plants often remain pale.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light; minimum 10 hours daily.

conditions.name: Slow growth or growth stall
conditions.symptoms: New growth is minimal or ceases; the plant remains the same size for extended periods.
conditions.remedy: Increase light exposure and water more consistently during the growing season (but still allow soil to dry). Feed with a diluted balanced fertilizer.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright light; feed every 3–4 weeks during spring and summer; water when soil is barely moist.

conditions.name: Rootstock overgrowth beyond the graft
conditions.symptoms: The green rootstock produces offsets or grows vigorously around the colored top; the balance becomes aesthetically awkward.
conditions.remedy: Trim away green rootstock growths with a clean knife just below the graft line. Focus the plant's energy on the colored top.
conditions.prevention: Monitor the rootstock regularly; remove green growth early before it dominates.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.

facts: Moon cactus is a mutant discovered by Eiji Watanabe at Yamato in Saitama, Japan (1941).
The colored cultivars (red, pink, yellow, orange) all lack chlorophyll entirely.
Achlorophyllous mutants cannot survive independently without a green rootstock.
The colored top is always grafted onto a green rootstock (usually Hylocereus).
Gymnocalycium means 'naked calyx,' referring to the spineless flower tube.
Mihanovichii honors Argentine botanist Miguel Mihanovich who studied cacti.
Hibotan is the Japanese name, meaning 'fire ball,' referencing bright colors.
Without grafting onto green stock, the colored mutant cannot photosynthesize.
The graft union must be maintained; separation means death for the colored top.
Colored variants depend entirely on the rootstock for water and nutrient transport.
The discovery sparked worldwide interest in grafted succulent novelties.
Multiple color morphs exist: red (most common), pink, yellow, and orange forms.
The Hylocereus rootstock (dragon fruit) is cold-hardy and vigorous.
Grafting success depends on maintaining proper moisture and protection.
This curious mutant has become a popular ornamental houseplant worldwide.

care.placement_hints: Full sun; drought-tolerant; fast-draining gritty soil; spineless flat ribbed disc.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; risk from handling is minimal due to lack of spines.

common_conditions: brown patches from sun exposure changes
lore: Critically endangered in wild from illegal poaching; highly prized by collectors worldwide. The 8-ribbed structure resembles both a sea urchin and a starfish, earning multiple common names.
etymology: Named 'asterias' for its distinctive 8-pointed star outline when viewed from above; 'astrum' is Latin for star.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun daily in summer
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Slow growth, pale color, elongation
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald (brown spots) if moved suddenly from shade to intense afternoon sun

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun
Place on a bright bench near glass — no filtering
Repot every 2–3 years into cactus soil mixed with perlite
Keep in a warm spot (18–25°C); avoid temperatures below 10°C in winter

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Mushy base, dark discoloration at soil line, weak growth, mushy texture when gently pressed.
conditions.remedy: Unpot immediately, trim all blackened roots with a sterile knife, let callus 3 days, repot in fresh cactus soil.
conditions.prevention: Water only when soil is completely dry (4–6 weeks); never water in winter; always use drainage holes.

conditions.name: Fungal infection from humidity
conditions.symptoms: Soft rotting patches, often greenish or black mold on surface, musty smell from soil.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected tissue with a sterile knife, dust with sulfur powder, move to brighter drier location, reduce watering.
conditions.prevention: Ensure excellent air circulation; use gritty well-draining soil; avoid wetting the body of the plant.

conditions.name: Pallid color in low light
conditions.symptoms: Cactus becomes pale or grayish; loses the characteristic blue-grey hue; growth halts.
conditions.remedy: Move to a sunnier location gradually (5–7 days transition) to avoid sun scald.
conditions.prevention: Provide at least 6 hours of direct sun; rotate pot quarterly to encourage even growth.

conditions.name: Sun scald after movement
conditions.symptoms: Brown or bleached patches appear after moving from shade to intense sun.
conditions.remedy: Move back to bright indirect light for a week, then acclimate gradually; trim damaged tissue after recovery.
conditions.prevention: Acclimate gradually over 7–10 days when moving to brighter locations.

conditions.name: Slow growth despite care
conditions.symptoms: No visible new areoles (spine clusters) or body expansion over a full growing season.
conditions.remedy: Check light levels (may need 7+ hours of sun); water during growing season (May–Aug); feed monthly with dilute cactus fertilizer.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 3–4 weeks from May to August; maintain consistent temperatures 20–25°C.

conditions.name: Root rot
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.

facts: Astrophytum asterias is critically endangered in its native habitat due to illegal poaching by collectors.
Named 'asterias' — meaning 'star' in Greek — for the distinctive 8-pointed star shape visible from above.
Completely spineless, making it unusual among cacti; instead it has white woolly areoles.
Native to the Chihuahuan Desert where it grows in limestone soil among rocks for protection.
Can take 5–8 years to reach flowering maturity in cultivation; flowers are pale yellow.
Highly prized by succulent collectors; specimens can cost $50–500+ depending on age and rarity.
The common name 'sand dollar cactus' refers to its flat coin-like shape resembling a sand dollar.
In Mexico it's known as 'estrella de Astrophytum' — the star of the Astrophytum genus.
Requires extremely well-draining soil; many growers use a 1:1 ratio of cactus soil to perlite.
Flowers only during the day and close at sunset, lasting just one afternoon.
Can survive temperatures as low as 5°C if kept completely dry during winter dormancy.
Growth rings in the spiny ribs (areoles) can be used to estimate the plant's age.
Contains alkaloid compounds that may explain its popularity in traditional folk medicine.
Some cultivars show unusual coloring patterns — blue-grey bodies are most prized.
Propagation from seed is the standard method; cuttings rarely root due to the low-moisture lifestyle.

care.placement_hints: Bright light; small globose; produces bright pink-red flowers; beginner-friendly.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; spines present but relatively mild.

common_conditions: flowering failure if light is too low
lore: The 'chin' in chin cactus refers to the distinctive protruding chin-like chin beneath the areoles. Gymnocalycium means 'naked calyx' — the flower buds lack spines unlike many cacti.
etymology: Gymnos (naked) + Calyx (flower cup); reflects the spine-free flower buds unique to this genus.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–5 hours of morning sun; afternoon shade in summer
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale color, failure to flower, elongation toward light
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Red or purple discoloration in intense afternoon sun (not harmful but aesthetically different)

placement_tips: Bright windowsill, 50 cm back from south-facing glass to filter afternoon intensity
East-facing windows are ideal; water droplets on morning-sun-exposed plants dry quickly
Repot into 1:1 cactus soil and perlite mix every 2–3 years
Keep above 5°C in winter; dormancy at 10–15°C triggers flowering

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Flowering failure
conditions.symptoms: No flower buds appear despite the plant being of flowering age (3+ years).
conditions.remedy: Provide bright light (6+ hours); ensure winter dormancy at 10–15°C for 4–6 weeks; reduce water in winter.
conditions.prevention: Winter chilling below 15°C is essential for flower bud initiation.

conditions.name: Pale color in low light
conditions.symptoms: Cactus becomes washed-out green; lacks the vibrant coloring seen in brighter specimens.
conditions.remedy: Move gradually to a sunnier location over 5–7 days; increase light to 6+ hours daily.
conditions.prevention: Maintain bright indirect light year-round; rotate quarterly.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation
conditions.symptoms: White cotton-like clusters at areoles (spine clusters) and on the body; sticky honeydew.
conditions.remedy: Isolate plant; treat with isopropyl alcohol on cotton swab; repeat every 3–5 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Inspect new plants before bringing home; monitor monthly.

conditions.name: Slow growth
conditions.symptoms: Minimal expansion or new areole formation over a full growing season.
conditions.remedy: Increase light; feed monthly (May–August) with cactus fertilizer; ensure winter dormancy.
conditions.prevention: Provide 6+ hours of bright light; apply dilute fertilizer every 3–4 weeks in growing season.

conditions.name: Soft spots or rot
conditions.symptoms: Dark soft areas on the body that progress outward; mushy texture.
conditions.remedy: Use a sterile knife to remove all soft tissue; dust with sulfur powder; allow 5 days to callus; repot in fresh soil.
conditions.prevention: Never water on the body; use fast-draining soil; ensure good air circulation.

facts: Gymnocalycium contains approximately 70 recognized species, all native to South America.
Named for the 'naked' flower buds — they lack spines unlike buds of most other cacti.
The 'chin' (chin cactus) refers to the protruding chin-like projection beneath each areole.
Produces vivid pink, red, or magenta flowers that open in late spring to early summer.
Among the easiest cacti to bloom indoors — flowers appear within 3–5 years with proper care.
Winter chilling at 10–15°C for 4–6 weeks is essential for reliable flower bud formation.
Flowers open during the day and last 2–3 days before closing in evening.
Small size (typically 5–8 cm diameter) makes it ideal for windowsill collections.
Native to dry rocky slopes and grasslands of Catamarca Province in the Argentine Andes.
Can survive brief freezes down to 5°C if kept completely dry.
Often used as rootstock for grafting other cacti due to its vigor and reliable growth.
The species name 'baldianum' honors a region in Catamarca where it's naturally found.
Red-pigmented varieties show enhanced color in cooler, brighter conditions.
Some cultivars have unusual characteristics like flattened or ridged bodies.
Propagation from seed is reliable; seedlings flower within 3–4 years with bright light.

care.placement_hints: Full sun; blue-grey ribbed ball; robust grower; beginner-friendly.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; spines present but not aggressive.

lore: The type species of Parodia; recent taxonomic reclassification consolidated several genera (Notocactus, Wigginsia) into Parodia in 2021.
etymology: Magnifica means 'magnificent' in Latin; references the impressive golden-yellow flower display.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun daily
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Elongation, pale color, failure to flower
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald if moved suddenly from shade

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun
Can tolerate full midday sun if acclimated gradually (7–10 days)
Repot every 2–3 years into 1:1 cactus soil and perlite
Keep above 5°C in winter; dormancy below 15°C improves flowering

conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Yellowing in poor light
conditions.symptoms: Body becomes pale green or yellowish; loses the characteristic blue-grey tone.
conditions.remedy: Move to a sunnier location gradually (7–10 days); aim for 6+ hours of direct sun.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light year-round; rotate pot quarterly.

conditions.name: Failure to flower
conditions.symptoms: No flower buds despite plant being 4+ years old and healthy.
conditions.remedy: Ensure winter dormancy below 15°C for 6+ weeks; provide 6+ hours of direct sun; reduce water in winter.
conditions.prevention: Winter chilling is essential; maintain bright light.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation
conditions.symptoms: White cottony patches at areoles and base; sticky honeydew on soil.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; treat with 70% isopropyl alcohol every 3–5 days for 3 weeks; improve air circulation.
conditions.prevention: Inspect new plants; quarantine for 2 weeks before mixing with collection.

conditions.name: Soft rot patches
conditions.symptoms: Dark soft areas on body that expand over days; mushy or squishy texture.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; use sterile knife to remove all soft tissue; dust with sulfur; let callus 5 days; repot.
conditions.prevention: Avoid wetting the body; use fast-draining soil; never water in winter.

conditions.name: Slow growth despite care
conditions.symptoms: Minimal expansion or new spines over a growing season; no new areoles forming.
conditions.remedy: Feed monthly (May–August) with dilute cactus fertilizer; ensure 6+ hours of daily light; check pot size.
conditions.prevention: Apply light fertilizer every 3–4 weeks in growing season; repot every 2–3 years.

facts: Parodia magnifica is the type species of the Parodia genus, established in the 1800s.
Recently reclassified from Notocactus; old hybrids still carry the 'Notocactus magnificus' name.
The blue-grey coloration comes from a waxy cuticle that reflects light and protects from UV.
Produces golden-yellow flowers arranged in a ring around the apex in late spring.
Grows vigorously in good light and can reach 20–30 cm diameter over 5–10 years.
Native to subtropical grasslands in southern Brazil and northern Uruguay.
One of the most reliable cacti for home growers — forgiving and floriferous.
Winter dormancy below 15°C for 6+ weeks is critical for flower bud initiation.
Can tolerate brief freezes to 0°C if kept completely dry during dormancy.
Often appears in the 'easiest cacti' lists for beginners due to hardiness.
The genus Parodia now contains over 100 species after the 2021 taxonomic revision.
Young plants are globular; mature specimens become more cylindrical with age.
Yellow flowers fade to orange-red as they age; each flower lasts 2–3 days.
Seeds are readily produced and germinate reliably in moist warm conditions.
Makes an excellent specimen plant for a sunny windowsill or bright shelf display.

care.placement_hints: Full sun; columnar; densely white-spined silvery appearance; intermediate grower.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; spines are numerous and sharp — handle carefully.

lore: Named for Auguste Strauss, a cactus enthusiast and collector. The genus Cleistocactus means 'closed flower' — the flowers stay partially closed but are pollinated through the narrow tube-like opening.
etymology: Cleistos (closed) + Cactus; Strausii honors Auguste Strauss.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun; can take full midday sun if acclimated
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Etiolation (tall thin growth), pale spines, slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald if moved suddenly from shade

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun
Can reach 1m+ in height; place where it has vertical space
Acclimate gradually (7–10 days) when moving to brighter spots
Repot every 2–3 years into well-draining cactus mix

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Underwatering

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Etiolation from poor light
conditions.symptoms: Thin pale growth; spines become sparser and less white; stem elongates without thickening.
conditions.remedy: Move to a brighter location over 5–7 days; prune off the etiolated section if severely stretched.
conditions.prevention: Maintain 6+ hours of direct sun; avoid shadier locations.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters hidden among the silver spines; honeydew residue on lower stems.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; use an old toothbrush dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol to brush affected areas; repeat every 3 days.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect monthly for hidden bugs among dense spines.

conditions.name: Soft rot at base
conditions.symptoms: Base becomes mushy or discolored; progression upward over days; foul smell.
conditions.remedy: Remove from pot; trim all soft tissue with sterile knife; dust with sulfur; let callus 5 days before repotting.
conditions.prevention: Never water the base directly; use fast-draining soil; ensure good air circulation.

conditions.name: Slow upward growth
conditions.symptoms: Minimal height gain or new growth segments over a full growing season.
conditions.remedy: Feed monthly (May–August) with dilute cactus fertilizer; ensure 6+ hours of daily sun; check pot drainage.
conditions.prevention: Apply light fertilizer every 3–4 weeks during growing season; repot every 2–3 years.

conditions.name: Spine discoloration or loss
conditions.symptoms: Silvery white spines become dull grey or brown; spines fall out or look withered.
conditions.remedy: Increase light (6+ hours daily); reduce humidity; improve air circulation; check for pest damage.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light; maintain low humidity; keep pot in well-ventilated spot.

facts: Grows as a slender columnar stem reaching 1–2m tall in 10–20 years of cultivation.
Covered in dense silvery-white spines that give it a glow in bright sunlight — hence the 'silver torch' name.
Native to high-altitude rocky slopes of Bolivia and northern Argentina.
Flowers are tube-shaped, remaining partially closed even when fully open — the genus name Cleistocactus means 'closed flower'.
Produces vivid red or orange flowers that bloom in spring; rarely flowers indoors without ideal conditions.
The genus contains about 50 species; C. strausii is the most commonly cultivated.
Pollinated by hummingbirds in its native habitat; the flower tube is perfectly shaped for a hummingbird's beak.
Can survive brief freezes to -5°C if kept completely dry; dormancy below 15°C over winter.
Columnar growth habit makes it ideal for tall narrow shelves or corner displays.
Despite the dense spines, they are relatively soft and won't cause injury if brushed carefully.
Takes 4–6 years to reach flowering maturity from seed or cuttings.
Mealybugs are attracted to the soft base hidden beneath the dense spines — regular inspection essential.
Propagation by cuttings: remove 10 cm segment, let callus 3 days, root in dry sand-cactus mix.
Spines reflect light, reducing water loss — an adaptation to high-altitude sun exposure.
Often used as an accent plant in tall arrangements or grouped with other columnar cacti.

care.placement_hints: Full sun preferred; climbing/epiphytic; edible dragon fruit production; intermediate.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; fruit is edible and commercially cultivated.

common_conditions: root rot if pot lacks drainage
low fruit yield from insufficient sun
lore: Produces the exotic dragon fruit sold commercially worldwide; flowers open at night and are pollinated by bats and moths. The fruit's pink skin and white speckled flesh make it visually striking.
etymology: Hylios (forest/wood) + Cereus (wax-bearing); undatus refers to the wavy edges of the stems.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun for optimal fruiting
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Thin weak stems, no flowering, yellowing
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald if moved suddenly from shade

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun for fruit production
Provide a sturdy trellis or stake — stems can grow 3m+ and become heavy
Stems prefer slightly moist (not wet) conditions during growing season; drier in winter
Repot every 2 years into well-draining mix; epiphytic origins mean loose, airy soil

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Low fruit yield
conditions.symptoms: Plenty of flower buds but few fruits set; fruits remain small or drop before ripening.
conditions.remedy: Increase light to 6+ hours of direct sun; hand-pollinate flowers using a small brush; improve humidity during fruiting.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light; maintain warm nights (18–20°C) during flowering; feed monthly with low-nitrogen fertilizer.

conditions.name: Stem rot in high humidity
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy sections on stems; dark discoloration; foul smell; rapid progression.
conditions.remedy: Trim off all affected sections with a sterile knife; let cut ends callus 3 days; repot in fresh airy mix; improve airflow.
conditions.prevention: Ensure excellent air circulation; reduce watering in humid seasons; never water stems directly.

conditions.name: No flowering despite maturity
conditions.symptoms: Plant is 3+ years old, healthy, but produces no flower buds.
conditions.remedy: Reduce water in winter (November–February) to trigger flowering; ensure 6+ hours of daily sun; feed with low-N fertilizer.
conditions.prevention: Winter dormancy and bright light are essential for flower initiation.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation on stems
conditions.symptoms: White cottony patches on aerial stems and new growth; sticky residue; stunted growth.
conditions.remedy: Isolate plant; treat with 70% isopropyl alcohol on cotton swab; repeat every 3 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect aerial stems monthly.

conditions.name: Weak spindly growth
conditions.symptoms: Thin pale stems that look fragile; lack of vigor; stems lean toward light source.
conditions.remedy: Move to brighter location (6+ hours direct sun); feed monthly with balanced cactus fertilizer; improve air circulation.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light; apply dilute fertilizer during growing season.

facts: Produces the exotic 'dragon fruit' (pitaya) sold commercially in supermarkets worldwide.
The white-fleshed variety (pink skin, white flesh with black seeds) is the most common in commerce.
Flowers bloom at night; large showy white blooms open after sunset and close by sunrise.
Pollinated by nocturnal bats and moths in nature; hand-pollination needed for reliable fruit indoors.
Edible fruits contain high vitamin C and antioxidants; sweet mild flavor with small crunchy seeds.
Climbing epiphytic cactus native to Central American rainforests; naturally grows on trees.
Can produce 5–20 fruits per plant per year in optimal conditions; first fruits within 2–3 years.
Reaches 1–3m in length; requires sturdy trellis or stake support as stems become heavy with fruit.
Thrives in warm temperatures (18–30°C); stopped below 10°C; frost-sensitive.
Relatively fast-growing compared to other cacti — new stems appear throughout the growing season.
Stems are flattened with shallow wavy lobes, not round like typical cacti.
Originally thought to be Selenicereus (renamed to Hylocereus in 2021).
In Asia, cultivated commercially at large scale with specialized trellising systems.
Fruits ripen 6 weeks after flowering; ripe when skin turns bright pink or yellow.
Makes an impressive conversation piece when fruiting; otherwise valued for night-blooming flowers.

care.placement_hints: Bright indirect; climbing epiphytic; spectacular night flowers; intermediate.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; flowers are fragrant but inedible.

common_conditions: failure to flower in low light
lore: Famous for its magnificent flowers that open only one night per year, lasting just 6 hours before closing at sunrise. The nocturnal blooming is an adaptation to bat pollination in rainforest canopies.
etymology: Selene (moon goddess) + Cereus; Grandiflorus means 'large-flowered' in Latin.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours of morning sun; afternoon shade
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale weak stems, no flowering, slow growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald on exposed areas

placement_tips: East-facing window with bright indirect light and 3–4 hours of morning sun
Avoid harsh afternoon sun; provide afternoon shade in summer
Support with sturdy trellis — stems grow 2–3m long and become woody with age
Keep above 10°C; winter dormancy below 15°C triggers flowering

conditions.name: Failure to flower
conditions.symptoms: Plant is 3+ years old but produces no flower buds; vigorous foliage growth instead.
conditions.remedy: Reduce water in winter (Nov–Feb); ensure 3+ hours of morning sun; stop feeding from September.
conditions.prevention: Winter dormancy below 15°C for 6+ weeks is essential to trigger flowering.

conditions.name: Stem rot in humid conditions
conditions.symptoms: Soft mushy sections on stems; dark discoloration; foul smell; spreads rapidly.
conditions.remedy: Trim all soft tissue with sterile knife; let cut ends callus 5 days; repot in airy soil; improve air circulation.
conditions.prevention: Never water stems directly; ensure excellent air circulation; reduce humidity in winter.

conditions.name: Weak pale growth
conditions.symptoms: Light green thin stems; slow growth; plant appears unhealthy despite adequate water.
conditions.remedy: Increase light to 3+ hours of bright morning sun; feed monthly during growing season.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light with morning sun exposure.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters on aerial roots and stem nodes; sticky honeydew residue.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; treat with 70% isopropyl alcohol weekly for 3 weeks; improve air circulation.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect aerial roots monthly.

conditions.name: Aerial root issues
conditions.symptoms: Aerial roots become brown or withered; root tips blacken; plant growth slows.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity around the plant (mist aerial roots 2–3x weekly); improve air circulation; check for pests.
conditions.prevention: Aerial roots prefer humid air; mist regularly; avoid direct cold drafts.

facts: Flowers open at night and close by sunrise — a nocturnal display lasting only 6 hours.
The 'Queen of the night' blooms only once per year, making each night a special event.
Flowers are large, fragrant, and white with a lemony vanilla scent.
In nature, pollinated by nocturnal bats and moths that visit during the night bloom.
Native to rainforest canopies in the Caribbean and Central America.
Epiphytic climbing cactus that grows on trees in nature using aerial roots.
Can reach 2–3m in length; mature plants become thick and woody.
Winter dormancy below 15°C for 6+ weeks is essential to trigger flowering.
The name Selenicereus means 'moon cactus' — honoring its nocturnal flowering.
Botanical gardens often cultivate this species specifically to display the spectacular night blooms.
Stems are flattened with wavy lobes, quite different from typical round cacti.
Takes 3–5 years from a young plant to reach flowering maturity.
Rarely produces fruit indoors; flowers fail to set seed without bat or moth pollination.
In homeopathic tradition, tea made from flowers is credited with sedative properties.
The unpredictable single-night bloom makes it a thrilling plant for patient collectors.

care.placement_hints: Full sun; tall ribbed columnar; Brazilian folklore symbol; beginner-friendly.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; white nocturnal flowers are fragrant.

lore: Cultural symbol of Brazil's sertão (interior); appears in local folklore and art. Mandacaru is Portuguese for the plant and is deeply embedded in Northeastern Brazilian identity.
etymology: Cereus derived from Latin 'cereus' (torch/wax); Jamacaru from the Tupi indigenous word for the plant.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun daily
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Etiolation, pale color, slow growth, weak spines
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald if moved suddenly from shade

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun
Tall columnar growth — place where vertical space allows (can reach 2m+ indoors)
Acclimate gradually (7–10 days) when moving to brighter locations
Repot every 2–3 years into well-draining cactus mix

conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Etiolation from low light
conditions.symptoms: Thin pale growth; weak spines; stem elongates without proportional thickening.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright location gradually (7–10 days); prune etiolated sections if severely stretched.
conditions.prevention: Provide 6+ hours of direct sun; avoid shadier indoor positions.

conditions.name: Slow growth
conditions.symptoms: Minimal height gain or new spines over a growing season.
conditions.remedy: Feed monthly (May–August) with dilute cactus fertilizer; ensure 6+ hours of daily sun.
conditions.prevention: Apply light fertilizer every 3–4 weeks during growing season.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clusters on stems and nodes; sticky residue; yellowing growth.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; treat with 70% isopropyl alcohol every 3 days for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants; inspect monthly for hidden bugs.

conditions.name: Base rot
conditions.symptoms: Base becomes mushy or dark; rot spreads upward; foul smell.
conditions.remedy: Remove from pot; trim soft tissue; dust with sulfur; let callus 5 days before repotting.
conditions.prevention: Never water the base directly; use fast-draining soil; ensure good drainage.

conditions.name: Spine loss or discoloration
conditions.symptoms: Spines become dull or fall out; body looks bare in patches.
conditions.remedy: Increase light (6+ hours daily); check for pest damage; improve air circulation.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light; maintain low humidity around the plant.

facts: Tall columnar cactus that can reach 5–10m in its native habitat and 2m indoors.
Deeply significant in Northeastern Brazilian culture; appears in local art and literature.
Survives the harsh caatinga (semi-arid scrubland) through extreme drought tolerance.
Produces fragrant white flowers that bloom at night; pollinated by bats and nocturnal insects.
The mandacaru has protective symbolism in Brazilian folklore of the sertão.
Can survive temperatures below 0°C for brief periods if completely dry.
One of the most cold-hardy Cereus species; suitable for temperate climates.
Columnar growth makes it ideal for tall narrow spaces or floor-standing displays.
The Portuguese name 'Mandacaru' has roots in Tupi indigenous language.
Relatively fast-growing columnar cactus compared to most desert cacti.
Spines are relatively mild; not as sharp as some other columnar cacti.
Rarely flowers indoors — requires significant age and ideal conditions.
Seeds germinate reliably in warm moist conditions; cuttings root slowly.
The sertão symbol status makes it a beloved plant among Brazilian plant enthusiasts.
Serves as a living connection to Northeastern Brazil's semi-arid interior landscape.

care.placement_hints: Full sun; tall columnar; very long sharp spines; intermediate grower.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; spines are extremely sharp and long — handle with extreme care.

lore: The Spanish common name 'toothpick cactus' comes from the indigenous practice of using the long spines as toothpicks and sewing needles.
etymology: Named for Gustav Stetson, a cactus collector; coryne means 'club-shaped' in Greek.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 6+ hours of direct sun; heat-loving species
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Etiolation, pale spines, thin weak growth
light_profile.too_much_symptoms: Sun scald if moved suddenly from shade

placement_tips: South or west-facing window with 6+ hours of direct sun in a warm location
Acclimate gradually (7–10 days) when moving to bright spots
Can reach 3–5m; requires space to grow tall; ideal for high ceilings or outdoors
Very long sharp spines — place away from walkways and children

conditions.name: Root rot from overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Mushy blackened roots, foul smell, wilting despite moist soil.
conditions.remedy: Unpot, trim blackened roots, repot in fresh dry mix with a pot that has drainage holes.
conditions.prevention: Let the top 2–3 cm of soil dry between waterings; never leave the pot standing in water.

conditions.name: Fungus gnats
conditions.symptoms: Small black flies rising from the soil when disturbed; larvae in the top layer.
conditions.remedy: Let the topsoil dry out fully; apply yellow sticky traps; treat with BTI (mosquito bits) if persistent.
conditions.prevention: Avoid constantly wet topsoil; bottom-water when possible.

conditions.name: Spider mites
conditions.symptoms: Fine webbing under leaves, stippled yellow speckling, leaves turn dull.
conditions.remedy: Rinse under the shower, wipe leaves with neem oil or insecticidal soap weekly for 3 weeks.
conditions.prevention: Keep humidity above 50%; inspect new plants before bringing them home.

conditions.name: Mealybugs
conditions.symptoms: White cottony clumps in leaf joints and along stems; sticky residue on leaves.
conditions.remedy: Dab each bug with a cotton swab dipped in 70% isopropyl alcohol; follow up weekly until clear.
conditions.prevention: Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks; wipe leaves monthly.

conditions.name: Scale insects
conditions.symptoms: Small brown bumps stuck to stems or leaf undersides; sticky honeydew underneath.
conditions.remedy: Scrape off by hand, then treat with neem oil every 7 days for a month.
conditions.prevention: Inspect leaf undersides monthly, especially after bringing new plants home.

conditions.name: Fungal leaf spot
conditions.symptoms: Dark or yellow-haloed spots on leaves, sometimes with a concentric pattern.
conditions.remedy: Remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting foliage, apply a copper-based fungicide if widespread.
conditions.prevention: Water at the soil, not on leaves; keep plants from crowding each other.

conditions.name: Overwatering
conditions.symptoms: Yellowing leaves that feel soft, consistently damp soil, base of stem blackening.
conditions.remedy: Stop watering, move to brighter spot, check drainage holes, repot if the soil stays wet >10 days.
conditions.prevention: Check soil moisture with your finger before watering; use a pot with drainage.

conditions.name: Underwatering
conditions.symptoms: Crispy leaf edges, drooping, soil pulling away from the pot sides.
conditions.remedy: Soak the pot in a basin for 15 minutes to rehydrate the rootball, then resume a regular schedule.
conditions.prevention: Set a watering reminder; don't skip more than one scheduled watering in a row.

conditions.name: Cold draft damage
conditions.symptoms: Sudden leaf drop, black patches on foliage near a window or door.
conditions.remedy: Move the plant away from drafts and heating vents; trim damaged leaves once growth resumes.
conditions.prevention: Keep plants at least 30 cm from single-glazed windows in winter and away from exterior doors.

conditions.name: Nutrient deficiency
conditions.symptoms: Uniformly pale leaves, weak new growth, older leaves yellowing first.
conditions.remedy: Flush the pot with plain water, then resume feeding with a balanced liquid fertilizer at half strength.
conditions.prevention: Feed every 2–4 weeks during the growing season; refresh the top 2 cm of soil annually.

conditions.name: Etiolation from poor light
conditions.symptoms: Thin pale weak growth; spines become sparse and dull; elongation toward light.
conditions.remedy: Move to bright location gradually (7–10 days); prune off badly etiolated sections if severely stretched.
conditions.prevention: Provide 6+ hours of direct sun; avoid shaded indoor positions.

conditions.name: Slow growth
conditions.symptoms: Minimal height gain or new spine development over a full growing season.
conditions.remedy: Feed monthly (May–August) with dilute cactus fertilizer; ensure 6+ hours of daily sun; check drainage.
conditions.prevention: Apply light fertilizer every 3–4 weeks during growing season; repot every 2–3 years.

conditions.name: Spine loss or dullness
conditions.symptoms: Spines become dull gray or brown; spines fall out in patches; plant looks unhealthy.
conditions.remedy: Increase light to 6+ hours daily; improve air circulation; check for pest damage.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright light; maintain low humidity; keep plant well-ventilated.

conditions.name: Base rot
conditions.symptoms: Base becomes soft or dark; rot progresses upward; foul smell.
conditions.remedy: Remove from pot; trim all soft tissue with sterile knife; dust with sulfur; let callus 5 days before repot.
conditions.prevention: Never water the base; use fast-draining soil; ensure excellent drainage.

conditions.name: Mealybug infestation in spine clusters
conditions.symptoms: White cottony patches hidden among the long spines; detection difficult; yellowing growth.
conditions.remedy: Isolate; use an old toothbrush dipped in isopropyl alcohol to brush affected areas; repeat every 3 days.
conditions.prevention: Inspect monthly among spine clusters; quarantine new plants.

facts: Grows as a tall columnar cactus reaching 3–10m in height; one of Argentina's tallest cacti.
Covered in extremely long (10–15 cm) sharp spines that resemble toothpicks or needles.
Indigenous peoples historically harvested the long spines to use as toothpicks and sewing needles.
The species name 'coryne' means 'club-shaped' — referring to the clubbed stem shape.
Native to Argentina, Bolivia, and Paraguay; found in dry rocky areas at moderate elevations.
Known as the 'Argentine saguaro' due to its size and columnar shape resembling the Saguaro.
Very heat- and drought-tolerant; adapted to hot dry climates with sparse rainfall.
Can survive temperatures below freezing for brief periods if completely dry.
Slow-growing; takes many years to reach significant height indoors.
Rarely flowers indoors; white flowers appear in late spring if mature and well-cultivated.
One of only two species in its genus; relatively uncommon in cultivation.
Extremely sharp spines present a safety risk — not suitable for homes with children or pets.
Makes an impressive statement plant for large interior spaces like atriums or conservatories.
Propagation from cuttings: remove long segments, let callus 2 weeks, root in dry sand-cactus mix.
The moniker 'toothpick cactus' reflects its historical use by indigenous South American peoples.

care.placement_hints: Bright indirect; rare TRUE-LEAFED cactus; primitive lineage; edible fruit; intermediate.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic; fruits and leaves are edible.

common_conditions: leaf drop from cold or dry air
root rot if kept too wet
lore: The most 'primitive' surviving cactus genus; retains TRUE LEAVES unlike nearly all other cacti, which evolved leaves into spines. Represents the ancestral cactus form and is the oldest surviving lineage.
etymology: Named for botanist Nicolas-Claude Fabre de Peiresc; aculeata means 'spiny' in Latin.

light_profile.direct_sun_hours: 3–4 hours of morning sun; afternoon shade preferred
light_profile.too_little_symptoms: Pale leaves, thin weak growth, few flowers

Keep above 10°C; prefers warm (18–25°C) humid conditions
Provide a trellis or stake — grows as a vine reaching 3–5m with support

conditions.symptoms: Lower leaves drop off despite adequate water; plant appears sparse.
conditions.remedy: Increase humidity by misting 2–3x weekly; group with other plants; use a pebble tray.

conditions.name: Few flowers or fruit

conditions.remedy: Feed with a lower-nitrogen fertilizer (higher phosphorus); ensure 4+ hours of bright light; improve humidity.

conditions.name: Weak spindly growth
conditions.symptoms: Vines are thin and weak despite adequate water; few leaves between nodes.
conditions.remedy: Increase light to 4+ hours of bright morning sun; feed monthly during growing season.
conditions.prevention: Provide bright indirect light; apply dilute fertilizer every 3–4 weeks.

conditions.name: Pest infestation
conditions.symptoms: Spider mites, mealybugs, or scale insects; leaves become stippled or show cotton clusters.
conditions.remedy: Isolate plant; mist with neem oil weekly; treat with isopropyl alcohol on cotton swab for mealybugs.
conditions.prevention: Increase humidity above 50%; quarantine new plants; inspect monthly.

facts: Pereskia is the most primitive surviving cactus — it retains true leaves unlike 99% of cacti.
Unlike all other cacti, Pereskia has genuine photosynthetic leaves that look like regular plant foliage.
Represents the ancestral cactus form from 200+ million years ago — a living fossil of cactus evolution.
Produces small edible fruits (berries) that taste lemony-sweet; called 'Barbados gooseberry'.
Vining growth habit — climbs trees or structures; reaches 3–5m with support.
True flowers with many petals, unlike the characteristic cactus flower structure.
Leaves are modified spines in all other cacti — Pereskia is unique in retaining true leaves.
More closely related to the common trees and shrubs than to other cacti; evolutionary anomaly.
Tropical origin; thrives in humid warm conditions; dislikes cold drafts.
Edible leaves are used in some tropical cuisines; also known as 'leaf cactus'.
Often used as rootstock for grafting other cacti due to its vigor and ease of propagation.
Grows vigorously in good conditions; becomes rootbound quickly and needs frequent repotting.
Fruit ripens 3–4 months after flowering; yellow when mature, edible fresh or cooked.
The genus Pereskia has 17 species; P. aculeata is the most common and hardy in cultivation.
Makes a fascinating botanical specimen — a living window into cactus evolutionary history.

care.placement_hints: Keep in bright indirect light; trailing habit makes it perfect for hanging baskets; maintain moderate humidity.

lore: A trailing relative of the African violet, Streptocarpus saxorum is prized for its cascading habit and prolific tubular flowers that bloom nearly year-round.
etymology: From Greek streptos (twisted) and carpus (fruit), referring to the spiralled seedpod that follows the flower.

facts: The genus name Streptocarpus literally translates to 'twisted fruit' from Greek streptos (twisted) and carpus (fruit).
Unlike most flowering houseplants, Streptocarpus saxorum flowers almost continuously from spring through autumn with minimal rest.
This species is a trailing epiphyte in nature, growing on moss-covered rocks in the cloud forests of East Africa.
Streptocarpus leaves are remarkably thin and delicate, which is why overwatering causes rapid rot.
The tubular flowers are pollinated by sunbirds in their native habitat, not by insects or other pollinators.
Cape primroses prefer cooler night temperatures (around 15°C) to trigger prolific blooming.
New plants can be propagated from a single leaf placed in moist sphagnum moss — a tiny plantlet emerges in 4–6 weeks.
Streptocarpus saxorum is far more forgiving than the African violet (Saintpaulia), which shares a similar appearance.
The plant earned the nickname 'false African violet' because it was once classified in the same family before modern reclassification.
In ideal conditions, a mature trailing specimen can produce 50+ blooms simultaneously.
The flowers come in shades of pink, purple, white, and blue, depending on the cultivar.
Streptocarpus responds well to humidity from misting but dislikes water droplets sitting on the leaves overnight.
This species is widely used in hanging baskets and terrarium plantings throughout Northern Europe.
The seed pods are extremely small and produce thousands of dust-like seeds, making propagation from seed tedious but viable.
In Kenya and Tanzania, wild Streptocarpus species have been harvested unsustainably, making cultivated forms crucial for conservation.

care.placement_hints: Needs at least 6 hours of direct sun daily; a heavy feeder requiring regular balanced fertilizer; prefers warm, humid air.

lore: The Hawaiian state flower from 1923–1988, Hibiscus rosa-sinensis is steeped in Tahitian and Polynesian culture where its bright blooms adorn ceremonial dress and mark romantic gestures.
etymology: Named for its rose-like flowers (rosa) and presumed Asian origin (sinensis), though the plant is now found in tropical regions worldwide.
